event_blocks.summary: Opening with dramatic music and the 'FRONTLINE' title card, the segment highlights the Russia investigation's intensification, showing nighttime aerial views of the White House and Washington Monument. A newspaper article, 'The Quiet Resistance Inside the Trump Administration,' is displayed as voices discuss Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein and the possibility of invoking the 25th Amendment. The Woman in Blazer speaks to the camera, followed by Donald Trump at a rally dismissing the "phony Russia witch hunt." The segment transitions to a woman in a green top, then Donald Trump at a rally, as a voice describes Trump attacking the Justice Department and FBI, with another voice declaring "It is a disgrace." The J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building is shown, followed by men in suits, as the narrator introduces a special investigation, noting Trump viewed the intelligence community and the FBI as enemies. The Capitol Building and White House are shown at night, as a voice warns of fear. Donald Trump speaks from a podium, addressing the "cloud" over his presidency, while the Man with Glasses appears pensive. A helicopter flies over Washington D.C., as a voice suggests Trump might fire Bob Mueller, Jeff Sessions, or Rod Rosenstein. Donald Trump descends from a helicopter, and the narrator announces a showdown with the 45th President, as a voice mentions impeachment. The segment concludes with a blurred White House, Trump looking upward solemnly, and American flags waving.

Woman in Blazer.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a dark blazer over a blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, visually introducing the segment's serious tone.

White House.description: Shown multiple times at night, illuminated with spotlights, appearing grand and imposing, sometimes with a blurred or dramatic visual effect to emphasize its symbolic importance, representing the seat of power and the presidency under scrutiny.

Newspaper Article.description: A printed article titled 'The Quiet Resistance Inside the Trump Administration,' featuring a silhouette of the U.S. map with figures in resistance, and text discussing anonymous dissent within the administration, which is described in the audio as a "stunning op-ed headlined I am a part of the resistance claiming to be a senior official in."

Washington Monument.description: Visible in the background during the aerial shot, standing tall and lit, symbolizing national identity and governance, contextualized by discussions of national investigations.

Man with Glasses.description: A middle-aged man with thin-rimmed glasses, shown in a close-up with a serious, concerned expression, in black and white, suggesting a moment of reflection or tension, appearing as Donald Trump's voice is heard.

Donald Trump.description: Appears multiple times: first at a rally, gesturing toward a cheering crowd while speaking into a microphone, wearing a dark suit and red tie; later, seated behind a podium in a formal room with yellow drapes, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious expression; and finally, descending from a helicopter, walking with purpose, wearing a dark suit and red tie. His voice is heard declaring 'No collusion, no nothing,' and he is discussed by narrators and other speakers as 'President Trump,' with 'the cloud as he calls it' hanging over his presidency, and as the subject of investigations and potential removal, including the possibility of impeachment.

Capitol Building.description: Shown at night, illuminated and grand, symbolizing legislative power and national governance, and visible during the aerial shot, contextualized by discussions of impeachment.

J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building.description: A large, imposing government building with a prominent sign above the entrance, symbolizing federal law enforcement and institutional authority, specifically mentioned in the audio as the target of attacks by Trump.

Helicopter.description: A military-style helicopter flying over Washington D.C., with its lights on, adding a sense of urgency and official movement, also seen as Trump descends from one.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a bold red screen displaying 'FRONTLINE' as intense, dramatic, and suspenseful background music begins. A male voice states, "Here's Mueller has convinced yet another witness to cooperate." The scene transitions to a nighttime aerial view of Washington, D.C., showcasing the illuminated White House and Washington Monument, with the text 'Originally aired on October 2, 2018' visible. A female voice declares, "The Russia investigation heating up on several fronts," followed by another male voice asserting, "A White House at war." The title 'THE BATTLE' emerges with a red glow over a blurred cityscape, as a male voice questions, "But I say, how do you beat somebody that hasn't done anything wrong?" and another adds, "With itself." A close-up of a newspaper article titled 'The Quiet Resistance Inside the Trump Administration,' featuring a silhouette of the U.S. map, is shown while the female voice describes it as "The stunning op-ed headlined I am a part of the resistance claiming to be a senior official in..." This is followed by a black and white close-up of a concerned Man with Glasses. Multiple male voices discuss the Justice Department, with one mentioning, "Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein talked about possibly invoking the 25th Amendment," and another stating, "Rosenstein has disputed and denied that report." The title 'THE STAKES' appears with a red glow, preceding a dramatically blurred White House. The female voice warns, "If Democrats take control of the House, they are going to be a subpoena generating machine. This White House is going to find itself playing permanent defense." The title 'THE WHOLE STORY' then appears, and the female voice continues, "Trump has been waging a deliberate war on the whole idea that there is such a thing as independent justice." The Woman in Blazer is then seen speaking directly to the camera. A brief shot of Donald Trump at a rally shows him gesturing as his voice is heard saying, "All we hear about is this phony Russia witch hunt. That's all we hear." The White House is shown again, and the segment concludes with Trump speaking from behind a podium in a formal room, as a male voice states, "President Trump is escalating his assault on the..."

The video opens with a close-up of a woman in a green top speaking directly to the camera with a serious expression in a dimly lit setting, as a male voice immediately states, "investigators who are investigating him." This quickly transitions to Donald Trump, seen from behind at a rally, gesturing toward a cheering crowd while wearing a dark suit and red tie, as the male voice continues, "He's attacking very nature of the Department of Justice. The FBI," followed by another male voice declaring, "It is a disgrace." Dramatic music begins as the scene shifts to a formal meeting room where officials, including a man in a dark suit, are seated around a long table, suggesting a high-level government discussion. A narrator introduces, "From Frontline's award-winning political team," explaining that "Trump viewed the entire intelligence community and the FBI as enemies." The exterior of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building is shown, emphasizing its institutional gravity, followed by a man in a suit walking briskly outside, then a close-up of another man in a suit with a concerned expression. The narrator announces, "A two-hour special investigation," and a male voice adds, "And the team Mueller has assembled may be the A-team of prosecutors for an entire generation." A nighttime view of the illuminated Capitol Building and then the White House, both imposing, symbolize the seat of power, as a male voice warns, "You should be afraid, you should be very afraid." Donald Trump is then seen seated behind a podium in a formal room with yellow drapes, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious demeanor, while dramatic music intensifies and a narrator states, "Inside the turmoil." A male voice elaborates on "President Trump," describing "the cloud as he calls it," which "hangs over his entire presidency," noting "that he doesn't really understand where it's going, but what's coming next, and if it's coming for him." A black-and-white close-up of a man with a pensive expression, possibly the Man with Glasses, adds introspection, as Donald Trump's voice is heard declaring, "No collusion, no nothing." A helicopter flies over Washington D.C., with the Washington Monument and Capitol visible, reinforcing the national context, while a male voice suggests, "This president may feel empowered to move to either fire Bob Mueller or fire Jeff Sessions or fire Rod Rosenstein or find some way to shut this investigation down." Donald Trump is then seen descending from a helicopter, walking with purpose in a dark suit and red tie, as the narrator announces, "And the showdown with the 45th President." A male voice explains, "The way that the president could be removed if that's the goal is through impeachment and conviction by the Senate or through..." The video concludes with a dramatic, blurred shot of the White House at night, followed by a close-up of Trump’s face looking upward with a solemn expression, and ends with American flags waving in the wind, symbolizing patriotism and national tension. Throughout, the on-screen text 'Originally aired on October 2, 2018' and the PBS FRONTLINE logo remain visible.

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit setting, a man with glasses and a beard, wearing a dark suit and red tie, stated that "the American people... are going to decide Trump's fate," setting a serious tone. This transitioned to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump’s face, accompanied by urgent soundbites declaring "fireworks on Capitol Hill" and "the stakes could not be higher," as a deep male voiceover announced "Trump's showdown." An aerial view of New York City at dusk, with the Empire State Building illuminated, and a timestamp of 'January 6, 2017' appeared, while news reports discussed "the news that Michael Cohen made yesterday" and an "extraordinary moment in American history." A female news anchor reported on "today's showdown at Trump Tower between the President-elect and top intelligence," followed by a male anchor stating "President-elect Trump is about to get all of the details from US intelligence." A silhouette of a man in a suit stood before three bright vertical windows, as another male anchor explained that "intelligence officials are expected to meet face-to-face with President-elect Trump at Trump Tower two weeks before the inauguration for what could be a day of fireworks," and that "the battle lines between the new President and Washington's establishment were about to be drawn." This was followed by a black-and-white profile shot of Donald Trump. John Brennan, 'Fmr. CIA Director,' seated in a formal interview setting, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, expressed concern that "Mr. Trump was ill-prepared for the job," lacking a "good grasp of international affairs, the legislative process, US law, intelligence capabilities," and mentioned a "highly classified report." The scene then shifted to a chaotic street scene with emergency vehicles, including a Highway Patrol truck with flashing red lights, surrounded by barriers and activity, as dramatic music continued. The minute opened with a chaotic urban emergency scene, featuring a black SUV with flashing red and blue lights, surrounded by barriers and other emergency vehicles, including a Highway Patrol truck and an FDNY ambulance, all set to a tense, dramatic underscore. Armed police officers in tactical gear, including helmets and rifles, were seen exiting a building labeled '725' and moving with urgency. Michael Isikoff, Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' appeared in a formal interview setting, wearing a suit and red tie, speaking with a serious expression about "Russia's hacking of US political institutions." A narrator stated that "four of the most powerful men in the United States government arrived" "on the street, far below." Isikoff continued, describing "a great deal of apprehension" as "the intelligence chiefs" went in, "knowing that their audience is skeptical of what they're about to say." The scene shifted to a partially obscured man in a car, followed by a black-and-white montage of men in suits, including a bald man with glasses and another with a stern expression, walking through a corridor. The narrator identified these as "senior leaders of a group known as the IC, the intelligence community," specifically naming James Clapper as "the Director of National Intelligence," Admiral Mike Rogers as "in charge of the NSA," Jim Comey as "the director of the FBI," and John Brennan as having "ran the CIA." A close-up of a glowing orange light on a wooden surface was shown, followed by a digital display of the number '4' in blue. The narrator explained that "they had come to tell Donald Trump that his election may have been compromised by Russian interference." The segment concluded with James Clapper, 'Fmr. Dir. of Natl. Intelligence,' seen in a formal interview setting, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie with a serious demeanor, as Michael Isikoff's voice returned, describing the situation as "the most aggressive, most direct, and most assertive." John Brennan, 'Fmr. CIA Director,' speaking seriously in a dimly lit interview setting, wearing a dark suit and a patterned tie, stated that a Russian campaign was the most significant "that the Russians ever mounted in the history of our elections... to interfere and and to somehow influence the outcome." A subtle, serious background music played. The scene transitioned to a dramatic black-and-white profile shot of Donald Trump, illuminated from the side, as a narrator's voice explained, "Behind closed doors, the IC briefing began." James Clapper, 'Fmr. Dir. of Natl. Intelligence,' was then seen in a formal interview, wearing a dark suit and a purple polka-dot tie, gesturing with his hands as he spoke, detailing that the briefing "was several hours long. There was no equivocation in our language, and we were very uh direct and uh very uh very clear in terms of what it is that uh we knew and assessed." The video cut back to John Brennan, continuing his testimony with a focused expression, asserting, "There was no pushback... because... the evidence that we laid out at the highly highly classified level was pretty pretty compelling and been very hard to to have pushback." Following this, another black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump appeared, this time with his hand resting on his forehead, conveying deep thought or concern, as the narrator explained, "Trump didn't argue, but he later said he saw the talk of Russian interference as an assault on the legitimacy of his victory." Sarah Ellison, a reporter from The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text, spoke directly to the camera in a warm-lit setting, wearing a purple blouse, her expression engaged and articulate, commenting, "It's a challenge not just his legitimacy as president, but as his overall power, his overall sort of sense of..."

New York City Skyline.description: A sweeping aerial view of Manhattan at dusk, featuring the Empire State Building illuminated against a fiery sky, with a timestamp 'January 6, 2017' overlaid, capturing the city's density and energy.

John Brennan.description: Fmr. CIA Director, shown in a close-up interview, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious and focused expression in a dimly lit setting, discussing the significant Russian campaign to influence US elections and later asserting the compelling nature of highly classified evidence presented, which left no room for 'pushback'.

Man with Glasses.description: A bald man with glasses, shown in a black-and-white close-up, wearing a suit and tie, with a serious expression, walking through a corridor, visually representing one of the "four most powerful men in the United States government" and "senior leaders of the IC" mentioned by the narrator.

Donald Trump.description: Appears in two distinct black-and-white shots: first, in a side-profile illuminated by dramatic lighting, and second, with his hand on his forehead, conveying contemplation or distress. He is discussed by a narrator who states he saw talk of Russian interference as 'an assault on the legitimacy of his victory,' and by Sarah Ellison who notes it challenges his 'overall power'.

event_blocks.summary: The video opens with stark black-and-white close-ups of a Man with Glasses and Donald Trump, the latter shown in dramatic side-profile with his hand on his forehead, as a narrator reveals that James Comey stayed behind to deliver news to the President-elect. Matt Apuzzo of The New York Times, in a color interview, recounts Comey informing Trump about the dossier, a set of memos prepared by a former British spy, partially paid for by Democrats, which the narrator describes as 'political dynamite' containing claims about Russian efforts to cultivate Trump for at least five years. Jane Mayer of The New Yorker, in a dimly lit setting, adds that the dossier is 'full of things that may be able to allow the Russians to blackmail him' and includes information about 'perverted sexual acts.' Redacted documents with phrases like 'sexual perversion' and 'compromising material' underlined in red are shown as the narrator emphasizes the intent to 'exploit Trump's personal obsessions and sexual perversion in order to obtain suitable compromat.' A redacted newspaper article detailing allegations of Russian efforts to exploit Trump's obsessions and sexual perversion for 'compromising material' is then presented, with a narrator reading about 'salacious and unverified allegations' involving Trump and Russian prostitutes. Carrie Johnson of NPR expresses concern about the relationship, while a dramatic close-up of Robert Mueller's face appears as the narrator states Comey had been warned not to threaten Trump. Jeh Johnson, former Secretary of Homeland Security, recounts a conversation with Jim Comey where he asked if Comey had met Donald Trump before, to which Comey replied no, and Jeh Johnson stated, 'Jim, you're in a very awkward spot here,' emphasizing a 'fine distinction' between informing and warning. A somber Trump is shown as the narrator states, 'We're going to run a country.' Robert Ray, former Independent Counsel, discusses how a new president would be cautious receiving information, and later elaborates on briefing someone to 'inform him of its existence' and a 'duty to warn.' Michael Isikoff, Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' describes a 'critical moment' that would shape Trump's presidency. Carrie Johnson quotes Trump's denial of allegations, asking, 'Do I look like the kind of guy who needs to hire prostitutes?' Jeh Johnson reflects on whether Comey understood the high stakes of the meeting. The segment concludes with dramatic close-ups of Robert Mueller, reinforcing the gravity of the narrative against a persistent dramatic instrumental track, as the narrator adds, 'Comey handed Trump a summary of the dossier.'

Jane Mayer.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a dark blazer over a blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Jane Mayer, The New Yorker.' She audibly discusses the dossier's content, specifically mentioning its potential for blackmail due to information about Trump being 'involved in perverted sexual acts.'

Man with Glasses.description: A bald man with glasses, shown in a black-and-white close-up, wearing a suit and tie, with a serious expression, walking through a corridor, suggesting official or governmental context.

Donald Trump.description: Appears in dramatic black-and-white close-ups, first in a side-profile with an intense, somber gaze, then full-face with a stern expression, as his presidency is discussed and he is quoted denying allegations, asking, 'Do I look like the kind of guy who needs to hire prostitutes?'

Newspaper Article.description: A printed document with redacted sections, featuring text about Russian efforts to cultivate Donald Trump, including claims of exploiting his personal obsessions and sexual perversion for 'compromising material,' with key phrases underlined in red for emphasis. A male narrator with a serious, dramatic tone reads aloud from it, detailing 'salacious and unverified allegations' involving Trump and Russian prostitutes in a Moscow hotel suite.

Matt Apuzzo.description: A man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark blazer over a patterned shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, identified by on-screen text as 'Matt Apuzzo, The New York Times.' He audibly explains how James Comey informed the President-elect about 'the dossier.'

Robert Mueller.description: A man shown in a dramatic black-and-white close-up, with intense lighting highlighting his facial features, wearing a dark suit and tie, conveying a serious and contemplative mood.

Robert Ray.description: A man with glasses, wearing a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, formal setting with a calm, authoritative voice, discussing the implications of receiving information as a 'heads up' or a 'threat,' and later emphasizing a 'duty to warn' by informing someone of 'its existence'.

Carrie Johnson.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit environment with a clear, direct voice, quoting Donald Trump's vehement denial of allegations.

Jeh Johnson.description: A bald man in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious and calm, measured voice in a formal interview setting, reflecting on whether Jim Comey understood the high stakes of a particular meeting.

Michael Isikoff.description: Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' shown in a formal interview setting, wearing a dark suit and red tie, with gray hair and glasses, speaking with a serious and emphatic, slightly gravelly voice about a 'critical moment' that was 'the most important moment that would shape Trump's presidency'.

raw_segments: The video opens with a series of black-and-white close-ups, first showing a bald Man with Glasses in a suit with a serious expression, followed by Donald Trump in a dramatically lit side-profile, then with his hand on his forehead conveying distress or contemplation. A narrator explains that "The IC Chiefs had one more piece of news for the President-elect," and after Brennan, Clapper, and Rogers left, "James Comey stayed behind to deliver it." The scene then shifts to a color interview with Matt Apuzzo from The New York Times, who, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, recounts how "Comey pulls the President aside and he tells him, 'Hey, listen, I need you to know that there's this what we now call the dossier.'" A montage of redacted documents appears, with text highlighting claims about Russian efforts, as the narrator describes "The dossier, a set of memos prepared by a former British spy, partially paid for by the Democrats," calling it "political dynamite." The narrator continues, reading from the document, that the "Russian regime has been cultivating, supporting, and assisting Trump for at least five years." Jane Mayer from The New Yorker, seen in a dimly lit setting, adds that the dossier is "full of things that may be able to allow the Russians to blackmail him," and "has information about him involved in perverted sexual acts." The documents are shown again, with key phrases like 'sexual perversion' and 'compromising material' underlined in red, as the narrator emphasizes the intent "to exploit Trump's personal obsessions and sexual perversion in order to obtain suitable compromat, compromat."

The minute opens with a close-up of a redacted newspaper article, its key phrases underlined in red, detailing allegations of Russian efforts to exploit Donald Trump's personal obsessions and sexual perversion for 'compromising material.' A male narrator with a serious, dramatic tone reads aloud from the article, describing 'salacious and unverified allegations' involving Trump and Russian prostitutes in a Moscow hotel suite. The scene transitions to Carrie Johnson from NPR, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, expressing concern with a clear, slightly worried voice, 'Whoa, not great, not a great stretch of this relationship. And Comey worries about that.' A dramatic black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller's intense face follows as the narrator's voice returns, stating Comey had been warned not to appear to threaten Trump. Next, Jeh Johnson, former Secretary of Homeland Security, appears in a formal interview setting, recounting a conversation in a deliberate, formal tone: 'I called Jim, I got him on the phone. I said, 'Jim, have you ever met Donald Trump before?' And a little to my surprise, he said, 'No, I have not.' I said, 'Jim, you're in a very awkward spot here.'' He continues, emphasizing a 'fine distinction' between simply informing someone and a more forceful warning, concluding with a bleeped expletive. A somber, dramatically lit black-and-white side-profile of Donald Trump is shown as the narrator's voice states, 'We're going to run a country.' The segment concludes with Robert Ray, former Independent Counsel, speaking with a focused, analytical tone in a dimly lit room, discussing how a new president would be cautious if someone came to them with information, stating, 'on the one hand, you might...'

The segment opens with Robert Ray, Fmr. Independent Counsel, in a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, wearing glasses, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, formal setting, his calm, authoritative voice discussing how information might be received as a 'heads up' or implicitly as a 'threat.' A low, dramatic, investigative instrumental track begins as the scene cuts to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump’s face, illuminated from the side, emphasizing his intense gaze and somber expression. A deep, serious male narrator states, 'As the two men sized each other up, the stakes could not have been higher.' Michael Isikoff, Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' then appears in a formal interview setting, wearing a dark suit and red tie, with gray hair and glasses, speaking with a serious and emphatic, slightly gravelly voice about a 'critical moment' that was 'the most important moment that would shape Trump's presidency.' The video transitions to a black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller, his face partially shadowed, conveying a contemplative and intense mood. Carrie Johnson, NPR, is then shown in a blue blazer, speaking with an engaged, clear, and direct expression in a dimly lit environment, quoting Trump's vehement denial of allegations, asking, 'Do I look like the kind of guy who needs to hire prostitutes?' The video returns to a black-and-white shot of Donald Trump, this time in a full-face view with a stern expression. Jeh Johnson, Fmr. Sec. of Homeland Security, a bald man in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaks with a serious, calm, and measured voice in a formal interview setting, reflecting on whether Jim Comey understood the high stakes of a meeting. The narrator then adds, 'Comey handed Trump a summary of the dossier.' Robert Ray's voice returns, continuing his earlier point about briefing someone to 'inform him of its existence' and a 'duty to warn.' The segment concludes with a final black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller, his face in profile, lit dramatically, reinforcing the gravity of the narrative, all set against the persistent dramatic instrumental music.

event_blocks.summary: A bald man with glasses, James Comey, walks seriously in a suit and tie as a narrator explains Donald Trump's pre-existing distrust of an FBI director who served under President Obama. The scene shifts to a close-up of a classified document, marked 'SECRET//ORCON//NOFORN', featuring Comey's notes from a January 6, 2017, private session with President Trump, with key text underlined in red; the narrator reveals Comey typed this memo in his SUV immediately after the meeting. The narrator recounts Trump's alleged comments about women and a stripper from the memo, as a man in a suit, possibly Comey, exits a vehicle with a briefcase. Matthew Miller, from the Department of Justice (2009–11), appears in a formal interview setting, stating it was unsurprising that the President-elect immediately memorialized the meeting for record-keeping. Exterior shots of Trump Tower with its golden signage are shown, as the narrator describes the President-elect's fury in the aftermath of the meeting. An older interviewee with gray hair and glasses, wearing a suit and red tie, explains that Trump viewed the situation as 'blackmail' and a 'shakedown,' believing Comey was trying to show he 'got something on him.' The segment concludes with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, his hand thoughtfully on his chin, illuminated by stark lighting, as the interviewee's analysis fades, underscored by a subtle, suspenseful background score.

event_blocks.summary: Sebastian Gorka, a Trump adviser, expressed concern over politically appointed individuals weaponizing intelligence, while J.D. Gordon, Director of National Security for the Trump campaign, explained Trump perceived an FBI director's approach with unverified information as a 'shakedown' due to his business acumen. A CNN broadcast announced 'BREAKING NEWS' that top intelligence officials had briefed President-elect Donald Trump, accompanied by a dramatic close-up of Trump's face and a voiceover revealing his fear of a leak, which soon occurred on cable news. An MSNBC screen displayed a BuzzFeed News headline about Trump's alleged deep ties to Russia, noting the report was unverified, while a voiceover mentioned claims by Russian operatives of possessing compromising information about Trump. A Fox News reporter discussed BuzzFeed's 'controversial move' in publishing a dossier, which subsequently appeared online in its entirety. A Bloomberg news anchor reported Trump was informed of unverified Russian intelligence, with financial data scrolling on screen, as a voiceover confirmed these details had been reported by numerous outlets. Later, at a press conference from 'THE OFFICE OF THE PRESIDENT ELECT,' Trump, standing at a podium flanked by American flags, passionately denounced the release of 'false and fake' information by intelligence agencies as 'disgraceful,' comparing it to 'Nazi Germany' and 'something the Gestapo would have done.' John Cassidy of The New Yorker noted Trump's 'vituperative and angry' response, while J.D. Gordon stated Trump felt it was a 'setup' and a 'plot to destroy him.' Trump reiterated that 'information that was false and fake and never happened got released to the public,' leading Cassidy to observe that Trump now viewed the entire intelligence community and the FBI as enemies, amidst a backdrop of journalists actively taking notes and recording.

event_blocks.summary: Donald Trump, clad in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie adorned with an American flag pin, passionately addressed the audience from a podium labeled 'THE OFFICE OF THE PRESIDENT ELECT, New York, New York,' with numerous American flags behind him against a blue backdrop. The audience, comprised of reporters actively taking notes and using phones, vociferously questioned him, with shouts of "Can you give us a chance?" and accusations of "attacking our news organization," met by responses of "Don't be." On-screen text identified the production team, including 'PRODUCED BY MICHAEL KIRK, MIKE WISER, PHILIP BENNETT,' 'PRODUCED & REPORTED BY JIM GILMORE, GABRIELLE SCHONDER,' 'WRITTEN BY MICHAEL KIRK & MIKE WISER,' and 'DIRECTED BY MICHAEL KIRK.' A dramatic, suspenseful score introduced a transition to black-and-white footage overlaid with bold red text reading 'TRUMP'S SHOWDOWN,' as a narrator explained that Trump's "combative strategy" would characterize his initial years in office and culminate in a "showdown with a special counsel" that now jeopardizes his presidency. This narrative was accompanied by grainy black-and-white footage of a suited historical figure, followed by a close-up of another suited man in a dimly lit, high-contrast environment, while the narrator elaborated that Trump's "combative nature" had been forged decades prior through his learned methods of attack and confrontation.

This narrative is accompanied by grainy black-and-white footage of a man in a suit, possibly a historical figure, followed by a close-up of another man in a suit in a dimly lit, high-contrast setting, as the narrator continues, stating that Trump's "combative nature" had developed decades earlier when he learned his method for attack and confrontation.

event_blocks.summary: Grainy black-and-white archival footage depicts notorious lawyer and fixer Roy Cohn intensely speaking to another man in a dark suit, while a third man briefly joins, before transitioning to a modern interview setting where Marie Brenner of Vanity Fair, wearing glasses and a dark sleeveless top, states that "Trump was created by the politics of intimidation taught to him by his mentor Roy Cohn," describing Cohn as "his alter ego," "confidant," and "ersatz father." On-screen text identifies her as "MARIE BRENNER, Vanity Fair" and displays the "FRONTLINE" logo. A close-up of a mechanical device with a glowing light and moving parts is shown, followed by archival black-and-white footage of the U.S. Capitol Building, bustling with cars and people, as a narrator explains that "Cohn had become a national figure in the 1950s." Dramatic, old-timey music begins, accompanying a shift to a crowded hearing room filled with men in suits, microphones, and documents, where an archival announcer states, "The Red-hunting Senator Joseph McCarthy attends," and the narrator adds, "during a different showdown in Washington." Typewriter and siren-like sounds are heard as the archival announcer continues, "The scene is Washington and a Senate investigating subcommittee. Mr. Cohn, his friend and aide, was present with Senator McCarthy," with the narrator referring to "the McCarthy hearings, an investigation to expose American communists," calling it "a witch hunt." The camera focuses on individuals at the table, including a man with glasses and another with a serious expression, while another archival voice describes McCarthy "ripping into the reputations of both friend and foe alike." The segment ends on a close-up of a man in a suit looking intently off-camera, as the narrator concludes, "Helping to run the show, Roy Cohn," and the music fades. Marie Brenner, with short blonde hair and tortoiseshell glasses, returns, speaking animatedly and introducing Roy Cohn as an 'architect of the most sinister period in American history.' The scene transitions back to grainy black-and-white archival footage of a crowded hearing room, where a formal male voice presents 'detailed testimony' regarding 'Julius Rosenberg,' as a male narrator describes Cohn's tactics to 'root out communists deep inside the government,' with visuals showing intense close-ups of two men in suits in high-contrast lighting. Brenner elaborates on Cohn's destructive methods, stating 'they smeared people as communists,' 'made up charges,' and 'ruined lives,' even driving some 'to suicide,' adding that Cohn 'never felt guilty for any of it.' Archival footage shows a close-up of two men, one covering his mouth, as the narrator details Cohn's downfall and return to New York City, coinciding with a visual of a man reading a 'DAILY NEWS' newspaper with the headline 'COHN RESIGNS M’CARTHY POST.' The final shot shows a man in a light suit walking beside a vintage car with a New Jersey license plate reading 'ROY C,' reinforcing the historical context as the narrator describes Cohn's reputation as a 'ruthless attack dog and political' figure before the audio cuts off.

event_blocks.summary: In court filings, Roy Cohn compared the Department of Justice to the Nazis, alleging 'gestapo-like interrogation' tactics, a move Frank Rich described as a consistent pattern of attacking accusers to create a smoke screen. A signed legal document showed signatures from Roy M. Cohn, Fred C. Trump, and Donald Trump for the defendants, but Cohn's suit was thrown out and Trump was forced to settle, though they declared victory in the press. Elyse Goldweber commented that the facts were irrelevant, as it was presented as a victory of 'beating the government,' which 'had nothing to do with reality.' Tony Schwartz explained Trump as a 'counter-puncher' who 'admits nothing' and always declares victory even in defeat, a formula Cohn had given Trump for survival and success in lawsuits and life, which Trump adopted and would rely on through more than 4,000 lawsuits. Adam Leitman Bailey stated that Donald Trump plays the sport of litigation very well, comparing it to the 'sport of kings and queens.' The segment concluded with an aerial view of the White House and Washington Monument under overcast skies, with a narrator mentioning a 'brand new team in charge of the White House.'

The segment opens with a close-up on a legal document, highlighting the phrase 'into a gestapo-like interrogation' underlined in red, as a narrator explains that in court filings, Roy Cohn compared the Department of Justice to the Nazis, alleging such tactics. Frank Rich of New York Magazine, speaking in a dimly lit interview, describes this as a consistent pattern of attacking accusers and the Justice Department to create a smoke screen around the original crime. The scene transitions to a signed legal document featuring signatures from Roy M. Cohn, Fred C. Trump, and Donald Trump for the defendants, alongside the Department of Justice for the plaintiff, as the narrator reveals Cohn's suit was thrown out and Trump was forced to settle, having lost. However, a black-and-white archival clip of an intently speaking man is shown while the narrator notes that Trump and Cohn declared victory in the press. Elyse Goldweber, a former Justice Department lawyer, then comments in a modern interview, gesturing that the facts were irrelevant, as it was presented as a victory of 'beating the government,' which 'had nothing to do with reality.' Following a black-and-white archival clip of a younger, intense Donald Trump, Tony Schwartz, co-author of 'The Art of the Deal,' animatedly explains Trump as a 'counter-puncher' who 'admits nothing,' never acknowledges mistakes, and always declares victory even in defeat.

The video opens with a man in a blue blazer and white shirt, speaking animatedly with expressive hand gestures, describing someone who "lost as clearly as you can lose, but he loudly proclaimed his victory." This commentary continues over black-and-white archival footage showing two men in formal attire, one possibly Roy Cohn, engaged in a serious conversation at a social event with balloons, as the narrator explains that "Cohn had given Trump a formula for survival and success in lawsuits and life," which "Trump adopted." The scene transitions to grainy color footage of Donald Trump, in a suit and red tie, surrounded by reporters and cameras in a crowded area, with audible press scrum noise. Trump's voice is heard saying, "I haven't gone to...", "I don't like...", and more clearly, "You can't say that I give up very easily," and later, "Not at all, we had a great victory. I'm happy as hell. Thank you very much." The narrator adds that Trump would "rely on what Roy Cohn taught him" through "more than 4,000 lawsuits." The segment then cuts to Adam Leitman Bailey, a bald man in a dark pinstripe suit and red tie, identified by on-screen text as 'ADAM LEITMAN BAILEY, New York attorney,' speaking directly to the camera in a professional setting. He states, "In the old days, the sport of kings and queens was horse racing. Now, it's litigation. And Donald Trump definitely plays that sport. And he plays it very well." The video concludes with an aerial view of the White House, surrounded by green lawns, with the Washington Monument visible in the distance under overcast skies, as the narrator mentions "a brand new team in charge of the White House," accompanied by dramatic, investigative-style background music and the PBS and FRONTLINE logos.

event_blocks.summary: Donald Trump's presidency is depicted as a deliberate confrontation with the established order, employing aggressive "attack strategies" and a "brawl with critics and the media," as noted by Ben Shapiro who anticipated Trump would "fix everything" immediately. Trump's "man always in motion" approach, utilizing his Twitter feed to directly engage the American people, was characterized by Corey Lewandowski as something people "aren't used to." News reports highlighted "a wild day at the Trump White House" with "executive actions, tweets, firings," and "chaos and controversy," yet this "was Trump's comfort zone." A "looming showdown with the FBI and James Comey" was anticipated, with Trump's "visceral" dislike for Comey amplified by the "Russian collusion" investigation and the "infamous briefing about the dossier." In an opulent White House room, Trump, amidst Secret Service agents and officials, engaged in a highly scrutinized interaction with Comey, who was positioned "as far away from Trump as it is possible to be." Despite Comey's attempt at distance, Trump "pulls him in" for a hug and whispers in his ear, a gesture magnified by the presidency, creating a moment perceived as "danger" and "politics" by Comey, who "doesn't want any part of this."

Ben Shapiro.description: A man with dark hair, wearing a dark shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'BEN SHAPIRO, The Daily Wire', with the FRONTLINE logo visible. He describes Trump's immediate, action-oriented approach, stating he 'is just going to start action right away... he's going to fix everything, he's going to set the world right immediately.'

J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building.description: A large, imposing government building with a prominent sign above the entrance, symbolizing federal law enforcement and institutional authority.

White House.description: An opulent interior room with blue carpeting, chandeliers, large windows with blue drapes, and ornate portraits, serving as the setting for Trump’s formal engagement.

Airplane Interior.description: An interior view of a private aircraft with wood-paneled walls, a large desk, and a seated Donald Trump in a white shirt and striped tie, signing documents while being filmed by a crew member.

Corey Lewandowski.description: Fmr. Trump Campaign Manager, shown in a close-up interview, wearing a dark suit and blue shirt with a red tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, with the FRONTLINE logo displayed. He characterizes Trump as 'a man always in motion' who 'always wants things' and goes 'directly to the American people with his Twitter feed.'

Motorcade.description: A convoy of vehicles, including a black SUV with flashing blue and red lights and police motorcycles, moving through a city street near a government building, indicating high-security transport.

Donald Trump.description: Visually seen in a dark suit and red tie, moving through a press scrum and later in a formal White House setting, gesturing as he addresses a group. The audio describes his actions of pulling someone in, going for a hug, whispering in their ear, and giving what appears to be a kiss on the cheek, as well as 'pushing Jim Comey to come across'.

Helicopter.description: A military-style helicopter flying over Washington D.C., with its lights on, adding a sense of urgency and official movement.

Benjamin Wittes.description: A man with short dark hair, wearing a dark suit and tie, identified by on-screen text as 'Friend of James Comey,' speaking in a dimly lit interview setting. He is heard describing James Comey 'standing in a blue blazer against blue drapes,' emphasizing it 'was not an accident' and that Comey was 'literally as far away from Trump as it is possible to be in that room.' He also recounts Trump calling Comey out, saying, 'No, no, no, come here, come here,' followed by sounds of a microphone adjustment and indistinct speech.

Man in Uniform with Gold Epaulets.description: A man in a dark uniform with gold epaulets and a badge, standing among the group greeting Trump in the grand room, likely a high-ranking security or military official.

David Urban.description: A bald man in a dark suit with a red and blue striped tie and a patterned pocket square, identified by on-screen text as 'Trump campaign,' speaking in a dimly lit interview setting. He is heard discussing the bureaucracy struggling to keep up with a rapid pace, causing 'whiplash.'

Chris Whipple.description: A man with short brown hair, wearing a blue suit and striped tie, identified by on-screen text as 'Author, The Gatekeepers,' speaking in a dark interview setting. He is heard detailing a 'looming showdown with the FBI and James Comey,' describing 'Trump's dislike for James Comey' as 'visceral,' and explaining Trump's nervousness about the 'Russian collusion' investigation and the 'dossier' that 'drove Trump crazy.' He also states that 'Trump made it clear who was in charge.'

Secret Service Agents.description: Uniformed men in dark suits with gold epaulets and badges, standing in formation and greeting Trump as he enters the grand room.

Gwenda Blair.description: A woman with short, white hair, wearing a vibrant pink blouse, speaking in a softly lit room with bookshelves in the background, identified by on-screen text as 'Author, The Trumps'.

Carol Leonnig.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red necklace, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Carol Leonnig, The Washington Post'.

Carrie Johnson.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Carrie Johnson, NPR'.

raw_segments: The segment opens with an aerial view of the White House, its grand white facade and manicured lawns visible with the Washington Monument in the distance under overcast skies, displaying the FRONTLINE logo, as a news reporter announces, "President Trump wakes up with a busy day ahead." A female voice adds, "This is just the beginning of what it's going to be." Dramatic, suspenseful music begins to play as the scene transitions to black-and-white footage of Donald Trump walking through a dimly lit room and then exiting a vehicle, conveying a somber, dramatic tone. A deep, authoritative narrator's voice states, "As President, Donald Trump came to Washington determined to confront the established order," while a motorcade, including a black SUV with flashing lights and police motorcycles, moves through a city street near a government building, suggesting high-security movement. The narrator continues, "He used Roy Cohn's attack strategy," as a news clip mentions taking "on the powers that be." The video then cuts to an interview with Ben Shapiro of The Daily Wire, who, in a close-up with a dark background, asserts that Trump "is just going to start action right away... he's going to fix everything, he's going to set the world right immediately." Donald Trump is next seen in a crowded outdoor press scrum, wearing a dark suit and red tie, surrounded by reporters and cameras, gesturing as he speaks. The scene shifts to Trump seated at a desk inside an aircraft, wearing a white shirt and striped tie, signing documents while being filmed. The narrator describes Trump's approach as a "brawl with critics and the media," with news clips mentioning "falsely accusing the press" and the need to "sort out friend from foe." This is followed by an interview with Corey Lewandowski, former Trump Campaign Manager, who, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, characterizes Trump as "a man always in motion. He always wants things. But you know what? People aren't used to that. People aren't used to a president who's going directly to the American people with his Twitter feed." Trump is then shown walking through a grand hallway with chandeliers, accompanied by others, before returning to an outdoor press event where he is again surrounded by media, pointing and speaking animatedly. News clips report "a wild day at the Trump White House" and "President Trump's travel ban has been blocked in the courts," alongside mentions of "executive actions, tweets, firings," and "a lot of chaos and controversy here in Washington D.C." The narrator concludes, "In Washington, it was seen as chaos. But this was Trump's comfort zone," as the final shot captures a chaotic indoor scene with Trump in a crowded room, surrounded by people holding phones and cameras, capturing a tense, high-energy moment, with the dramatic music swelling to a peak.

The segment opens with a chaotic, close-up shot of Donald Trump navigating a dense press scrum, surrounded by reporters and cameras, as a male voice, identified as Trump campaign figure David Urban, discusses the bureaucracy struggling to keep up with a rapid pace, creating a sense of 'whiplash.' The scene transitions to a formal interview with Urban, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting. This is followed by an interview with Chris Whipple, author of 'The Gatekeepers,' who, in a dark setting, details a 'looming showdown with the FBI and James Comey,' noting they would 'face off again at a photo op.' Whipple describes 'Trump's dislike for James Comey' as 'visceral,' explaining that Comey's investigation into 'Russian collusion' made Trump 'extremely nervous,' partly due to the 'infamous briefing about the dossier' that 'drove Trump crazy.' The footage then cuts to a grand, ornate room, likely within the White House, where Trump, in a dark suit, walks through a corridor lined with uniformed Secret Service agents and staff, entering a large, circular room with blue carpeting, chandeliers, and portraits. He is greeted by applause and handshakes from a group of men in suits and uniforms, including a man with gold epaulets. During this, Benjamin Wittes, identified as a 'Friend of James Comey,' describes Comey 'standing in a blue blazer against blue drapes,' emphasizing it 'was not an accident' and that Comey was 'literally as far away from Trump as it is possible to be in that room.' The video shifts to black-and-white archival footage of Trump in the same room, speaking to a group, with a dramatic silhouette shot, possibly of Comey, adding tension. Whipple's voice returns, stating, 'This time, Trump made it clear who was in charge,' followed by Wittes recounting how 'the President calls him out and says, 'No, no, no, come here, come here,'' amidst sounds of a microphone adjustment and indistinct speech. The segment concludes with Trump, seen from behind, addressing the assembled group in the opulent room, gesturing with his right hand, as the decor remains prominent.

The video opens with Carrie Johnson from NPR, in a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting. The scene transitions to an opulent White House room, where Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, is seen shaking hands with a man in a blue suit and pink tie, surrounded by Secret Service agents in dark uniforms with gold epaulets and other officials. The grand room features ornate portraits, chandeliers, and a large blue carpet with a circular pattern. An unseen speaker's voice describes this interaction, noting how 'this guy's more famous than me,' and that 'Comey knows even then, it's going to be a problem.' The speaker details the man's attempt to maintain distance with an outstretched arm for 'just a nice, distant handshake,' but instead, 'the President pulls him in' and 'goes for the hug.' The speaker questions, 'Why is it unclear exactly why, but goes in for the hug.' Another voice adds, 'Trump pulls him in, and whispers something in his ear,' observing that 'to the camera, it looks as if Trump is giving him a kiss on the cheek.' The audio emphasizes that 'when somebody's the President, the least tiny gesture is magnified by 12 billion times,' explaining that pulling someone over, whispering, or hugging them is 'a big deal.' The camera follows Trump engaging with attendees, with photographers capturing the moment. The footage cuts to Carol Leonnig from The Washington Post speaking in a similar dimly lit interview setting, followed by a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Trump and the man in the blue suit shaking hands, emphasizing the moment’s significance. Gwenda Blair, author of 'The Trumps,' then speaks from a softly lit room with bookshelves, wearing a vibrant pink blouse. The video returns to the White House setting, showing Trump gesturing as he addresses a group of officials and security personnel amidst the grand room’s chandeliers and large windows. The audio concludes with a voice stating, 'Comey sees danger, Comey sees politics, Comey doesn't want any part of this,' attributing the situation to 'pushing Jim Comey to come across.' Trump turns to face the group, continuing his address, while the camera captures the formal atmosphere and the presence of photographers and onlookers.

event_blocks.summary: Peter Baker described how someone demanded loyalty, stating, "You work for me. Uh, you're loyal to me, right? That's what he wants to know. He wants to know you're loyal to me," while a narrator explained that for James Comey, Donald Trump posed a threat to the FBI's independence and his career, transitioning to stark black-and-white close-ups of intense male faces. Nelson Cunningham recounted meeting Jim Comey 30 years prior when they both started as Assistant US Attorneys in the Southern District of New York, with Rudy Giuliani swearing them in, and described Comey as a young prosecutor who was "straight down the middle, uh, always turning square corners." The narrator noted that under George W. Bush, Comey was named Deputy Attorney General but clashed with the president when he led a revolt of Justice Department officials, concluding with Baker's statement, "His real claim to fame is this..." Matt Apuzzo discussed a moment during the Bush administration when someone refused to support President Bush's warrantless wiretapping program, inciting White House anger, followed by Jack Goldsmith explaining the individual's strong belief in the Justice Department's role to uphold the law. As the White House "backed down" after Comey and other Justice Department officials threatened to resign, Matt Apuzzo stated that Comey "testified in a major way before Congress," where archival footage showed him declaring he "couldn't stay" if the administration acted without legal basis, leading an interrogator to commend "Mr. Comey" for his actions. Bob Anderson observed that Comey was "in the spotlight more than any FBI director that I ever worked for" and "felt that he could use that spotlight in ways that other directors hadn't used," while a male voice added, "having the courage to speak the truth," followed by applause. A woman in a blue blazer commented that Republicans called Comey "Saint Jim, a guy in love with his own rectitude," and Robert Ray asserted that Comey had a "very black and white view of the world with very little room for gray," adding that "if he decides that it's the right thing to do, everybody else should agree with him." Following a news sounder confirming "FBI Director James Comey" and President Barack Obama naming him FBI Director, an exterior shot of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building was shown, concluding with a man in a dark suit and yellow tie preparing to speak at a podium, as another man entered behind him, while the audio discussed Comey making himself "the face of the FBI's investigation of Hillary Clinton." A man in a dark suit and gold tie spoke formally about individuals being "extremely careless in their handling of very sensitive, highly classified information," as a man with glasses explained that Comey "has been at the middle of this election and arguably is the person who most significantly influenced it other than the candidates themselves," interspersed with dramatic black-and-white shots. Matthew Miller asserted that a "through line in Jim Comey's career" was an "intense conviction that he is sometimes the only righteous person in any organization," and that his "self-regard... can cross into self-righteousness," while black-and-white shots added tension. A man with a beard stated that "Russia has come up again and again," and that as Comey led the investigation into Russian interference, he "would find himself on a collision course with the President," adding that "The very inauguration of President Trump... poses challenges to the FBI because they have investigations on..." concluding with a black-and-white close-up of a shadowed face. Intense black-and-white visuals depicted men in suits, as a narrator listed individuals under FBI investigation: Paul Manafort, Carter Page, George Papadopoulos, and Michael Flynn, the National Security Advisor, all described as being in the national security space, while another voice mentioned "players like Russia." An exterior shot of a black van with wooden lattice structures was seen near the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, as a narrator stated Comey dispatched two FBI agents to the White House on a sensitive mission to interview the National Security Advisor days after a new administration began, an act considered unprecedented. A close-up of a dark door with a gold handle and number '725' was shown, and another man questioned the FBI's presence at the White House and Michael Flynn's meeting with agents without a lawyer, asserting that such an event should never happen, concluding with black-and-white shots of a man with a patterned tie, arms crossed, then a close-up of his face, conveying contemplation. A male narrator explained the FBI's interest in a phone call Flynn had with Russian Ambassador Sergey Kislyak during the transition, as Karen DeYoung detailed how Kislyak initiated the call and Flynn eventually spoke with him, posing the question, "The question is what was said." An exterior rooftop shot established a Washington D.C. setting, as the narrator revealed the FBI knew from electronic surveillance that Flynn and Kislyak had discussed sanctions, and that "on the record, they asked Flynn about it." Michael Isikoff described how Flynn "dissembles," suggesting he did not have such conversations, and the narrator stated, "Court documents detailed what happened," with a voice reading text accusing Flynn of falsely stating he did not ask Russia’s ambassador to refrain from escalating tensions, leading the narrator to declare, "Comey's agents had caught Flynn lying to the FBI. A federal crime." A female voice raised "new questions about coincidental timing of a call," before the segment concluded with an exterior view of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building. A moving shot of a city street featured the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, as a narrator explained that explosive information Comey had against Flynn was shared with the Justice Department, leading Acting Attorney General Sally Yates to be "read in" to Flynn's FBI interview. Carrie Johnson described Sally Yates as a "career Justice Department prosecutor, 27 years in the department, a tough cookie, but a genteel Southern woman," detailing her stance: "She's saying, we need to tell the White House. Flynn has lied. And the Russians know that those are lies. We need to mitigate the risk." Emily Bazelon described Don McGahn as a "stalwart conservative lawyer," not "schooled in exactly these kinds of questions," and the narrator added that Yates sent her deputy, Mary McCord, to inform McGahn about Michael Flynn's lying. Mary McCord reflected that for someone in office for only six days, the information was "surprising," concluding with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man with a stern, partially shadowed face.

Peter Baker.description: A man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit, light blue shirt, and a patterned dark tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Co-Author, Kremlin Rising'. He speaks with a clear, authoritative tone, discussing the nature of a relationship where loyalty is demanded, and later referring to someone's 'claim to fame'.

Asst. U.S. Attorney'. He speaks with a measured, experienced tone, recounting his early career alongside Jim Comey and describing Comey's character as a prosecutor.

He is the subject of discussion regarding lying to the FBI.

U.S. Attorney,' who, in a measured, experienced tone, recounts meeting Jim Comey "30 years ago when he and I both started as Assistant US Attorneys in the Southern District of New York," adding that "Rudy Giuliani swore us in a few weeks apart." Cunningham, speaking seriously and gesturing in a dimly lit setting, describes Comey as a young prosecutor who was "straight down the middle, uh, always turning square corners." Bush, he was named Deputy Attorney General," but "clashed with the president when he led a revolt of Justice Department officials."
