The segment opens with Matt Apuzzo, identified as from The New York Times, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, his clear voice discussing a moment during the Bush administration when someone refused to support a provision of President Bush's warrantless wiretapping program, leading to the White House being "incensed." A black-and-white close-up then shows a man in a dark suit and patterned tie walking in profile with a serious expression, followed by Jack Goldsmith, identified as a Fmr. Asst. Attorney General, speaking animatedly in a dimly lit interview. Goldsmith's earnest voice explains that the individual felt strongly about the Justice Department's job to uphold the law, taking it "really seriously." The scene transitions to an exterior shot of the White House under overcast skies, with a fountain in the foreground, as a narrator's voice explains that the White House "backed down" after Comey and other Justice Department officials threatened to resign. Matt Apuzzo reappears, stating that what happened next reveals a lot about Comey, specifically that Jim Comey ultimately "testified in a major way before Congress." Archival footage then shows a man in a dark suit and checkered tie, presumably Jim Comey, speaking at a microphone in a formal hearing room, surrounded by other attendees, some with laptops. His firm voice declares he "couldn't stay" if the administration engaged in conduct without legal basis. The camera briefly cuts to an intently listening audience member before returning to the speaker. Another male voice, seemingly an interrogator, commends "Mr. Comey" for his actions, before the narrator concludes that this was one of many times Comey would go before Congress, all underscored by subtle, dramatic background music.

The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit and checkered tie speaking at a microphone in a formal hearing room, surrounded by attendees. The scene transitions to an interview with Bob Anderson, identified as 'Fmr. FBI Exec. Asst. Director,' who, in a dimly lit setting, states that the subject was 'in the spotlight more than any FBI director that I ever worked for' and 'felt that he could use that spotlight in ways that other directors hadn't used.' A male voice then adds, 'having the courage to speak the truth,' followed by the sound of applause and a brief 'Yeah' from the hearing room. The footage cuts back to the hearing room, showing a man with glasses and a striped tie leaning forward, appearing engaged, then a wide shot of the crowded room. A man in a dark suit stands and walks away, with others following, as the applause continues. The scene shifts to an interview with a woman in a blue blazer, who, speaking directly to the camera, explains that 'Republicans who don't like Comey because of his steps in the George W. Bush administration tend to call him Saint Jim, a guy in love with his own rectitude.' The video returns to the hearing room with attendees rising and moving, including the man in the dark suit and checkered tie walking through the crowd. Next, Robert Ray, 'Fmr. Independent Counsel,' is seen in a dimly lit interview, asserting that 'Jim Comey is by his own acknowledgement has a very black and white view of the world with very little room for gray,' adding that 'if he decides that it's the right thing to do, everybody else should agree with him.' A news broadcast sounder then plays, with a voice confirming 'FBI Director James Comey' and another voice stating, 'President Barack Obama named Comey FBI Director.' The news sounder continues as the scene transitions to an exterior shot of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building with American flags waving. The segment concludes with a man in a dark suit and yellow tie walking to a podium in a room adorned with American and Department of Justice flags, preparing to speak, as another man in a suit enters behind him, while the audio discusses Comey making himself 'the face of the FBI's investigation of Hillary Clinton' during the 2016 campaign.

The segment opens with a man in a dark suit and gold tie at a podium, flanked by Department of Justice flags, speaking formally about individuals being "extremely careless in their handling of very sensitive, highly classified information." The scene transitions to a close-up of a man with glasses in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting, explaining that "Comey has been at the middle of this election and arguably is the person who most significantly influenced it other than the candidates themselves." A dramatic black-and-white sequence then shows a man in a suit, his face illuminated from below, gazing upward with a serious expression. This is followed by 'MATTHEW MILLER, Department of Justice, 2009–11,' speaking in a formal interview setting, asserting that "There's a through line in Jim Comey's career, and that is this intense conviction that he is sometimes the only righteous person in any organization," and that his "self-regard... can cross into self-righteousness." A black-and-white shot of a man in a suit walking through a dimly lit corridor with silhouetted figures adds tension. Finally, a man with a beard and graying hair, wearing a dark blazer over a checkered shirt, speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, stating that "Russia has come up again and again," and that as "Comey led the investigation into Russian interference, he would find himself on a collision course with the President," adding that "The very inauguration of President Trump... poses challenges to the FBI because they have investigations on..." The segment concludes with a black-and-white close-up of a man's shadowed face, conveying intensity. The video uses shifts in lighting, color, and camera angles to build a narrative of official proceedings, personal reflection, and dramatic tension.

The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white sequence, featuring a man in a suit and tie in a high-contrast close-up, followed by another man in a suit engaged in a tense conversation, and a third with a stern expression. Over these intense visuals, a male voice lists individuals under FBI investigation: Paul Manafort, Carter Page, George Papadopoulos, and Michael Flynn, the National Security Advisor, all described as being in the national security space. The tone shifts to color as a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark blazer over a checkered shirt, speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, as another male voice mentions "players like Russia." Tense, investigative music underscores the entire segment. An exterior shot of a black van with wooden lattice structures is seen near the imposing J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, as a narrator states that Comey dispatched two FBI agents to the White House on a sensitive mission. A man with glasses and dark hair, wearing a dark suit, then speaks in a dimly lit setting, explaining that the agents went to the White House to interview the National Security Advisor just days after a new administration began, an act he considers unprecedented. Exterior views of the White House under overcast skies and a grand, classical-style building with a portico follow, with people walking nearby. A close-up of a dark door with a gold handle and number '725' is shown. Another man with glasses and graying hair, wearing a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, speaks seriously in a dimly lit room, questioning the FBI's presence at the White House and Michael Flynn's meeting with agents without a lawyer, asserting that such an event should never happen. The final frames return to black-and-white, showing a man with a patterned tie, arms crossed, then a close-up of his face as he touches it, conveying contemplation and concern over the unfolding events.

The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of an older man, his face etched with deep lines, hand pressed to his mouth, as a male narrator with a deep, serious tone explains the FBI's interest in a phone call Flynn had with Russian Ambassador Sergey Kislyak during the transition. The scene transitions to Karen DeYoung, identified by on-screen text as 'The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, speaking directly to the camera with a clear, articulate voice, detailing how Kislyak initiated a call to Flynn, who then got word and eventually spoke with him later that day. DeYoung poses the central question, "The question is what was said," before the video cuts to an exterior rooftop shot at dusk, showing satellite dishes and a distant Washington Monument, with a plane flying overhead, establishing a Washington D.C. setting. The narrator continues, revealing the FBI already knew from electronic surveillance that Flynn and Kislyak had discussed the Obama administration's sanctions on Russia, and that "on the record, they asked Flynn about it." A stark black-and-white close-up of a man with short, graying hair and intense eyes appears, looking directly at the camera, as Michael Isikoff, identified by on-screen text as 'Co-Author, Russian Roulette', speaks calmly and analytically, describing how Flynn "dissembles," suggesting he did not have such conversations with the Russian ambassador. The narrator then states, "Court documents detailed what happened," leading to a close-up of a legal document with red underlines, where an authoritative male voice reads the highlighted text, accusing Flynn of falsely stating he did not ask Russia’s ambassador to refrain from escalating tensions in response to U.S. sanctions. The scene returns to the black-and-white close-up of the man with graying hair, now looking downward with a somber expression, as the narrator declares, "Comey's agents had caught Flynn lying to the FBI. A federal crime." A brief, news reporter-style female voice interjects, raising "new questions about coincidental timing of a call," before the segment concludes with an exterior view of the imposing J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building on a gray day, with cars moving along the street, as the narrator's voice fades out, stating, "At FBI headquarters..."

The segment opens with a wide, moving shot of a city street, likely Washington D.C., featuring the imposing J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building on a gray, overcast day, with cars and traffic lights, establishing a formal, institutional atmosphere. A male narrator begins, explaining that information Comey had was "explosive," and evidence against Flynn was shared with the Justice Department, leading Acting Attorney General Sally Yates to be "read in" to Flynn's FBI interview. The scene transitions to a series of black-and-white close-ups of individuals with intense, serious expressions, interspersed with color shots. Carrie Johnson from NPR, seen in a blue blazer, describes Sally Yates as a "career Justice Department prosecutor, 27 years in the department, a tough cookie, but a genteel Southern woman." She continues, detailing Yates's stance: "She's saying, we need to tell the White House. Flynn has lied. And the Russians know that those are lies. We need to mitigate the risk." The male narrator then states Yates would meet with White House counsel Don McGahn. Emily Bazelon, a Lecturer at Yale Law School, appears in a red top, using animated hand gestures as she describes Don McGahn as a "stalwart conservative lawyer," not someone "schooled in exactly these kinds of questions." The male narrator adds that Yates sent her deputy, Mary McCord, to inform McGahn about Michael Flynn's lying. Mary McCord, identified as a former Acting Assistant Attorney General and seen in a gray blazer, speaks with a composed, authoritative tone, reflecting that for someone in office for only six days, the information was "surprising." The video concludes with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man with a stern, partially shadowed face, emphasizing gravity and tension throughout the narrative focused on legal and investigative themes, characteristic of a FRONTLINE documentary.

event_blocks.summary: A dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man's face transitions to Jeh Johnson, identified as Fmr. Sec. of Homeland Security, calmly stating someone in the West Wing was "compromised," while a narrator explains information given to McGann was meant to compel presidential action. Carol Leonnig of The Washington Post explains Yates and McCord perceived a "very vulnerable situation" for the White House and Mr. Flynn, anticipating his dismissal. The narrative details how McGann warned the president that the Justice Department believed Flynn had been lying, placing a trusted advisor "in the crosshairs." Michael Isikoff, co-author of 'Russian Roulette,' identifies Michael Flynn as the former DIA director and Trump's top national security advisor. The 2016 Republican Convention erupts with an announcer introducing "retired United States Army Lieutenant General Michael Flynn" to a cheering crowd as he walks toward a podium, with J.D. Gordon, a Trump campaign adviser, noting Flynn became a "trusted and close confidant" of Trump and his family. Flynn delivers a passionate speech, declaring, "We do not need a reckless president who believes she is above the law!" to a crowd chanting "Lock her up!" as the backdrop displays 'MAKE AMERICA SAFE AGAIN.' The narrator explains Flynn's "harsh rhetoric" endeared him to Trump and his supporters, and that "Trump and Flynn bonded." Sebastian Gorka, a former Trump adviser, remarks on the "very good chemistry" and "personal chemistry" between Flynn and the President, as ominous music plays and the narrator reveals Trump was informed Flynn was "in trouble with the FBI." Black-and-white archival footage shows Donald Trump beside Flynn, and Peter Baker of The New York Times states Flynn "knows a lot of things" and could be "dangerous" if pursued by investigators, with the investigation potentially getting "out of his control." The segment concludes with a black-and-white close-up of Flynn speaking intensely into a microphone.

Carol Leonnig.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red beaded necklace, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Carol Leonnig, The Washington Post', whose clear, analytical female voice describes a 'vulnerable situation' for the White House and Mr. Flynn.

Michael Isikoff.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie, speaking in a dimly lit room, with on-screen text identifying him as 'MICHAEL ISIKOFF, Co-Author, Russian Roulette', whose analytical male voice identifies Michael Flynn as the former director of the Defense Intelligence Agency and Trump's top national security advisor.

Republican Convention, 2016.description: A wide, vibrant shot of a massive indoor arena filled with a crowd, banners reading 'RNC 2016 Cleveland' and 'Make America Great Again,' with a man in a dark suit walking toward a podium, indicating a major political event.

Jeh Johnson.description: A bald man in a pinstripe suit and light blue tie, speaking with a serious expression in a formal interview setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Jeh Johnson, Fmr. Sec. of Homeland Security', whose calm, authoritative male voice discusses a 'compromised' individual within the West Wing.

Black-and-White Close-Up of Man.description: A dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man’s face, partially shadowed, with short, graying hair and intense eyes, wearing a dark suit, conveying seriousness and contemplation, shown in two different expressions—first looking directly at the camera, then looking downward.

Peter Baker.description: A man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit, light blue shirt, and a patterned dark tie, seen speaking directly to the camera with a composed demeanor in a dimly lit interview, stating that "Mike Flynn knows a lot of things" and "could be a dangerous person to have under pursuit of investigators," noting that Flynn's investigation "has a potential of getting out of his control."

Sebastian Gorka.description: A man with a beard and glasses, wearing a dark suit, a yellow and blue striped tie, an American flag pin, and a white pocket square, seen speaking thoughtfully in a formal interview, remarking on the "very good chemistry" and "personal chemistry" between General Flynn and the President.

Lt. Gen. Michael T. Flynn, Ret..description: A man in a dark suit, light blue shirt, and red-and-white striped tie, with an American flag pin, seen walking confidently to a modern, illuminated podium on a grand stage at the Republican Convention, 2016. He delivers a passionate speech with emphatic hand gestures, his face animated and intense, declaring, "We do not need a reckless president who believes she is above the law!" as the crowd cheers and chants "Lock her up!" His image and the slogan 'MAKE AMERICA SAFE AGAIN' are displayed on a large screen behind him. He is also shown in black-and-white footage, earnest and focused, and in a dramatically illuminated close-up speaking into a microphone, eyes wide and mouth open mid-sentence. He is described by J.D. Gordon as a "trusted and close confidant" of Trump, by Sebastian Gorka as having "personal chemistry" with Trump, and by Peter Baker as someone who "knows a lot of things" and "could be a dangerous person" under investigation by the FBI.

J.D. Gordon.description: A man with a receding hairline, wearing a dark suit, purple tie, and an American flag pin, seen speaking with a serious expression in a dimly lit interview setting, stating that Flynn "became a very trusted and close confidant of not just President Trump, but President Trump's family."

Donald Trump.description: Wearing a dark suit and red tie, shown in black-and-white archival footage in profile, standing beside Lt. Gen. Michael T. Flynn, Ret., in a dimly lit, formal setting.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man’s face, partially shadowed, conveying intensity and contemplation. This transitions to a color interview with Jeh Johnson, identified as 'Fmr. Sec. of Homeland Security,' who speaks in a calm, authoritative male voice about someone "compromised" within the West Wing. A black-and-white shot of a man in deep thought, hand on chin, suggests serious reflection, as a male narrator explains that information given to McGann was intended to force the president to act. Carol Leonnig of The Washington Post then appears, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, analytical female voice, stating that Yates and McCord believed it was a "very vulnerable situation" for the White House and Mr. Flynn, presuming he would be fired. The scene returns to black-and-white, showing a man in a suit listening intently to another man, followed by a stern-faced man, as the narrator continues, detailing how McGann informed the president that the Justice Department believed Flynn had been lying, a warning that a trusted advisor was in the crosshairs. Michael Isikoff, co-author of 'Russian Roulette,' then speaks in an analytical male voice, identifying Michael Flynn as the former director of the Defense Intelligence Agency and Trump's top national security advisor. The segment concludes with a wide, vibrant shot of the 2016 Republican Convention, where an enthusiastic female announcer introduces "retired United States Army Lieutenant General Michael Flynn" to a massive, cheering crowd, as a man in a dark suit walks confidently toward a podium.

The video opens with a wide shot of the Republican Convention, 2016, as Lt. Gen. Michael T. Flynn, Ret., dressed in a dark suit and red-and-white striped tie, walks confidently toward a modern, illuminated podium on a grand stage. As he begins speaking, J.D. Gordon, a Trump campaign adviser, appears in a dimly lit interview, stating that Flynn "became a very trusted and close confidant of not just President Trump, but President Trump's family." The scene returns to Flynn, delivering a passionate speech with emphatic hand gestures, his face animated and intense, declaring, "We do not need a reckless president who believes she is above the law!" as the crowd cheers and chants "Lock her up!" The stage backdrop displays his image and the slogan 'MAKE AMERICA SAFE AGAIN'. A narrator explains that "Flynn's harsh rhetoric had endeared him to Trump and his supporters" and that "Trump and Flynn bonded." The video transitions to black-and-white footage of Flynn speaking earnestly, then to Sebastian Gorka, a former Trump adviser, in a formal interview, who remarks on the "very good chemistry" and "personal chemistry" between General Flynn and the President. Ominous music begins as the narrator reveals that "Trump had been told Flynn was in trouble with the FBI." Black-and-white archival footage shows Donald Trump in profile beside Flynn. Peter Baker of The New York Times then appears in a dimly lit interview, stating that "Mike Flynn knows a lot of things" and "could be a dangerous person to have under pursuit of investigators," noting the investigation's potential to get "out of his control." The final shot is a black-and-white close-up of Flynn, dramatically illuminated, speaking intensely into a microphone.

event_blocks.summary: Following a discussion about Flynn with McGann, Donald Trump made a significant move by calling James Comey, an action Carrie Johnson described as a 'surprise call' to invite Comey for dinner, which Matt Apuzzo noted was 'clearly not a coincidence' given Flynn's 'criminal jeopardy.' Comey arrived to find the table set for two, an intimate setting Carrie Johnson characterized as an 'attempted bromance.' FBI documents, marked 'CONFIDENTIAL/NOFORN,' detailing this dinner, state Comey 'memorialized the conversation,' noting they sat at a small oval table set for two, and that Trump had 'very nice words for him,' describing it as a 'pleasant conversation' before the audio cuts off. The documents further reveal Trump's assertion, 'He replied that he needed loyalty and expected loyalty,' to which Comey responded, 'I replied that he would always get honesty from me.' During the dinner, Trump repeatedly asked for Comey's loyalty, a moment described as 'remarkable' and 'not the role' for a president. Robert Costa explained that Trump was 'feeling out Comey about where the investigation stood,' while Comey perceived it as intimidation and possible obstruction of justice, marking a critical divergence. Michael Isikoff likened Trump's approach to that of Roy Cohn, stating it was a 'with me or against me' mentality, highlighting the intense focus on loyalty, power dynamics, and the ensuing media scrutiny.

Robert Mueller.description: A man shown in a dramatic black-and-white close-up, with intense lighting highlighting his facial features, wearing a dark suit and tie, conveying a serious and contemplative mood.

FBI documents.description: A typed document marked 'CONFIDENTIAL/NOFORN,' with text describing a dinner between President Trump and James Comey, including details about the conversation and servers, with key phrases underlined in red for emphasis. Audio describes its content, specifically Comey's memorialization of the meeting and a direct quote about the setting.

James Comey.description: A man in a suit and tie, shown in a black-and-white close-up, walking with a serious expression. Audio describes him being called by Trump, arriving to a table set for two, and memorializing the conversation, including the president's 'very nice words' for him.

Carrie Johnson.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Carrie Johnson, NPR.' Her voice is heard recounting Trump's dinner invitation to Comey and commenting on the 'attempted bromance' during the meeting.

Matt Apuzzo.description: A man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark blazer over a patterned shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, identified by on-screen text as 'Matt Apuzzo, The New York Times.' His voice is heard explaining the context of Trump's dinner invitation to Comey, reading from Comey's memorialized account of the dinner setting, and describing the initial 'pleasant conversation' before the president's request for loyalty.

Donald Trump.description: Wearing a dark suit and tie, shown in black-and-white archival footage, seated at a desk and holding a telephone receiver to his ear, his expression serious and focused. He is the central subject of discussion regarding loyalty and power dynamics, though he does not speak in this segment.

Michael Isikoff.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie, speaking in a dimly lit room, with on-screen text identifying him as 'MICHAEL ISIKOFF, Co-Author, Russian Roulette'. He speaks with expressive hand gestures, analyzing Trump's approach to loyalty as reflecting a 'Roy Cohn view of the world' and a 'with me or against me' business philosophy.

Robert Costa.description: A man with dark hair, wearing a dark suit and red tie, identified by on-screen text as 'ROBERT COSTA, The Washington Post,' speaking directly to the camera in a formal interview setting. He analyzes President Trump's interactions with Comey, interpreting them as Trump 'feeling out' Comey regarding the investigation, which Comey perceived as 'intimidating, possible obstruction of justice,' marking a significant split.

Man with Glasses.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting. He provides analysis on the President's repeated demands for loyalty from the FBI director, describing it as a 'remarkable moment' and an inappropriate role for an FBI director.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a black-and-white archival shot of Donald Trump, serious and focused, seated at a desk with a telephone receiver to his ear, as an unidentified male narrator explains that one day after discussing Flynn with McGann, Trump took a fateful step by calling Comey. This transitions to a color interview with Carrie Johnson of NPR, wearing a blue blazer, who recounts Trump's 'surprise call' inviting Comey for dinner. A dramatic black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller, partially shadowed, conveys intensity. Over a visual of an unidentified man in a dark suit and purple tie speaking seriously, Matt Apuzzo's voice from The New York Times clarifies that the dinner invitation was 'clearly not a coincidence' given the briefing about Mike Flynn's 'criminal jeopardy.' The scene then cuts to a black-and-white image of James Comey, smiling slightly while walking, as the narrator continues, detailing Comey's arrival to find the table set for two. Carrie Johnson reappears, speaking with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing the intimate setting and the 'attempted bromance.' A close-up of FBI documents, marked 'CONFIDENTIAL/NOFORN,' detailing the dinner between President Trump and James Comey, is shown as the narrator states Comey 'memorialized the conversation.' Matt Apuzzo's voice then reads from the document, describing the setting: 'We sat facing each other at a small oval table set for two and placed in the center of the room.' The video then shows Matt Apuzzo in a warmly lit interview setting, continuing to explain that Comey noted the president had 'very nice words for him,' making it a 'pleasant conversation,' before the audio cuts off with 'And then the president's...' The final shot returns to the FBI document, highlighting in red the critical line: 'He replied that he needed loyalty and expected loyalty.'

The video opens with a close-up of a typed document, highlighting a red-underlined passage. A male voiceover reads the text: 'He replied that he needed loyalty and expected loyalty. I did not reply, or even change my facial expression.' The camera then transitions to a series of black-and-white close-ups of Donald Trump, shown in various formal settings—seated, speaking, and looking pensive—emphasizing a serious, introspective mood. The footage alternates between these archival-style shots and color interview segments. A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting, stating, 'The President would ask for Comey's loyalty several times during the dinner,' and elaborating on the 'remarkable moment' of a president demanding loyalty from an FBI director, which is 'not the role.' The male voiceover returns, reading more text: 'He then returned to loyalty, saying, 'I need loyalty.' I replied that he would always get honesty from me.' Robert Costa of The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text and appearing in a formal interview setup, then explains that 'President Trump was feeling out Comey about where the investigation stood,' and that 'Comey saw it as intimidation, possible obstruction of justice. This is the moment where things really start to split.' The sequence continues with Michael Isikoff, Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' identified by on-screen text and speaking with expressive hand gestures in a dimly lit room, asserting, 'This is the Roy Cohn view of the world, this has been the Donald Trump view of the world. This is the way he's done business, either you're with me or you're against me.' The visual rhythm alternates between the somber, monochromatic Trump footage and the more polished, color interview segments, creating an overall atmosphere of gravity and investigative depth, focusing on political loyalty, power dynamics, and media scrutiny.

event_blocks.summary: A black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump in profile, appearing serious, is juxtaposed with a question about team allegiance, followed by a statement that Trump failed to win Comey over. The rhythmic whirring and clatter of a printing press leads to a close-up of The Washington Post dated February 10, 2017, with headlines on immigration and Russia. A narrator explains a subsequent leak to The Washington Post, which Karen DeYoung confirms, stating the newspaper broke the news about National Security Advisor Michael Flynn. The Post's scoop revealed details of electronic surveillance of Flynn's call with the Russian Ambassador, and DeYoung adds that intercepts indicated discussions about sanctions. As multiple news outlets report, Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, exits a helicopter on a tarmac, accompanied by staff and military personnel, while Melania Trump, in a dark coat and white pants, smiles as she enters an aircraft, followed by Trump. The narrator notes that as the story dominated news coverage, Trump headed to Florida with the Prime Minister of Japan and Mike Flynn, while another reporter highlights that this contradicts Flynn's denials. Inside the aircraft doorway, with a 'PRESIDENTIAL CABIN' display, a female reporter asks Trump about conversations with Russians regarding sanctions before his inauguration; Trump, with a serious expression, responds, "I don't know, I haven't seen it," as Melania looks on neutrally. Trump, in a dark suit and red patterned tie, and Melania Trump, in a dark coat, stand in the 'PRESIDENTIAL CABIN' doorway at '3:46'; Trump audibly responds to an off-screen question about a report, stating, "I don't know about it. I haven't seen it," and "What report is that? I haven't seen that, I'll look at that," before adding, "Thank you. Thank you very much." A narrator interjects that "though he had known about the intercepts for weeks, Trump claimed ignorance." They then exit the aircraft, greeted by a woman, and a motorcade of black SUVs and a limousine, including a Suburban with an American flag, drives away on the tarmac under a dusky sky, with the 'FRONTLINE' logo visible. A montage of black-and-white 'The Washington Post' headlines from February 10, 2017, details allegations that Michael Flynn discussed U.S. sanctions with the Russian ambassador, with headlines like 'Flynn, Russian Envoy Discussed Sanctions' and 'Trump to Look Into Reports on Flynn'. A female news anchor discusses former acting Attorney General Sally Yates informing the Trump White House about Flynn, and a narrator adds that the meeting between Yates and White House counsel Don McGahn had also leaked, with the news anchor explaining that the National Security Advisor might be "personally compromised" and "vulnerable to Russian blackmail." Matthew Miller, identified as 'Department of Justice, 2009–11,' wearing a dark suit and purple tie, explains that when it emerges that "they were warned that he was compromised and that he might have lied to the FBI and they did nothing about it," it becomes a scandal implicating "not just Mike Flynn, but the White House Chief of Staff, the President, and anyone who knew about this warning and failed to take action." The narrator concludes that "a full force of the Washington establishment was turned on Trump," as a black-and-white, ghostly overlay of a man with long hair and a beard is superimposed over Miller’s image. A black-and-white, ghostly close-up of a historical figure with long hair and a beard precedes John Cassidy from The New Yorker describing a "Bermuda Triangle in Washington" where the FBI, media, White House lawyers, and Justice Department pressured for Flynn's departure. Cassidy, in a color interview in a dimly lit room, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, gestures earnestly. Donald Trump, in black-and-white, stands in a formal setting with a bookshelf, as a narrator explains that "Washington was turning out to be a very different place than New York" for him. Robert Ray, a former Independent Counsel, appears in a color interview, speaking with a focused expression about how many New Yorkers found "that's not how it is" in Washington. A stark black-and-white profile shot of a man in a suit with an intense gaze accompanies the narrator's revelation that "Under pressure, Trump gave in. He could keep Flynn no longer." A close-up of Michael T. Flynn's resignation letter, praising Trump and Pence, is shown while multiple reporters announce that "National Security Advisor Michael Flynn has stepped down" and "Trump accepted Flynn's resignation," calling it a "shake-up" after a "tumultuous first month." A black-and-white shot of a man, possibly Flynn, looking upward seriously, accompanies the narrator's statement that "Flynn was gone, but still in jeopardy from an active FBI investigation." The segment concludes with a grave, black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump's focused face, as the narrator states, "Now, the President took an extraordinary step."

Karen DeYoung.description: A woman with short, dark, curly hair, wearing a dark blazer over a white collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit indoor setting, identified by on-screen text as 'KAREN DEYOUNG, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. Her voice is heard discussing the Post breaking news about National Security Advisor Michael Flynn and confirming intercepts regarding discussions of sanctions.

Melania Trump.description: Wearing a dark coat and white pants, standing beside Donald Trump in the aircraft doorway, with long blonde hair and a composed demeanor, later seen walking on the tarmac and entering the aircraft.

Airplane Interior.description: An interior view of a private aircraft with a digital display reading 'PRESIDENTIAL CABIN' and the time '3:46', where Donald and Melania Trump stand in the doorway.

Newspaper Article.description: A black-and-white montage of 'The Washington Post' headlines from February 10, 2017, with bold titles such as 'Flynn, Russian Envoy Discussed Sanctions' and 'Trump to Look Into Reports on Flynn,' highlighting political controversy.

Man in Dark Suit and Checkered Tie.description: A man in a dark suit and checkered tie, shown in a black-and-white shot smiling as he walks, possibly a member of Trump's entourage or staff, with a cheerful expression.

Donald Trump.description: Wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie, shown in black-and-white, standing in a formal indoor setting with a bookshelf and decorative plate, looking serious and contemplative, and later in a grave close-up. Narrators state that 'Washington was turning out to be a very different place than New York' for him, that 'Under pressure, Trump gave in. He could keep Flynn no longer,' and that 'Trump accepted Flynn's resignation,' before he 'took an extraordinary step.'

Helicopter.description: A military-style helicopter marked 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA,' shown on a tarmac as Donald Trump and others exit, with military personnel in dress uniforms standing nearby.

Matthew Miller.description: A man with dark hair, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, speaking directly to the camera in a formal interview setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Matthew Miller, Department of Justice, 2009–11', who audibly explains how the scandal implicates not just Mike Flynn but also the White House Chief of Staff, the President, and anyone who knew about the warning regarding Flynn's compromised status and failed to act.

Historical Figure (Black-and-White).description: A man with long hair and a beard, shown in a black-and-white, ghostly overlay, superimposed over Matthew Miller’s image, creating a dramatic and symbolic visual effect.

Motorcade.description: A convoy of black SUVs and a limousine, including a Suburban with an American flag, driving on the tarmac under a dusky sky, indicating high-security transport.

Michael T. Flynn, LTG (Ret.).description: Assistant to the President / National Security Advisor, shown in a black-and-white shot looking upward with a serious expression, wearing a dark suit and tie. His resignation letter is shown, and he is referred to by John Cassidy as the person who 'has to go.' Narrators and reporters confirm that 'National Security Advisor Michael Flynn has stepped down,' that Trump 'could keep Flynn no longer' and 'accepted Flynn's resignation,' and that 'Flynn was gone, but still in jeopardy from an active FBI investigation.'

Legal Document.description: A typed resignation letter signed by Michael T. Flynn, Assistant to the President / National Security Advisor, with text praising President Trump and Vice President Pence, and expressing confidence in their leadership, displayed in a close-up shot.

Robert Ray.description: Fmr. Independent Counsel, shown in a close-up color interview, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a focused expression in a dimly lit room. He comments on New Yorkers coming to Washington, finding that 'that's not how it is' compared to their expectations.

John Cassidy.description: A man with dark, wavy hair, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, speaking earnestly in a dimly lit interview setting, identified by on-screen text as 'JOHN CASSIDY, The New Yorker.' He describes Washington as a 'Bermuda Triangle' where various entities pressured for Flynn's removal, stating, 'this is just has to happen, Flynn has to go.'

Black-and-White Close-Up of Man.description: A dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man’s face, partially shadowed, with short, graying hair and intense eyes, wearing a dark suit, conveying seriousness and contemplation, shown in two different expressions—first looking directly at the camera, then looking downward.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump in profile, appearing serious, as a male voice asks, "Are you on my team or are you on the other guy's team?" and then states, "Trump had failed to win Comey over." The scene transitions to the rhythmic whirring and clatter of a printing press in motion, followed by a close-up of The Washington Post newspaper dated February 10, 2017, with headlines about an immigration ban and Russia discussions. A male narrator explains, "Then, two weeks later, there was another leak. This one to The Washington Post." Karen DeYoung, identified as from The Washington Post, appears on screen, her voice heard stating, "Washington Post broke this news. They say that National Security Advisor Michael Flynn did..." The narrator interjects, detailing how "The Post scoop publicly revealed the details of that electronic surveillance of Flynn's call with Russian Ambassador Kislyak." DeYoung then elaborates, "We had found out that in fact there were intercepts and had a variety of sources saying that yes, they had discussed sanctions." As a reporter's voice mentions "multiple news outlets report," the video shifts to an outdoor tarmac where Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, exits a 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA' helicopter, accompanied by staff and military personnel. Melania Trump, in a dark coat and white pants, walks towards him, smiling as she enters an aircraft, with Trump following. The narrator explains, "As the story dominated the news coverage in Washington, the President headed to Florida, accompanied by the Prime Minister of Japan and Mike Flynn." Another reporter adds, "that goes against the denials of Flynn himself." Inside the aircraft doorway, with a 'PRESIDENTIAL CABIN' display visible, a female reporter's voice asks Trump about "conversations with the Russians about sanctions before you were sworn in," to which Trump, with a serious expression, responds, "I don't know, I haven't seen it," while Melania looks on neutrally.

The video opens with Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red patterned tie, and Melania Trump, in a dark coat, standing in the doorway of an aircraft identified as the 'PRESIDENTIAL CABIN' with the time '3:46'. Trump, with a serious expression, audibly responds to an off-screen question about a report, stating, "I don't know about it. I haven't seen it," and "What report is that? I haven't seen that, I'll look at that," before adding, "Thank you. Thank you very much." A narrator interjects that "though he had known about the intercepts for weeks, Trump claimed ignorance." They then exit the aircraft, greeted by a woman, and a motorcade of black SUVs and a limousine, including a Suburban with an American flag, drives away on the tarmac under a dusky sky, with the 'FRONTLINE' logo visible. The scene transitions to a montage of black-and-white 'The Washington Post' headlines from February 10, 2017, detailing allegations that Michael Flynn discussed U.S. sanctions with the Russian ambassador, with headlines like 'Flynn, Russian Envoy Discussed Sanctions' and 'Trump to Look Into Reports on Flynn'. A female news anchor's voice is heard discussing how former acting Attorney General Sally Yates informed the Trump White House about Flynn, and a narrator adds that the meeting between Yates and White House counsel Don McGahn had also leaked, with the news anchor further explaining that the National Security Advisor might be "personally compromised" and "vulnerable to Russian blackmail." The video then cuts to an interview with Matthew Miller, identified as 'Department of Justice, 2009–11,' who, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, audibly explains that when it comes out that "they were warned that he was compromised and that he might have lied to the FBI and they did nothing about it," it becomes a scandal implicating "not just Mike Flynn, but the White House Chief of Staff, the President, and anyone who knew about this warning and failed to take action." The narrator concludes that "a full force of the Washington establishment was turned on Trump," as a black-and-white, ghostly overlay of a man with long hair and a beard is superimposed over Miller’s image, creating a dramatic effect.

The video opens dramatically with a black-and-white, ghostly close-up of a historical figure with long hair and a beard, as John Cassidy from The New Yorker begins to speak, describing a "Bermuda Triangle in Washington" where the FBI, media, White House lawyers, and Justice Department were all pressuring for Flynn's departure. Cassidy, seen in a color interview in a dimly lit room, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, gestures earnestly as he elaborates on this pressure. The scene then cuts to a serious and contemplative Donald Trump in black-and-white, standing in a formal setting with a bookshelf, while a narrator explains that "Washington was turning out to be a very different place than New York" for him. Robert Ray, a former Independent Counsel, then appears in a color interview, speaking with a focused expression about how many New Yorkers coming to Washington found "that's not how it is." This is followed by a stark black-and-white profile shot of a man in a suit with an intense gaze, as the narrator reveals that "Under pressure, Trump gave in. He could keep Flynn no longer." A close-up of Michael T. Flynn's resignation letter, praising Trump and Pence, is shown while multiple reporters announce that "National Security Advisor Michael Flynn has stepped down" and "Trump accepted Flynn's resignation," calling it a "shake-up" after a "tumultuous first month." A black-and-white shot of a man, possibly Flynn, looking upward seriously, accompanies the narrator's statement that "Flynn was gone, but still in jeopardy from an active FBI investigation." The segment concludes with a grave, black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump's focused face, as the narrator states, "Now, the President took an extraordinary step."

event_blocks.summary: On Valentine's Day, 2017, a meeting in the Oval Office involving the Attorney General and FBI Director Jim Comey was intended to address the 'Michael Flynn issue,' with someone stating they wanted to 'ease up on him' because 'he's a really good guy' and asking Comey to 'just kind of ease up on him' and 'hope you can let this go.' Following this, Donald Trump appeared in dramatic black-and-white close-ups, looking contemplative as the recounted dialogue continued with Comey's reply, 'I agree he is a good guy, but said no more,' a sentiment visually reinforced by a declassified document highlighting the phrase 'He is a good guy' underlined in red. The President then attempted to speak with Comey alone, with Attorney General Jeff Sessions lingering momentarily before leaving, a situation Comey perceived as Sessions knowing the President should not be meeting alone with the FBI Director, and which led Comey to believe his 'antenna were going up and saying, this is evidence of a guilty mind right here, what he's about to ask me to do.' A declassified document further showed the red-underlined phrase 'The President thanked everyone and said he wanted to speak with me alone,' signed 'JBC 2/14/17,' prompting Comey, described as 'fairly sweaty-palmed,' to begin typing down the words he could remember, 'that scared of what this is that has just happened.' Frank Montoya, Jr. explained that 'if it happened and you didn't write it down, it didn't happen,' and that the President was 'walking himself down this trail to an investigation,' while an exterior shot of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building was shown. Comey then headed to the Justice Department to confront Jeff Sessions, quoting Comey's urgent words to the Attorney General: 'Comey pulls the Attorney General aside and says, you can't leave me.'

Gwenda Blair.description: A woman with short, white hair, wearing a vibrant pink blouse, speaking in a softly lit room with bookshelves in the background, identified by on-screen text as 'Author, The Trumps'.

Man in Dark Suit and Purple Tie.description: A man in a dark suit and purple tie, speaking calmly in a dimly lit setting, providing commentary as a male narrator recounts the President's repeated attempts to clear the room to speak with Jim alone.

Legal Document.description: A typed declassified document with text describing a private meeting in the Oval Office, featuring a red-underlined phrase: 'The President thanked everyone and said he wanted to speak with me alone,' emphasizing a key moment of isolation and private conversation, signed 'JBC 2/14/17' with a handwritten date '2/14/17' beside it, visually reinforcing Carol Leonnig's narration about Comey's documentation.

Woman in Blue Blazer.description: A woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, wearing a blue blazer, speaking with wide eyes and an expression of surprise or concern, visually reacting as a female narrator discusses Attorney General Jeff Sessions lingering and Comey's suspicions about the President's desire for a private meeting.

Donald Trump.description: Shown in dramatic black-and-white close-ups, with intense, contemplative expressions, first looking directly at the camera, then downward, emphasizing seriousness and focus, appearing as Carol Leonnig narrates Comey's urgent need to document the President's words.

Carol Leonnig.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red beaded necklace, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Carol Leonnig, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, her clear voice narrating Comey's actions of urgently documenting a private meeting with the President, describing him as 'fairly sweaty-palmed' and 'that scared.'

Mary McCord.description: A woman with shoulder-length gray hair, wearing a gray blazer, speaking with a composed, authoritative tone, identified by on-screen text as 'Mary McCord, Fmr. Acting Asst. Attorney General'.

Michael Isikoff.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie, speaking in a dimly lit room, with on-screen text identifying him as 'MICHAEL ISIKOFF, Co-Author, Russian Roulette', and heard stating that actions discussed are 'really in direct contravention of policies that have been in place ever since Watergate'.

Document Text Highlight.description: A typed legal document with the phrase 'He is a good guy' underlined in red, visually reinforcing the spoken quote 'he's a really good guy' from the audio.

Black-and-White Close-Up of Man.description: A dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man’s face, partially shadowed, with short, graying hair and intense eyes, wearing a dark suit, conveying seriousness and contemplation, shown in two different expressions—first looking directly at the camera, then looking downward, reinforcing the gravity of the situation discussed by Frank Montoya, Jr.

Frank Montoya, Jr..description: A man with graying hair, wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Frank Montoya, Jr., Fmr. FBI Special Agent in Charge' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, his serious, analytical voice emphasizing the importance of documenting events, stating 'if it happened and you didn't write it down, it didn't happen,' and discussing the President potentially facing investigation, later quoting Comey's urgent plea to the Attorney General: 'you can't leave me.'

J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building.description: A large, imposing government building with a modern, blocky design, shown from the street on a gray day, with cars moving along the road in front, symbolizing federal law enforcement and institutional authority, appearing as Frank Montoya, Jr. discusses the implications of the President's actions.

Woman in Blazer.description: A woman with shoulder-length gray hair, wearing a dark blazer, speaking seriously in a dimly lit room, likely delivering an interview or commentary, her clear voice discussing 'interference by the White House in investigations undertaken by the department or the bureau.'

raw_segments: The segment opens with Gwenda Blair, identified by on-screen text as 'Author, The Trumps,' speaking in a softly lit room with bookshelves, wearing a vibrant pink blouse. She calmly states, "On Valentine's Day, 2017, there was a meeting in the Oval Office between the Attorney General and the Director of the FBI, Jim Comey." This transitions to a series of dramatic black-and-white close-ups of Donald Trump, showing intense, contemplative expressions as he looks directly at the camera and then upward, with shadows accentuating his features. A male narrator explains, "As the meeting ended, the President tried to empty the room. He wanted to speak to the FBI Director alone." The mood shifts to a woman in a blue blazer, appearing surprised or concerned with wide eyes, as a female voice adds, "And Attorney General Jeff Sessions kind of lingers. And Comey thinks that's because Sessions knows the President should not be meeting alone with the FBI Director." This is followed by a close-up of a declassified document, with text describing a private meeting in the Oval Office, specifically highlighting and zooming in on the red-underlined phrase: 'The President thanked everyone and said he wanted to speak with me alone.' A different male narrator's calm voice recounts, "The AG lingered momentarily by my chair. But the President thanked him and said he wanted to meet with Jim. He repeated this at least one more time to usher people out." The segment then cuts to a man in a dark suit and purple tie, speaking calmly in a dimly lit setting, before returning to another black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, this time with a more animated expression, mouth slightly open as if mid-sentence. A third male narrator speculates, "If you're Comey, you're sitting there and thinking, what is it the President needs to say to me that he can't say in front of the Attorney General. Jim Comey's a long-time prosecutor and right away, I suspect his antenna were going up and saying, this is evidence of a guilty mind right here, what he's about to ask me to do." Gwenda Blair's voice briefly returns, saying, "He finally gets to..." before the audio cuts.

The video opens with Gwenda Blair, author of 'The Trumps,' speaking in a softly lit room with bookshelves, wearing a vibrant pink blouse, as a narrator discusses two individuals in a room and the 'Michael Flynn issue,' recounting how someone 'began by saying he wanted to talk about Mike Flynn,' and asked, 'can you just kind of ease up on him, he's a really good guy.' This leads to a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump with intense, contemplative expressions, first looking directly at the camera, then upward, while the recounted dialogue continues with 'I hope you can let this go' and 'I replied by saying, I agree he is a good guy, but said no more.' The scene then highlights a typed document with the phrase 'He is a good guy' underlined in red, visually reinforcing the spoken quote. Carol Leonnig, a reporter from The Washington Post, then appears, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red beaded necklace, as a new voice questions whether 'the president [is] asking the FBI director to stop looking at Russian interactions with the campaign' and if 'he [is] trying to shut down a counterintelligence probe.' The video cuts back to a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, now with a more animated expression and mouth slightly open, as the speaker continues, stating 'Trump's talking to the director of the FBI about an ongoing investigation by the FBI. And at that point, he's really, from Comey's perspective, crossed the line.' Michael Isikoff, co-author of 'Russian Roulette,' then appears in a dimly lit room, wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie, speaking with a serious demeanor, and is heard stating that this is 'really in direct contravention of policies that have been in place ever since Watergate to not have...' The sequence briefly shows a black-and-white image of a man in a suit, partially obscured by shadows, before Mary McCord, former Acting Assistant Attorney General, is shown speaking with a composed, authoritative tone, wearing a gray blazer, with on-screen text identifying her role, as the audio continues to discuss the implications of such actions. The overall sequence uses alternating interview segments and dramatic black-and-white visuals to build tension and emphasize the political and legal gravity of the topic.

The segment opens with a woman in a dark blazer, her shoulder-length gray hair visible, speaking seriously in a dimly lit room, her clear voice discussing the 'interference by the White House in investigations undertaken by the department or the bureau.' This transitions to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, his face partially shadowed and contemplative, as Carol Leonnig, identified as 'Carol Leonnig, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, appears in a dimly lit setting, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red beaded necklace. Her clear voice narrates how Comey, 'fairly sweaty-palmed,' leaves a meeting, goes to his car, and begins 'typing down the words, the phrases that he can remember the President said,' because he's 'that scared of what this is that has just happened.' A close-up of a declassified document then appears, showing the red-underlined phrase 'The President thanked everyone and said he wanted to speak with me alone,' signed 'JBC 2/14/17,' visually reinforcing the urgency of Comey's documentation. Frank Montoya, Jr., identified as 'Fmr. FBI Special Agent in Charge' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, then speaks directly to the camera in a dark suit, his serious voice explaining the adage that 'if it happened and you didn't write it down, it didn't happen,' and how the President was 'walking himself down this trail to an investigation.' During his commentary, an exterior low-angle shot of the imposing J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building is shown, followed by a black-and-white close-up of a man in a suit, his face partially obscured by shadow, looking intensely forward. A distinct electronic sound effect marks a transition before a male narrator states, 'Once again, Comey and Trump were at odds. Comey headed to the Justice Department to confront Jeff Sessions about what had just happened.' Frank Montoya, Jr. then returns, quoting Comey's urgent words to the Attorney General: 'Comey pulls the Attorney General aside and says, you can't leave me.'

raw_segments: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man in a suit, his face partially shadowed, conveying a serious and intense mood, while a male voice (later identified as Matt Apuzzo) discusses the impropriety of being alone with the president and the need for backup. The scene transitions to a color interview with Matt Apuzzo from The New York Times, who, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, elaborates that the Attorney General's job is to insulate the FBI from inappropriate political interference, questioning if "Sessions realized that he did not do what was expected of him" and if he had the "spine to do it." The video then cuts back to black-and-white footage of an older man in a suit and striped tie, looking downward with a solemn expression, while a narrator's voice explains that "Sessions knew how things worked at the Justice Department" and had been a United States Attorney in Alabama, building a reputation as a "rock-ribbed conservative" which formed the foundation of his run for the Senate. This narration continues over a color interview with Jeffrey H. Smith, former CIA General Counsel, who is seen wearing glasses, a dark suit, and a maroon tie in a dimly lit setting, but does not speak in this segment. The narrative shifts to archival black-and-white footage of a man in a suit gesturing emphatically while speaking at a table, then to another man speaking into a microphone, as the narrator quotes a campaign message about undermining "honesty, hard work, and discipline." This leads into a vintage television screen displaying a 1996 campaign ad for Jeff Sessions for U.S. Senate, featuring him in a blue shirt with a woman and child in the background, accompanied by a voice-over declaring, "For Senate, Jeff Sessions." The final shot returns to black-and-white, showing a man in a light shirt and dark tie, seated and looking off to the side with a pensive expression, possibly in a courtroom or formal setting, as the narrator concludes that Sessions "served in the Senate for 20 years as a law and order conservative," followed by another male voice briefly stating, "I've been beat up by Jeff Sessions in..."

The video opens with Jeh Johnson, former Secretary of Homeland Security, dressed in a pinstripe suit and light blue tie, speaking seriously in a dimly lit interview setting. He discusses "Senate Judiciary hearings" and describes Jeff Sessions as "very predictable, hardline, conservative." The scene then transitions to a vibrant outdoor political rally in Madison, Alabama, where Donald Trump, wearing a red 'Make America Great Again' cap, a dark suit, and white shirt, walks through a dense, cheering crowd, waving and acknowledging supporters amidst loud applause and crowd noise. The crowd holds signs like 'TRUMP' and 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!', with many capturing the moment on their phones. A narrator's voice explains that "In 2016, Sessions decided to play a long shot," supporting Donald Trump with the belief he'd become Attorney General. Trump takes the stage at a podium with a blue 'TRUMP' sign and American flag bunting, where J.D. Gordon, a Trump campaign adviser, joins him. Gordon, with an energetic rally voice, exclaims, "And I want to just introduce you to him for a second. Senator Jeff Sessions!" to roaring applause. The video then cuts back to an interview with J.D. Gordon, who, in a dark suit with a purple tie and an American flag pin, states in a calm, articulate tone that Sessions "was invaluable to the campaign in helping to get President Trump elected." The rally footage returns, showing Trump and Gordon at the podium, with Gordon's energetic voice declaring, "Wow! What a crowd this is!" as the crowd continues to cheer. Gordon's interview voice further explains that Sessions "was the first senator to endorse President Trump" and held a rally with him in Alabama, noting that "at the time, people didn't know if he'd be the president or not." The segment concludes with Gordon's rally voice saying, "At this time in American's history, we..." as the audio fades.

event_blocks.summary: At a rally in Madison, Alabama, Donald Trump, wearing a red 'Make America Great Again' cap and dark suit, stood at a podium adorned with 'TRUMP' and patriotic bunting, his amplified voice declaring the need to "make America great again" to a cheering crowd. The scene shifted to Cameron Smith, a former Jeff Sessions aide, explaining in a dimly lit interview that Trump benefited from Sessions' endorsement, which served as a "good housekeeping seal of approval" for conservatives, especially in the South. Returning to the rally, Trump, his amplified voice stating, "I'm proud to have you with us. God bless," walked off stage to applause. A montage of newspaper headlines, including one stating 'Russian officials were in contact with Trump allies, diplomat says,' accompanied the narrator's statement that "Trump appointed Sessions as Attorney General." As headlines transitioned to a chaotic hallway scene with reporters and cameras, the narrator noted, "But from almost the beginning, there was a problem," while a female reporter's voice mentioned, "As two more Trump campaign officials face scrutiny over their contacts with Russia." In a formal hearing room, Senator Al Franken and Senator Jeff Sessions, identifiable by his nameplate and red patterned tie, were shown as the narrator explained, "Interactions between Russians and the Trump campaign were becoming public. And at his confirmation hearing, Sessions was asked about them." Senator Franken's clear voice then quoted a CNN story about "a continuing exchange of information during the campaign between Trump surrogates and intermediaries for the Russian government," before beginning to address "Senator Franken..." as the audio cut.

raw_segments: The video opens with Donald Trump, wearing his signature red 'Make America Great Again' cap, dark suit, and white shirt, at a rally in Madison, Alabama. He gestures from a podium adorned with 'TRUMP' and patriotic bunting, his amplified voice declaring, "need to make America great again," to a cheering crowd holding supportive signs. The scene then shifts to Cameron Smith, identified as a former Jeff Sessions aide, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting. He calmly explains that "Because Sessions endorsed Trump, Trump didn't have to prove that he was a conservative, because Jeff Sessions is sort of the good housekeeping seal of approval for a lot of conservatives, particularly in the South where Trump was very successful." The video briefly returns to the rally where Trump, his amplified voice stating, "I'm proud to have you with us. God bless," walks off stage to applause. This is followed by a montage of newspaper headlines, such as 'Russian officials were in contact with Trump allies, diplomat says', while a male narrator states, "Trump appointed Sessions as Attorney General." The narrator continues, "But from almost the beginning, there was a problem," as the headlines transition to a chaotic hallway scene with reporters and cameras surrounding a man. A female reporter's voice adds, "As two more Trump campaign officials face scrutiny over their contacts with Russia." The narrative then moves to a formal, brightly lit hearing room, showing Senator Al Franken and Senator Jeff Sessions, who wears a red patterned tie and has a 'Sen. Jeff Sessions' nameplate. The narrator explains, "Interactions between Russians and the Trump campaign were becoming public. And at his confirmation hearing, Sessions was asked about them." In the same hearing room, Senator Al Franken's clear, formal voice is heard, quoting a CNN story: "CNN has just published a story, say quote, 'There was a continuing exchange of information during the campaign between Trump surrogates and intermediaries for the Russian government.'" Senator Franken's voice is heard again, beginning to address "Senator Franken..." before the audio cuts.

event_blocks.summary: Montage of black-and-white Washington Post newspaper clippings from February 10, 2017, displayed headlines like 'Sessions’ Russia troubles' and 'For Sessions, questions of perjury,' with red-underlined text highlighting his undisclosed meetings, as a male voice stated the "story became impossible for Sessions to ignore." Philip Rucker, from The Washington Post, appeared in a dimly lit close-up, stating that "He was compromised. Jeff Sessions misled the Senate during his confirmation hearing about that. He had lied about whether he had that meeting." As a document titled 'Justice Dept. ethics guide' on 'Motions for Recusal or Disqualification' was shown, the narrator explained that "Department of Justice staff advised Sessions to recuse himself from the investigation." A woman with shoulder-length gray hair in a dark blazer spoke seriously in a dimly lit room, asserting with a clear, authoritative tone that "It was appropriate for him to recuse, and I think the appearance of impropriety, if not the reality, is something that's that was really important to avoid." The narrator then revealed, "But the President had disagreed. He wanted Sessions to be his first line of defense against Comey." Yamiche Alcindor from PBS NewsHour, in a green dress, spoke expressively, explaining that "The President expects of Jeff Sessions and of really a lot of people around him, complete loyalty," and that "He wants people to protect him. He wants people to, if possible, bend the rules a bit so that they can make sure that they have his back." The scene then shifted to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, his face partially shadowed, conveying intensity, followed by a bright, sunny view of an aircraft carrier deck where a man in a dark suit and others walked toward a 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA' helicopter, over which a male commentator remarked, "The Democrats, as you can imagine, having a field day with this," before another voice briefly mentioned "lawmakers."

raw_segments: The segment opens with Senator Jeff Sessions, an older man with white hair in a dark suit and red patterned tie, seated at a formal hearing with his nameplate visible. He speaks into a microphone with a serious, attentive expression, stating in a clear, formal voice, "I'm not aware of any of those activities... I didn't have not have communications with the Russians." Other men in suits are seen behind him in the well-lit, official room. A mechanical whirring sound accompanies a close-up transition to a metallic, industrial-looking device with moving parts and a glowing light, suggesting archival or investigative work. A male narrator then explains, "But before long, The Washington Post reported Sessions had in fact met on at least two occasions with the Russian ambassador Sergey Kislyak." This is visually reinforced by a montage of 'The Washington Post' newspaper clippings from February 10, 2017, with headlines like 'Sessions’ Russia troubles' and red-underlined text detailing his undisclosed meetings. The narrator continues, "Senator Jeff Sessions spoke twice last year with Russia's ambassador to the United States, Justice Department officials said, encounters he did not disclose." The video cuts to Sessions outside, still in his dark suit and red tie, walking near a black SUV with a Virginia license plate, speaking to someone off-camera. The scene returns to newspaper clippings, showing headlines such as 'For Sessions, questions of perjury,' accompanied by a black-and-white photo of Sessions. His voice returns, defensively stating, "Well, I have not met with any Russians at any time to discuss any political campaign and those remarks are unbelievable to me and are false." A female reporter's voice is then heard, reporting, "Republicans urged the Trump White House to quickly resolve the controversy," followed by another male reporter stating, "Attorney General Jeff Sessions has repeatedly resisted," before the narrator concludes, "In the face of an onslaught of..." The overall tone is investigative and serious, highlighting the contradiction between Sessions’ testimony and the revealed contacts.

event_blocks.summary: Donald Trump disembarked from a helicopter onto an aircraft carrier in Newport News, Virginia, wearing a red 'USA' cap and dark suit, to send a message to Jeff Sessions regarding investigations into Trump's campaign. Trump walked through the carrier's interior into a control room where reporters' questions about his confidence in his campaign manager and Sessions' recusal were posed. The narrator explained Trump's insistence that Sessions not recuse himself, which Trump then stated publicly. The scene shifted to Jeff Sessions at a Department of Justice podium, announcing his recusal from investigations related to the presidential campaigns. Later, Alan Dershowitz commented on Trump's frustration with an appointee who had 'abdicated responsibility,' and Robert Ray discussed presidents' feelings of powerlessness. Aerial shots of Washington D.C. were shown as reporters discussed James Comey going public with testimony and Sessions being 'on the sidelines.' Robert Mueller was then seen entering a crowded hearing room. Inside the hearing room, a man in a dark suit and yellow-striped tie was escorted away by officers amidst a swarm of reporters. Jeh Johnson reflected on Comey's communication and Trump's likely reaction to his testimony. A digital timer in the hearing room showed '4:55' as a man in a navy uniform with gold epaulets looked at documents. Visually identified as Robert Mueller, but audibly James Comey, testified at a podium, confirming the FBI was investigating links between Trump campaign associates and the Russian government. Matt Apuzzo analyzed the anticipation surrounding Comey's confirmation. The hearing room was shown again with photographers capturing the event, and the timer read '4:53' as the man at the podium spoke with a serious expression. Peter Baker stated that Trump perceived something as 'a direct and public threat to his presidency.' In a hearing room, Mr. Nunes questioned if there was evidence of coordination between Trump White House officials and Russian intelligence services, to which James Comey responded, 'Not a question I can answer.' A man with glasses and graying hair analyzed Comey's statement as 'the death knell' for Comey in Trump's mind. J.D. Gordon described the situation as 'surreal' and 'a waking nightmare' that put those close to Trump under a cloud. A black-and-white close-up showed Donald Trump with a stern expression. A narrator stated that with Flynn caught lying and Sessions recused, another showdown was inevitable, and that the press uncovered more meetings between Russians and Trump campaign officials. A man with graying hair and a beard explained that Jared Kushner had met with a Russian banker close to Putin and discussed setting up a backchannel communication with Moscow. The narrator identified Kushner as the focus of many stories, and a man with dark hair and glasses remarked on the increasing number of meetings being revealed. Kushner was described as being under scrutiny. Carol Leonnig discussed actions with Russians being investigated and whether Kushner had been honest about them. Black-and-white footage showed Donald Trump and likely Donald Trump Jr. walking through a room, with a voice stating the scandal had reached the president's family. Carrie Johnson expressed concern that the investigation could encompass Trump's children and son-in-law, making him nervous. A color clip showed Trump disembarking from Marine One in rainy weather, with mentions of Kushner and Trump being inundated by headlines and under pressure from Comey, leading to a foul mood at his Bedminster, New Jersey club. Sarah Ellison stated that Trump and his family felt they were being swept into the investigation. A black SUV drove toward a stately brick house. Robert Costa continued commentary on the investigation. Trump, wearing a 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN' cap and 'TRUMP' jacket, spoke animatedly as a narrator discussed ending the investigation by removing Comey, detailing 'new whirlwind developments' in Bedminster where Trump made a consequential decision without senior staff. Sarah Ellison stated there was 'essentially no adult in the room' to understand the implications. Black-and-white footage showed Trump holding a phone, as the narrator explained Trump decided to 'get rid of Comey.' Carol Leonnig described Trump's decision to fire Jim Comey based on 'gut instinct and raw anger' without consultation, and characterized a dictated letter to Comey as a 'rant.' Color footage showed Donald Trump and Melania Trump descending the stairs of Air Force One. At night, Trump, determined to stop Comey, descended Air Force One stairs, followed by Melania. Aerial views of the White House and Washington Monument were shown, followed by stylized black-and-white shots of men in suits. A female voice commented that Comey knew more than he was letting on, and a male voice added that Comey opened another investigation into potential collusion. The narrator explained Trump was preparing to send a letter. A woman in a blue blazer revealed that White House counsel Don McGahn freaked out upon learning of the prepared document, saying, 'you don't want to send that.' Steve Bannon was also stunned, quoting Bannon saying, 'You can't get rid of this guy, Jim Comey. This would be a terrible, terrible mistake. It's going to cause a firestorm.' The final shot was a black-and-white close-up of a man, inferred to be Jim Comey, at a table surrounded by microphones, appearing under intense scrutiny. Jane Mayer explained that Bannon understood the history of Watergate and obstruction of justice if Trump fired Comey. McGahn asserted that the document could not be used as a basis for firing Comey. The White House Counsel learned that Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein also had concerns with Comey. A meeting was set up between Rosenstein, Attorney General Jeff Sessions, and the President. The narrator stated the President made it clear he wanted to fire James Comey, and the directive was for Sessions and Rosenstein.
