raw_segments: The segment opens with a dramatic tone as Donald Trump disembarks from a military helicopter, marked 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA,' onto the sunlit deck of a huge new aircraft carrier in Newport News, Virginia, under a clear blue sky. He wears a red 'USA' cap, dark suit, and patterned tie, greeted by saluting naval officers and military personnel in dress uniforms. A male narrator explains that Trump's visit is a 'big event' and a 'photo op to send a message to Jeff Sessions.' Trump then walks alongside a group of men in suits and military officers, including one in a flight jacket with 'CVN 71' and a U.S. Navy patch, through the carrier’s interior corridors and into a dimly lit control room filled with monitors. Here, on-screen text appears, posing urgent questions from reporters: 'Mr. President, do you still have confidence in your campaign manager?' and 'Can Sessions recuse himself from investigations into your campaign?' A female reporter's voice is heard asking these very questions, along with follow-ups about Sessions' contact with the Russian ambassador. The narrator further explains that the President had already insisted his counsel tell Sessions not to recuse himself, and now Trump was saying it publicly. The scene abruptly shifts to Jeff Sessions, an older man with white hair, at a Department of Justice podium, with the DOJ seal and American flag behind him. The narrator reveals that without consulting Trump, Sessions called this press conference. Sessions, wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie and holding a green cup, formally addresses reporters, stating in his own clear voice, 'I have now decided to recuse myself from any existing or future investigations of any matter relating in any way to the campaigns for President of the United States,' before concluding with 'Thank you all, take care.' Throughout the segment, a consistent background music track underscores the unfolding political drama.

The segment opens with Senator Jeff Sessions, an older man with white hair in a dark suit and red patterned tie, walking away from a Department of Justice podium in Washington, D.C., against a backdrop of the DOJ seal and an American flag. Dramatic, investigative music plays throughout. The scene transitions to a dimly lit interview setting where Harvard Law School Professor Alan Dershowitz, wearing glasses, a dark suit, blue shirt, and striped tie, speaks directly to the camera about Trump losing his best chance to shut down an investigation and his frustration with an appointee who 'abdicated responsibility.' Following this, former Independent Counsel Robert Ray, also in a dark suit and tie, speaks in a similar interview setup, explaining that presidents feel a lack of power, though not typically so early in an administration. The video then cuts to a wide, overcast aerial shot of Washington D.C., showcasing the Lincoln Memorial, Washington Monument, and U.S. Capitol Building. As reporters' voices discuss FBI Director James Comey going public with testimony before Congress, and 'Sessions on the sidelines,' the final sequence shows Robert Mueller, a man in a dark suit and yellow-striped tie, walking through a government hallway flanked by police officers and aides. He then enters a formal, crowded hearing room, filled with reporters and officials, taking his seat at a large wooden table as cameras flash and onlookers watch intently, setting the stage for a high-stakes proceeding.

The video opens with a chaotic scene inside a formal government hearing room, where a man in a dark suit and yellow-striped tie is being escorted away by uniformed officers, while reporters and photographers swarm the area, capturing the tense moment with flashing cameras. The scene then cuts to a calm interview setting with Jeh Johnson, a bald man in a pinstripe suit and light blue tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, reflecting on Jim Comey's remarkable communication and how Mr. Trump likely watched his testimony. I'm honored to be here representing the people of the FBI. I have been authorized by the Department of Justice to confirm..." The video returns to the hearing room, showing a group of photographers and reporters capturing the event from the front row, with cameras pointed toward the podium. The final shot is a close-up of the man at the podium (visually Mueller, audibly Comey) speaking with a serious expression, as the digital timer now reads '4:53', emphasizing the gravity of the proceedings.

The final shot is a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, standing in a doorway with a stern, pensive expression, his hand slightly raised, with curtains and a window visible behind him, as a male narrator's voice declares, "The head of the FBI dropped two bombshells landing at the White House doorstep," followed by a brief female voice saying, "Comey put..."

A male narrator's deep voice states, "With Flynn caught lying, and Sessions having recused himself, another showdown was becoming inevitable." The scene transitions to an exterior shot of the White House under overcast skies, with a wet pavement reflecting the building and surrounding trees, as the PBS logo appears. Another male narrator mentions, "A notorious Moscow bank is now part of the invest..." The camera then zooms in on the White House’s front facade, showing illuminated windows behind white columns and a black iron fence, with the FRONTLINE logo appearing. The first narrator continues, "And the press had also ratcheted up the pressure, as they uncovered more meetings between Russians and Trump campaign officials." The view shifts to a closer shot through the fence, emphasizing the building’s grandeur and security. The scene cuts to a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting, explaining, "Kushner had met with a top Russian banker who was known to be close to President Putin during the transition. He had also had a conversation with Sergey Kislyak, the Russian ambassador, and there had been even this discussion about setting up a backchannel communication to Moscow." This is followed by a black-and-white close-up of a man in profile, with a serious expression, partially obscured by shadows. The first narrator then identifies him, stating, "Many of the stories focused on Jared Kushner, the president's son-in-law." The video then cuts to a man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit, speaking with an animated expression in a dimly lit room, urgently remarking, "This is just not normal. It just seems like there's more and more meetings, just keep, they just keep coming out." The first narrator adds, "Now, Kushner was increasingly under scrutiny," as the final shot returns to the black-and-white profile of Jared Kushner, now shown in a more formal, centered composition, with a focused and intense gaze, reinforcing a sense of gravity and scrutiny. A female voice is heard saying, "Jared is learning that his actions, especially with Michael Flynn, his involvement..."

The video opens with Carol Leonnig from The Washington Post, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting, her voice discussing how "actions with various Russians is under investigation as well, and whether he's been honest about them and what was going on in those conversations." The scene transitions to black-and-white archival footage of Donald Trump and a younger man, likely Donald Trump Jr., walking through a formal room with American flags, as a male voice states, "Kushner headline today and tonight. The scandal had now reached the president's family." Carrie Johnson from NPR then appears in a similar interview setup, her voice explaining, "The notion that there's this investigation at the Justice Department and the FBI that could encompass his children, his son-in-law, and possibly his business interests, is getting him very, very nervous." A color clip follows, showing Donald Trump disembarking from Marine One under umbrellas in rainy weather, saluting military personnel, while the audio mentions "Jared Kushner, President's son-in-law" and describes Trump as "inundated by the headlines and under pressure from Comey," having "left Washington" for his country club in Bedminster, New Jersey. A female voice adds that it's a "rainy weekend in Bedminster," so Trump, "supposed to be out golfing," is "stuck inside" and "in a sort of foul mood anyway." Sarah Ellison from The Washington Post then speaks in a warm-lit interview, her voice stating, "The president was frustrated, his family was frustrated, they felt like they were being swept into this rip tide of an investigation." This is followed by a shot of a black SUV driving on a wet driveway toward a stately brick house. The final segment features Robert Costa, Moderator of Washington Week, speaking directly to the camera, his voice continuing the commentary about the investigation, noting "they thought it was..." (audio cuts off).

The segment opens with a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, wearing a 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN' cap and a 'TRUMP' jacket, speaking animatedly with expressive hand gestures against a dark background, as a male narrator discusses the possibility of ending an investigation by removing Comey. Tense background music underscores the narration, which then details "new whirlwind developments" in Bedminster on a rainy weekend, where Trump, without senior staff, made a consequential decision. The scene cuts to Sarah Ellison from The Washington Post, a woman with shoulder-length blonde hair in a purple blouse, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit setting, stating there was "essentially no adult in the room," specifically no "legal adult" to understand the political and legal implications. The video returns to black-and-white footage of Trump, first in a stern, pensive close-up, then in profile holding a phone to his ear, as the narrator explains Trump decided he "would get rid of Comey." Next, Carol Leonnig from The Washington Post, a woman with short brown hair and a red beaded necklace, speaks seriously in a dimly lit room, describing Trump's conclusion to fire Jim Comey based on "gut instinct and raw anger" without consultation. She later characterizes a dictated letter to Comey as a "rant," where Trump was "unloading all of the reasons that Comey has failed him." The final scene shifts to color, showing Donald Trump descending the red-carpeted stairs of Air Force One in a dark suit, with Melania Trump following behind in a light coat and dark skirt, as the presidential seal is visible on the aircraft door, while the narrator mentions "On Sunday, Donald Trump..."

The video opens at night with Donald Trump, dressed in a dark suit, descending the red-carpeted stairs of Air Force One, followed closely by Melania Trump, as a narrator states he is "determined to carry out his plan to stop Jim Comey once and for all." The scene transitions to a daytime aerial view of the White House, framed by the Washington Monument, establishing a political context, followed by a close-up of a dimly lit interior through a window. A series of black-and-white, stylized shots of men in suits with serious or contemplative expressions appear, as a female voice comments that "Comey has been indicating that he knows so much more than he's letting on," and a male voice adds that "Comey opens up another investigation" into potential "collusion between Trump associates." The narrator then explains that "Trump was preparing to take the fateful step of sending the letter." A woman in a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera with an earnest expression, reveals that "Word gets back to Don McGahn, the White House counsel, that this document has been prepared. And he freaks out," with another male voice quoting McGahn saying, "you don't want to send that." The narrator adds that "Even Trump's abrasive advisor Steve Bannon was stunned," and the woman in the blue blazer continues, quoting Bannon, "'You can't get rid of this guy, Jim Comey. This would be a terrible, terrible mistake. It's going to cause a firestorm.'" The final shot is a black-and-white close-up of a man, inferred to be Jim Comey, at a table surrounded by microphones and glasses, appearing under intense scrutiny with a stern and focused demeanor, as the narrator concludes that "Although Trump had the executive power to fire Comey..."

The segment opens with a tense, investigative background score accompanying a series of stark black-and-white close-ups of political figures, starting with a stern, contemplative man in a dark suit and striped tie seated at a table. The scene then transitions to Jane Mayer, identified as 'The New Yorker,' speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, wearing a dark blazer, as she explains that 'Bannon has more of a sense of history than a lot of the people who are in that White House,' understanding 'the history of Watergate' and 'about obstruction of justice and what it could look like if Trump fires Comey.' The visuals cut back to black-and-white footage of two serious men in suits standing side by side, followed by a shadowed man in profile, a somber man in glasses, and a grave man with graying hair and a beard looking directly at the camera. A male voice, likely a narrator, dramatically quotes 'McGahn' asserting, 'There's no way... that this document can be used as the basis for firing Jim Comey. No way, no how. Give me the document.' The narrator continues, explaining that 'The White House Counsel had an alternative,' and 'McGahn had separately learned that Rod Rosenstein, the Deputy Attorney General, also had concerns with Jim Comey.' The narrator describes a deal being brokered, quoting a figure telling the President, 'You should really talk to Rod Rosenstein.' The video then shifts to color, showing a man in a suit and red tie walking briskly past a black SUV, as the narrator states, 'They set up a meeting between Rosenstein, Attorney General Jeff Sessions, and the President.' The final frames return to black-and-white, featuring a dramatically silhouetted man with glasses in profile and a pensive man with glasses looking downward, while the narrator concludes that 'The President lets them know he wants to fire James Comey, that's clear, and the directive for Sessions and Rosenstein is...' before the audio fades.

event_blocks.summary: Philip Rucker of The Washington Post discussed the rationale behind memos justifying Comey's firing, while a narrator identified Rosenstein, detailing his 'lifelong learning of the law' at the University of Pennsylvania and Harvard Law School, shown through yearbook photos, a formal group photo, and an older man speaking in a classroom, culminating in Rosenstein at a podium labeled 'OFFICE OF THE UNITED STATES ATTORNEY.' Robert Ray, identified as 'Fmr. Independent Counsel,' described Rosenstein as a 'lifelong Republican' and a member of 'the Federalist Society.' The narrator explained Rosenstein's involvement with Ken Starr in the Independent Counsel's investigation of Bill Clinton and his subsequent appointments as United States Attorney by George W. Bush and Barack Obama, with Rucker adding that Rosenstein 'has served in both Democratic and Republican administrations as the US Attorney in the District of Maryland,' enjoying a 'bipartisan reputation.' A man in a dark suit and patterned tie spoke about a 'professional' whose reputation was 'well earned,' as black-and-white footage showed a man in a suit and glasses, with the narrator stating that 'Rosenstein would build a case against Jim Comey's handling of the FBI.' A woman in a blue blazer explained that Rosenstein, a 'boy scout' with 27 years in the Justice Department, believed Comey 'violated the Justice Department norms by talking too much about Hillary Clinton during the election.' An exterior night shot of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building preceded the narrator's statement that 'The President wanted the memo as soon as possible. It was a rush job. He delivered it the next day.' A close-up of a legal memorandum dated May 9, 2017, from Rod J. Rosenstein, Deputy Attorney General, showed underlined phrases like 'the Director made serious mistakes' and 'the conclusion of the email investigation was wrong.' The man in the dark suit and glasses returned, stating that 'Rod Rosenstein's memo echoed what a lot of the Hillary Clinton campaign people had been saying for months. That Comey had inserted himself into the election, he made himself too public, he had taken on a role that did not really belong to him,' with the memorandum further underlining 'the Director ignored another longstanding principle: we do not hold press conferences to release derogatory information about the subject of a criminal investigation.'

Philip Rucker.description: A man with a beard and dark hair, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'PHILIP RUCKER, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. He speaks about the rationale behind memos to fire Comey and later describes Rosenstein's bipartisan reputation.

Robert Ray.description: Former Independent Counsel, wearing glasses, a dark suit, a white shirt, and a blue polka-dot tie, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting with a serious and engaged expression. He describes Rosenstein as a lifelong Republican and a member of the Federalist Society.

Older Man with Glasses (Classroom).description: An older man with white hair and glasses, shown in black-and-white, wearing a light shirt and tie, speaking in a classroom setting with a whiteboard in the background, appearing to lecture or teach, while a narrator discusses Rosenstein's legal education.

Man with Glasses.description: A man with dark hair and glasses, shown in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious, authoritative male voice, commenting on Rosenstein's professionalism and later on the memo's content regarding Comey's actions and public role.

Group Photo (Law School/Professional Cla.description: A black-and-white group photo of a formal gathering, likely a law school or professional class, with multiple individuals in suits and ties, including the man with glasses seated in the front row, and a man in a suit with a patterned tie standing in the back row.

J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building.description: A large, imposing government building with a modern, blocky design, shown from the street at night, with illuminated signage and architectural details, symbolizing federal law enforcement and institutional authority.

Legal Document.description: A typed memorandum from Rod J. Rosenstein, Deputy Attorney General, dated May 9, 2017, with key phrases underlined in red, such as 'the Director made serious mistakes' and 'we do not hold press conferences to release derogatory information,' emphasizing ethical and procedural violations within the FBI.

Woman in Blue Blazer.description: A woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, wearing a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, her clear, articulate female voice providing analysis on Rod Rosenstein's background and his view that Comey violated Justice Department norms.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Philip Rucker from The Washington Post, seen in a dimly lit, professional color interview setting, speaking about the rationale behind writing memos to justify Comey's firing. The scene then transitions to a dramatic, high-contrast black-and-white montage, beginning with a side-profile of two men in suits, one with glasses, followed by a serious close-up of the man with glasses. A narrator's voice identifies this individual as Rosenstein, detailing how he 'would do his part' in a historic decision, having spent his life 'learning the law' at the University of Pennsylvania and Harvard Law School, as visuals show a younger Rosenstein in a yearbook-style photo and later in a formal group photo, possibly from law school, and an older man with glasses speaking in a classroom. The montage culminates with Rosenstein standing at a podium labeled 'OFFICE OF THE UNITED STATES ATTORNEY,' delivering a speech. The video cuts back to a subdued, formal color interview with Robert Ray, identified as 'Fmr. Independent Counsel,' who, with a serious expression, describes Rosenstein as a 'lifelong Republican' and a member of 'the Federalist Society.' The narrator's voice continues over the black-and-white visuals, explaining Rosenstein's involvement with Ken Starr in the Independent Counsel's investigation of Bill Clinton, and his subsequent appointments as United States Attorney by George W. Bush and Barack Obama. Philip Rucker's voice returns, noting that Rosenstein 'has served in both Democratic and Republican administrations as the US Attorney in the District of Maryland,' enjoying a 'bipartisan reputation.'

The video opens with a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious, authoritative male voice about someone being a 'professional' whose reputation was 'well earned,' all set against a dramatic, investigative musical score. The visual transitions to black-and-white footage of another man in a suit and glasses, conveying a somber tone, as a male narrator states that 'Rosenstein would build a case against Jim Comey's handling of the FBI.' The scene shifts to a woman in a blue blazer, speaking earnestly with expressive hand gestures, her clear, articulate female voice explaining that Rod Rosenstein, a 'boy scout' who served 27 years in the Justice Department, believed Comey 'violated the Justice Department norms by talking too much about Hillary Clinton during the election.' An exterior night shot of the grand J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building establishes the federal law enforcement context, while the narrator explains that 'The President wanted the memo as soon as possible. It was a rush job. He delivered it the next day.' The video then cuts to a close-up of a legal memorandum dated May 9, 2017, from Rod J. Rosenstein, Deputy Attorney General, with key phrases underlined in red, such as 'the Director made serious mistakes' and 'the conclusion of the email investigation was wrong.' The man in the dark suit and glasses returns, speaking directly to the camera, his serious voice echoing that 'Rod Rosenstein's memo echoed what a lot of the Hillary Clinton campaign people had been saying for months. That Comey had inserted himself into the election, he made himself too public, he had taken on a role that did not really belong to him.' The final shot focuses on another section of the memorandum, underlining 'the Director ignored another longstanding principle: we do not hold press conferences to release derogatory information about the subject of a criminal investigation,' as the male narrator emphasizes this breach of protocol, maintaining the overall investigative and serious tone.

event_blocks.summary: In a series of news segments and interviews, the firing of FBI Director James Comey by President Donald Trump is dissected. Michael Isikoff notes that Trump's stated reason for the firing, Comey's actions regarding Hillary Clinton's emails, was an excuse, contrasting it with Trump's history of face-to-face dismissals. Timothy O’Brien describes how Trump's bodyguard, Keith Schiller, was sent to deliver the pink slip to the FBI, but was unable to gain entry. Jeff Sessions and Matt Apuzzo are shown as the news breaks, with a reporter announcing Comey's removal from heading the FBI. Bob Anderson recounts Comey's disbelief upon hearing the news in Los Angeles, initially thinking it was a mistake, a sentiment echoed by news reports calling the firing a "big shock" that "they did not see this coming at the FBI." An aerial view captures a high-security motorcade departing, while Frank Montoya, Jr. calls the firing a "major sucker punch" given Comey's standing. The narrative shifts to a nighttime aerial view of the U.S. Capitol Building and the White House amidst "shock, anger, and chaos," with reporters struggling for explanations and the White House press office thrust into a difficult position. Sean Spicer, White House Press Secretary, appears on Fox News, referencing Rod Rosenstein's determination regarding Comey's lost confidence. Carrie Johnson of NPR states the White House's message was clear: "we fired Comey because he botched the Hillary Clinton investigation. Period." On CNN, Anderson Cooper questions Kellyanne Conway about the timing, and Conway points to recommendations from Trump, Attorney General Sessions, and Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein. Howard Kurtz observes that the press was skeptical of the Rosenstein memo explanation, and Cooper challenges the focus on Hillary Clinton's emails, noting Trump had previously praised Comey. A man with graying hair and a beard questions the situation, while Cooper and Conway debate the logic of the firing, with Cooper identifying Rosenstein as the Deputy Attorney General. An exterior night shot of the U.S. Department of Justice building is shown, and a man with glasses expresses that Rosenstein was surprised by the blame, while Conway reiterates Trump's decision based on Rosenstein's recommendation. The man with glasses explains Rosenstein felt his reasons were being used as a pretext and was "none too pleased," a sentiment Conway echoes before the man with glasses concludes, "Rosenstein is blown..."

Timothy O’Brien.description: Identified by on-screen text as 'Author, TrumpNation,' he is seen in a color interview, wearing a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, speaking with a serious expression. His voice is heard describing how Trump's bodyguard delivered the 'pink slip' to the FBI and the difficulty of gaining entry to the FBI building.

J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building.description: A close-up of its sign reading 'J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, 935 Pennsylvania Avenue N.W.' is shown in color. The FBI is frequently mentioned in the audio as the target of the 'pink slip' delivery and the organization James Comey was removed from.

White House.description: Exterior view at night, with warm lighting on the facade, green lawns, and a black iron fence, establishing a secure and official atmosphere, contextualized by audio mentioning a 'closely kept secret here at the White House' and the White House struggling to offer an explanation, later hitting the airwaves with an official story.

Michael Isikoff.description: Identified by on-screen text as 'Co-Author, Russian Roulette,' he is seen in a color interview, speaking expressively with hand gestures. His voice is heard explaining Trump's motivations for firing the FBI director and contrasting it with his past actions on 'The Apprentice'.

James Comey.description: Seen in a black-and-white shot, standing in profile, wearing a dark suit, looking downward with a serious expression, suggesting contemplation. He is mentioned by name in the audio as the recipient of a note and as the person 'removed from heading the FBI'.

Matt Apuzzo.description: Seen in a color interview, wearing a dark blazer over a patterned shirt, gesturing as he speaks, identified by on-screen text as 'The New York Times'.

Jeff Sessions.description: Seen in a black-and-white shot, walking with a stern expression, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, with a small white pin on his lapel.

Donald Trump.description: Seen seated at a desk in a black-and-white scene resembling the Oval Office, wearing a dark suit and American flag pin, with a surreal animated horse floating above his head, gesturing. Audio discusses his actions regarding firing the FBI director and his past behavior on 'The Apprentice'.

Private Jet.description: A white private jet with registration N834X, shown accelerating on a runway and taking off, with a sleek design, dark-tipped tail, and visible landing gear retracting during ascent, its high-profile departure coinciding with audio announcing 'breaking news' of a presidential firing.

Motorcade.description: A convoy of black SUVs and a limousine, including a Suburban with an American flag, driving on the tarmac under a dusky sky, indicating high-security transport, shown in aerial views exiting a building, driving under an overpass, and along a road with palm trees.

Frank Montoya, Jr..description: Fmr. FBI Special Agent in Charge, shown in a color interview wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking with a focused and earnest expression, with on-screen text identifying him under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. He describes Comey's firing as 'a major sucker punch' and notes Comey's widespread respect within the FBI, even among those who weren't his biggest supporters.

Bob Anderson.description: Fmr. FBI Exec. Asst. Director, shown in a color interview wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie, speaking with a serious and composed expression in a dimly lit room, with on-screen text identifying him under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. He recounts in detail how then-FBI Director James Comey learned of his firing while giving a talk, describing Comey's initial disbelief and his staff's confirmation of the news.

Reporters and Journalists.description: A group of individuals, including a woman in a beige blazer holding a smartphone and a man in a dark suit with a microphone, gathered outside the White House at night, actively recording and interviewing, their presence underscored by audio describing the 'press office' being 'thrust out there to explain a decision' they had no part in, and the 'chaos' for reporters trying to understand the situation.

Capitol Building.description: Shown in a nighttime aerial view, illuminated with warm lights, its dome and columns clearly visible, with the Washington Monument in the background, symbolizing legislative power, while audio describes 'mounting outrage on Capitol Hill' and lawmakers 'questioning the country's very foundation'.

Sean Spicer.description: White House Press Secretary, shown on a Fox News broadcast screen, wearing a dark suit and blue patterned tie, speaking with a serious expression, with the White House in the background on the screen, and on-screen text identifying him and the program 'Lou Dobbs Tonight'. He is heard in a clear, professional male voice, explaining the rationale behind Comey's firing, specifically mentioning Rod Rosenstein's determination.

Man in Dark Suit and Light Blue Shirt.description: A man with short, graying hair, wearing a dark suit and light blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting, his voice heard at the beginning discussing someone being 'dismissed or fired'.

Howard Kurtz.description: Author of 'Media Madness,' shown in a dimly lit room, wearing a dark suit and blue striped tie, speaking with expressive hand gestures, identified by on-screen text under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. He has a clear, analytical male voice, discussing the White House's narrative regarding the Comey firing and the press's reception of it, later describing the situation as 'mind-bending'.

Carrie Johnson.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Carrie Johnson, NPR' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. She has a clear, assertive female voice, directly quoting the White House's stated reason for Comey's firing.

Anderson Cooper.description: A male news anchor with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and black tie, seated in a CNN studio, delivering a 'BREAKING NEWS' segment with a serious and focused demeanor, interjecting 'That makes no sense' during a discussion with Kellyanne Conway and identifying 'Rod Rosenstein' as the Deputy Attorney General.

Kellyanne Conway.description: Counselor to the President, shown on the right side of the CNN split-screen, wearing a light-colored blouse with a gold necklace, speaking with a composed expression, her blonde hair styled in waves, and identified by on-screen text. She explains the President 'took the recommendation of his Deputy Attorney General,' defends the decision as making 'complete sense because he has lost confidence in the FBI director,' reiterates that 'President Trump fired Comey because of Rosenstein's recommendation,' and notes that 'Rod Rosenstein is not particularly happy that the White House is painting the blame on him.'

Lou Dobbs.description: A man with short, light brown hair, wearing a dark suit, checkered shirt, and red patterned tie, seated in a studio with a blue background, appearing serious and focused during the Fox News broadcast.

Man with Graying Hair and Beard.description: A man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit over a checkered shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned room, delivering commentary on the President's decision to fire the FBI director and questioning 'what is going on here?'

U.S. Department of Justice.description: Exterior view at night, with illuminated signage reading '950 Pennsylvania Avenue, NW' and 'U.S. Department of Justice,' featuring classical architecture, a large stone urn, and lit windows, establishing a formal and official atmosphere, shown while commentary about the Justice Department is being discussed.

Man with Glasses.description: A man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, speaking with a serious expression in a dimly lit room, later appearing in a dramatic black-and-white profile shot with his face partially shadowed. He provides commentary, stating that 'Rosenstein was surprised he was receiving all the blame,' and explains that 'Rod Rosenstein sees what's happening and that his reasons are being used as the pretext to justify the President's action,' adding that Rosenstein 'was none too pleased' and concluding with 'Rosenstein is blown...'

Black-and-White Close-Up of Man.description: A dramatic black-and-white profile shot of the man with glasses, his face partially shadowed, with a bright light source behind him, creating a high-contrast, contemplative mood, emphasizing his serious expression.

raw_segments: The video opens with a black-and-white archival-style shot of Donald Trump, seated at a desk resembling the Oval Office, wearing a dark suit and American flag pin, with a surreal animated horse floating above his head as he gestures. A male voice, later identified as Michael Isikoff, states that "Trump doesn't care about what Comey did to hurt Hillary Clinton, but it becomes the excuse... the White House uses to explain why they were firing the FBI director." The scene transitions to a color interview with Michael Isikoff, co-author of 'Russian Roulette,' who continues, explaining that "Donald Trump had fired hundreds of people face-to-face on The Apprentice. This time, as president, it would be different." An exterior shot of the White House under overcast skies, with a Secret Service car and barricades, then appears as another male voice, later identified as Timothy O’Brien, describes how Trump "sends his long-time bodyguard in a White House car with the pink slip over to the FBI to deliver the bad news." Timothy O’Brien, author of 'TrumpNation,' is then seen in a color interview, elaborating that "Keith Schiller, the president's body man, can't get into the FBI," and that the FBI is not a place where one can simply walk in with a note for James Comey from the White House. He concludes that the bodyguard "dropped off the letter and left." A black-and-white shot of Jeff Sessions walking with a stern expression is shown, followed by Matt Apuzzo of The New York Times in a color interview. The video then cuts to a close-up of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building sign, and a black-and-white shot of a man, possibly James Comey, standing in profile looking downward, as a female news reporter's voice announces, "Breaking news, James Comey has been removed from heading the FBI."

The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man in a dark suit, his face partially shadowed, suggesting a tense moment, accompanied by tense, dramatic music. This transitions to a color interview with Bob Anderson, identified as 'Fmr. FBI Exec. Asst. Director,' who, with a composed and serious demeanor, recounts how then-FBI Director James Comey was giving a talk in Los Angeles when news broke that he had been fired by the President. The scene cuts back to the black-and-white profile shot of the man, now gesturing, before returning to a close-up of Bob Anderson, his expression more intense, as he describes Comey's initial disbelief, thinking it was a mistake, until a staff member confirmed the reality of the firing. News reports interject, stating, "President Trump has fired James Comey as Director of the FBI. It comes without warning," and calling it "a big shock" that "they did not see this coming at the FBI." An aerial view then shows a high-security motorcade of black SUVs and a limousine exiting a building, driving under an overpass, and along a road lined with palm trees, suggesting a high-profile departure in a warm climate, with news reports mentioning "News helicopters were waiting as Comey left the FBI field office." The video cuts to Frank Montoya, Jr., 'Fmr. FBI Special Agent in Charge,' who, with a focused and earnest expression, describes Comey's firing as "a major sucker punch," noting that Comey was "widely regarded throughout the organization," even by those who weren't "fans." The segment concludes with an aerial shot of a private jet on a tarmac, surrounded by black vehicles and personnel, hinting at a high-profile arrival or departure, while a news report draws comparisons to "President Richard Nixon's Saturday Night Massacre."

The minute opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting, his voice discussing someone being "dismissed or fired." The scene quickly transitions to an aerial view of a white private jet, registration N834X, accelerating down a runway and lifting off into the sky, as a news anchor's voice announces "Moments ago, breaking news that no one saw coming today, we learned that President Trump has fired..." Another male voice adds that it was "a very closely kept secret here at the White House." The footage then shifts to a nighttime aerial shot of the U.S. Capitol Building, illuminated against the dark cityscape with the Washington Monument visible, while a voice mentions "studying unprecedented Comey." A dramatic voiceover declares "Shock, anger, and chaos engulfed Washington," as the camera pans to the White House, also lit at night. A female reporter's voice describes "mounting outrage on Capitol Hill," with some lawmakers "questioning the country's very foundation." Outside, a group of reporters and journalists are gathered, holding microphones and recording devices, as a voiceover explains that "At the White House, they struggled to offer an explanation," and another reporter states, "The White House is not interested in getting to the bottom of this." The audio continues, detailing how the "press office is suddenly thrust out there to explain a decision they had no part in," and that "it was chaos" for reporters trying to figure out what was going on. The final shot is a television screen showing Sean Spicer, identified as 'White House Press Secretary' on a Fox News broadcast, speaking with a serious expression, with the White House visible in the background on the screen. A female news anchor's voice confirms, "The Comey firing came without warning," and a male voiceover adds, "By the time the White House hit the airwaves, the story the press office told was that Rosenstein's memo was the primary reason for the firing." Spicer's voice is then heard saying, "Deputy Attorney General..."

The video opens with a Fox News split-screen featuring Lou Dobbs and White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer. Spicer, in a dark suit and blue patterned tie, is heard in a clear, professional male voice explaining the firing of FBI Director Comey, referencing Rod Rosenstein's determination that the director had lost confidence. The scene then cuts to a FRONTLINE interview where Carrie Johnson of NPR, a woman with shoulder-length dark hair in a blue blazer, speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting. Her clear, assertive female voice states the White House's message: "we fired Comey because he botched the Hillary Clinton investigation. Period." The video transitions to a CNN broadcast with a 'BREAKING NEWS' banner, showing a split-screen of Anderson Cooper, with white hair and glasses, and Kellyanne Conway, Counselor to the President. Cooper, in a dark suit, is heard in his distinct, calm, and inquisitive male voice asking, "to those who say why now, why fire James Comey now, what do you say?" Conway, wearing a light-colored blouse and gold necklace, responds with a composed, articulate female voice, pointing to "the three letters that were received today," including those from President Trump, Attorney General Sessions, and Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, to whom the FBI Director reports. A brief cut shows Howard Kurtz, author of 'Media Madness,' speaking in a dimly lit room. His clear, analytical male voice discusses how "all the people spinning on behalf of the White House told the press that the Comey firing was based on a memo from Rod Rosenstein... which had to do with Comey's performance in the Hillary Clinton investigation. Well, the press wasn't buying that." The CNN broadcast returns, with Cooper's voice interjecting, challenging that "most of this letter focuses on on Hillary Clinton's emails, this is stuff that as a can they Donald Trump praised James Comey for." The scene briefly returns to Kurtz, whose voice describes the situation as "a mind-bending situation, right? Because..." The news segments maintain consistent studio blue backdrops and professional attire, while FRONTLINE interviews feature softer, more intimate lighting.

The video opens with a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit, speaking directly to the camera in a warm-toned room, commenting on the President's decision to fire the FBI director and questioning "what is going on here?" The scene transitions to a CNN broadcast split-screen featuring Anderson Cooper, a male anchor, on the left, and Kellyanne Conway, Counselor to the President, on the right, discussing the breaking news of 'TRUMP FIRES FBI DIRECTOR COMEY.' Conway explains that the President "took the recommendation of his Deputy Attorney General," to which Cooper interjects, "That makes no sense." Conway defends the decision, stating it "makes complete sense because he has lost confidence in the FBI director." Cooper then identifies the Deputy Attorney General as "Rod Rosenstein." The broadcast cuts to an exterior night shot of the U.S. Department of Justice building, with illuminated signage. A man with glasses, dark hair, and a dark suit then appears, speaking seriously in a dimly lit room, stating that "Rosenstein was surprised he was receiving all the blame." Conway's voice is heard again, reiterating that "President Trump fired Comey because of Rosenstein's recommendation." The man with glasses continues, explaining that "Rod Rosenstein sees what's happening and that his reasons are being used as the pretext to justify the President's action," adding that Rosenstein "was none too pleased." Conway's voice is heard once more, noting that "Rod Rosenstein is not particularly happy that the White House is painting the blame on him," before the man with glasses concludes, "Rosenstein is blown..." in a dramatic black-and-white profile shot, his face partially shadowed.

event_blocks.summary: A silhouetted man, possibly James Comey, is shown in contemplation as Carol Leonnig discusses threats of resignation; the scene shifts to Leonnig speaking seriously, accompanied by tense music, then to a color view of the White House and a Fox News broadcast announcing 'COMEY OUT: PRESIDENT ABRUPTLY FIRES FBI DIRECTOR.' News anchors debate Rod Rosenstein's memo and potential White House fears, with a narrator identifying Fox News as the president's preferred network and a dramatic anchor declaring, 'You're fired! President Trump ousting the FBI Director James Comey.' The narrator emphasizes the extensive coverage, while another anchor mentions a live report. A Marine opens a car door for a white vehicle driving away, as the narrator reveals President Trump celebrated Comey's firing privately with Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov and Ambassador Sergey Kislyak. Leonnig returns, sighing 'Oy' and commenting on the meeting with the highest-ranking Russian official the president had met so far. The narrative then shifts to a black-and-white scene of Donald Trump laughing heartily with Lavrov and another man, creating a jovial atmosphere, which Carrie Johnson of NPR notes is hard to believe, stating the president is 'essentially celebrating with the Russian diplomat.' Johnson, speaking seriously, is juxtaposed with a male voice discussing the firing of the man heading the probe into Trump campaign ties to Russia. The video returns to Trump laughing with the men, as Johnson highlights the absence of US-based reporters. A man in a dimly lit setting explains that Russians brought a TASS photographer who took propaganda photos. Karen DeYoung of The Washington Post appears, while a male voice remarks on the 'terrible optics.' Trump is quoted saying, 'we're gonna have a great relationship,' calling the investigation a 'total irritant' and Comey's firing a 'great weight lifted,' labeling Comey 'a nut job.' The segment concludes with black-and-white Washington Post headlines like 'COMFY FIRESTORM' and 'TRUMP LASHES OUT AT COMEY AND CRITICS,' as a male voice declares, 'First the firing, now the fallout,' noting the negative reaction was gaining momentum, all underscored by tense background music.

Marine in Dress Uniform.description: A man in a dark Marine dress uniform with a white hat and red trim, opening the door of a black vehicle, standing beside it with a formal posture. The audio provides context that this action is related to the arrival or departure of 'unlikely White House guests' (Russian officials) after Comey's firing.

Carol Leonnig.description: A woman with short brown hair, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red beaded necklace, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting. Her voice is heard providing commentary on the political events, including a sigh of 'Oy' and discussing 'That meeting,' conveying a sense of concern and analysis.

White House.description: Exterior view at daytime, seen through a black iron fence, with green lawns and trees, establishing a secure and official atmosphere. The audio mentions 'the White House feared' and 'unlikely White House guests,' providing direct context for its appearance in relation to the political events.

Silhouetted Man.description: A man in a suit, shown in black-and-white, first in profile and then bowed, his face in shadow, evoking contemplation or distress. The audio context of 'the president keeps saying this' and the subsequent news of Comey's firing strongly imply this is James Comey, reflecting the gravity of the events discussed.

Fox News Broadcast.description: A live news segment with a red and yellow graphic, featuring a man in a suit raising his hand under oath (identified as James Comey by audio), with a chyron reading 'COMEY OUT: PRESIDENT ABRUPTLY FIRES FBI DIRECTOR,' and a panel of three anchors discussing the event. The audio identifies it as 'the president's go-to network' and features multiple news anchors (male and female) discussing the memo, Comey's firing, and the Russian meeting, including a dramatic declaration of 'You're fired!'

White Vehicle.description: A white car, possibly a limousine, seen from the side as it drives away, partially obscured by foliage, suggesting official transport. The audio context links its movement to the departure of 'unlikely White House guests' (Russian officials) from the White House.

Sergei Lavrov.description: A man in a dark suit and tie, standing beside Donald Trump in a black-and-white scene, smiling and engaging in a lighthearted interaction, identified in the audio as the 'Russian diplomat' with whom the president is celebrating.

Karen DeYoung.description: A woman with short, dark, curly hair, wearing a dark blazer over a white collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit indoor setting, identified by on-screen text as 'KAREN DEYOUNG, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo.

Newspaper Article.description: A black-and-white montage of 'The Washington Post' headlines from February 10, 2017, with bold titles such as 'COMFY FIRESTORM' and 'TRUMP LASHES OUT AT COMEY AND CRITICS,' featuring red-underlined text revealing the political fallout from Comey's firing, and a black-and-white photo of Trump. These headlines visually represent the 'fallout' and 'negative reaction' to the Comey firing discussed in the audio.

Carrie Johnson.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Carrie Johnson, NPR' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. Her voice is heard commenting, 'you can't believe it really happened. But the president is essentially celebrating with the Russian diplomat,' and highlighting, 'No US-based reporters. No American White House reporters are in the room.'

Donald Trump.description: Seated at a desk in a black-and-white scene resembling the Oval Office, wearing a dark suit, striped tie, and American flag pin, with a surreal animated horse floating above his head, gesturing with his right hand, laughing heartily, and placing a hand on Sergei Lavrov’s shoulder. He is quoted saying, 'we're gonna have a great relationship,' describing the investigation as a 'total irritant for me,' and stating that 'Jim Comey's firing lifted a great weight for me,' calling Comey 'a nut job.'

raw_segments: The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a silhouetted man, likely James Comey, in deep contemplation, as a female voice (Carol Leonnig) discusses someone threatening to resign if the president continues a certain course of action. The scene cuts to Carol Leonnig, speaking with a serious demeanor, her commentary underscored by tense, dramatic music. A shift to color reveals the White House, followed by a Fox News broadcast with a chyron announcing 'COMEY OUT: PRESIDENT ABRUPTLY FIRES FBI DIRECTOR.' News anchors, both male and female, discuss Rod Rosenstein's memo and question if Comey was fired due to White House fears. A male narrator then identifies Fox News as 'the president's go-to network,' as a dramatic female anchor declares, 'You're fired! President Trump ousting the FBI Director James Comey.' The narrator continues, stating the news was 'all Comey, all the time,' while another male anchor mentions a live report from Kevin Corke. The visuals then show a Marine opening a car door and a white vehicle driving away, as the narrator reveals the president celebrated Comey's firing behind closed doors with 'unlikely White House guests': Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov and Ambassador Sergey Kislyak. Carol Leonnig returns, sighing 'Oy' and commenting on 'That meeting,' which a female anchor describes as with 'the highest-ranking Russian official the president has met so far,' before Leonnig trails off, describing it as 'like a...'

The segment opens with a black-and-white scene of Donald Trump, Sergei Lavrov, and another man in suits, where Trump is seen laughing heartily and placing a hand on Lavrov’s shoulder, creating a jovial atmosphere. A female voice, later identified as Carrie Johnson, comments that 'you can't believe it really happened. But the president is essentially celebrating with the Russian diplomat.' This is followed by a cut to Carrie Johnson from NPR, speaking with a serious expression, as an unidentified male voice discusses the firing of the man heading the probe into Trump campaign ties to Russia. The video returns to Trump laughing with the other two men, while Carrie Johnson's voice highlights that 'No US-based reporters. No American White House reporters are in the room.' A man with a beard and gray hair, wearing a dark suit, then appears in a dimly lit interview setting, as a brief Russian voice is heard, followed by an English male voice explaining that 'The Russians came in with a photographer from their state media agency TASS who took photos of this event,' which were 'used to some effect in Russia as propaganda.' The black-and-white scene of Trump gesturing and laughing reappears, further emphasizing the informal atmosphere. Karen DeYoung from The Washington Post is then shown speaking directly to the camera, while an unidentified male voice remarks on the 'terrible optics' of the situation, stating, 'you couldn't have scripted it worse.' The scene briefly returns to Trump, as a male voice quotes him saying, 'we're gonna have a great relationship,' and describing the investigation as a 'total irritant for me,' adding that 'Jim Comey's firing lifted a great weight for me,' and calling Comey 'a nut job.' The segment concludes with a montage of black-and-white Washington Post headlines, including 'COMFY FIRESTORM' and 'TRUMP LASHES OUT AT COMEY AND CRITICS,' with red underlines, as a male voice declares, 'First the firing, now the fallout,' and notes that 'the negative reaction to the Comey firing was gaining momentum.' Throughout, a consistent, slightly tense background music underscores the unfolding political controversy.

event_blocks.summary: Sensational newspaper headlines like 'TRUMPER TANTRUM' and 'FED UP' accompanied a news-style intro sound, highlighting media reactions to Donald Trump's firing of James Comey, with a female voice stating, "President Trump now facing outrage after firing Comey," and a male voice noting, "It is hard to over... To address the crisis, the president went on his old network for a one-on-one interview." The scene shifted to a television displaying an 'NBC NIGHTLY NEWS EXCLUSIVE TRUMP INTERVIEW,' with Lester Holt announcing, "This is NBC Nightly News with Lester Holt. Tonight, stunning revelations from President Trump in our NBC News exclusive interview." Trump, initially in a dark suit with a red tie against a White House backdrop, was then shown walking through a grand, dimly lit room with red carpeting, flanked by Secret Service and media, before sitting down with Holt, who wore a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, holding a notepad. In this formal room, adorned with American flags and the Presidential Seal, Trump, now in a blue striped tie and an American flag pin, spoke animatedly, gesturing with his hands, stating, "He's the decider... this Russia thing with Trump and Russia is a made-up story, it's an excuse by the Democrats for having lost an election that they should have won." A dramatic, investigative background score began and continued throughout. Holt intently asked, "Monday you met with the Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein... Did you ask for a recommendation?" Trump responded, "What I did is I was going to fire Comey. My decision. It was not..." Holt interjected, "You had made the decision before they came in the room," to which Trump firmly stated, "I I was going to fire Comey." This interview was interspersed with commentary from Matthew Miller, a former Department of Justice official, who appeared in a dimly lit setting, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, speaking directly to the camera, remarking, "It is a dramatic moment to see the president come out and not only completely undermine the case that his White House had been making... The president comes out and demolishes that case immediately." Trump was then heard again, asserting, "I was going to fire regardless of recommendation," as Miller continued, "I think there's a level on which President Trump doesn't want to be portrayed as just doing the bidding of some aides who write a memo." The segment then cut to a nighttime exterior shot of the illuminated White House, followed by a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Trump with his eyes closed, and then a profile shot of him smiling in a white cap behind a fence, during which a narrator's voice explained that "The interview backfired. The President's comments contradicted the White House. It triggered questions about whether Comey's firing was an attempt to obstruct justice by the President," and highlighted "contradictions and confusion from the White House over the President admitting Russia was on his mind." Philip Rucker of The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text, with a beard and dark hair, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, spoke directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, commenting on Trump: "he often says what he believes, and if you just wait long enough, he'll tell you the truth." The segment concluded with a woman in a blue blazer and black top, with shoulder-length blonde hair, speaking animatedly with hand gestures, stating that "For his part, even out of a job, Jim Comey was not going to be sidelined. Jim Comey, who's not known for staying silent... begins to defend him." The video then opened with a black-and-white, grainy shot of a man in a suit entering a dimly lit garage, setting a tense, clandestine atmosphere, as a narrator explained that Comey decided to force the Justice Department to name a special counsel, transitioning to a close-up of a typed memo with the phrase 'He is a good guy' underlined in red. A montage of newspaper printing presses in motion symbolized information dissemination, culminating in a close-up of a New York Times front page headline: 'TRUMP APPEALED TO COMEY TO HALT INQUIRY INTO AIDE,' with the subheading 'Ex-F.B.I. Chief Noted Request in Memo: ‘I Hope You Can Let This Go.’' A news reporter's voice detailed the memo's contents, stating it was written after an Oval Office meeting. Frank Montoya, Jr., a former FBI Special Agent in Charge, appeared in a color interview, earnestly describing the event as 'Jim's leak,' adding that 'it was information that he had that he passed on to the New York Times,' and concluding, 'I think that was the straw that broke the camel's back.' A black-and-white shot of James Comey looking upward with a serious expression followed, as background voices discussed 'another cloud of controversy' and 'calls demanding a special prosecutor.' A woman in a blue blazer then spoke with intensity in a color interview, asserting that Jim Comey 'knows way more about political dynamics and the way Washington works than most people in this story.' The narrator confirmed that 'as Comey hoped, the call for a special counsel grew louder,' before the segment concluded with an exterior view of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building at dusk, illuminated with American flags, establishing the institutional setting.

Matthew Miller.description: Former Department of Justice official (2009–11), shown in a dimly lit interview setting, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, speaking directly to the camera with a composed expression, identified by on-screen text as 'FRONTLINE'; he provides commentary, stating, "It is a dramatic moment to see the president come out and not only completely undermine the case that his White House had been making... The president comes out and demolishes that case immediately," and "I think there's a level on which President Trump doesn't want to be portrayed as just doing the bidding of some aides who write a memo."

Donald Trump.description: Seated in a formal interview setting, wearing a dark suit, blue striped tie, and an American flag pin, speaking with expressive facial movements and a serious demeanor, with the American flag and Presidential Seal in the background. He is heard denying the 'Russia thing' as a 'made-up story' and is later shown in dramatic black-and-white close-ups and a profile shot while a narrator discusses the fallout from his comments and the Comey firing.

Lester Holt.description: NBC News anchor, wearing a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, seated across from Trump, holding a notepad, listening intently, and occasionally gesturing during the interview; he asks, "Monday you met with the Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein... Did you ask for a recommendation?" and interjects, "You had made the decision before they came in the room."

Philip Rucker.description: A man with a beard and dark hair, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified by on-screen text as 'PHILIP RUCKER, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. He is heard commenting on Donald Trump's communication style, noting that 'he often says what he believes'.

White House.description: Exterior view at nighttime, illuminated against a dark sky, with trees and lawns visible, establishing a secure and official atmosphere.

Woman in Blue Blazer.description: A woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, wearing a blue blazer and black top, speaking animatedly with expressive hand gestures in a dimly lit room, delivering commentary on Jim Comey's political acumen, stating he 'knows way more about political dynamics and the way Washington works than most people in this story'.

Frank Montoya, Jr..description: Fmr. FBI Special Agent in Charge, shown in a color interview wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking with a focused and earnest expression, with on-screen text identifying him under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, as he describes the event as 'Jim's leak' and 'the straw that broke the camel's back'.

J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building.description: Close-up of the building's sign reading 'J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, 935 Pennsylvania Avenue N.W.', shown in color.

James Comey.description: In a black-and-white shot, standing in profile, wearing a dark suit, looking downward with a serious expression, suggesting contemplation or tension, while being discussed as the subject of the memo and the leak, and his understanding of political dynamics.

Newspaper Article.description: A black-and-white montage of 'The Washington Post' headlines from February 10, 2017, with bold titles such as 'COMFY FIRESTORM' and 'TRUMP LASHES OUT AT COMEY AND CRITICS,' featuring red-underlined text revealing the political fallout from Comey's firing, and a black-and-white photo of Trump.

Document Text Highlight.description: A typed legal document with the phrase 'He is a good guy' underlined in red, emphasizing a controversial or critical point in the narrative.

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit, formal setting, a man in a dark suit and purple tie speaks directly to the camera, initiating a narrative that transitions to a solemn black-and-white shot of a man in a suit and striped tie, hands clasped, who begins by stating that Rod Rosenstein, finding his reputation "dragged through the mud," appointed a special counsel to "undo the damage." A subsequent profile close-up shows the same man, now wearing glasses, emphasizing Rosenstein's need to "stabilize the investigation" and the "Department of Justice," which had been "under siege." A voiceover then defines a special counsel as "the most powerful investigative weapon the Justice Department wields." The scene shifts to Robert Costa, identified as 'Moderator, Washington Week' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking composedly in a softly lit room. A dramatic black-and-white close-up of an older man with graying hair, in a suit and patterned tie, is illuminated against a dark background as the voiceover reveals Rosenstein "named one of the nation's legendary prosecutors, former FBI Director Robert Mueller, to be the special counsel." The man with glasses reappears, highlighting Mueller's "ultimate presence" as "discreet, but also experienced." The video concludes with a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking earnestly to the camera, asserting that Mueller is "the guy who has no problem with holding people accountable, being direct and driven to get the answer," and that "he's going to do it right... in accordance with the rules." The alternating warm and stark black-and-white lighting underscores the narrative's gravity, accompanied by faint background audio of a female news anchor reporting on White House crisis and the Justice Department's special counsel appointment.

Robert Costa.description: A man with dark hair, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking directly to the camera in a formal interview setting, identified by on-screen text as 'ROBERT COSTA, Moderator, Washington Week' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo.

Man with Glasses.description: A man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, shown in black-and-white, first standing solemnly with hands clasped, then in a close-up profile speaking with a focused expression, his voice clear and analytical as he discusses Rod Rosenstein's decision to appoint a special counsel and Robert Mueller's suitability for the role.

Man in Dark Suit and Light Blue Shirt.description: A man with short, graying hair, wearing a dark suit and light blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting, his expression earnest and engaged, his voice firm and direct as he describes the special counsel as someone who will 'hold people accountable' and 'do it right' in accordance with the rules.

Man in Dark Suit and Patterned Tie (Blac.description: An older man with graying hair, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, shown in a dramatic black-and-white close-up against a dark background, his face illuminated with an intense, contemplative expression, his voice serving as a solemn narrator, describing the special counsel as 'the most powerful investigative weapon' and identifying Robert Mueller as the appointee.

A solemn voiceover then describes a special counsel as "the most powerful investigative weapon the Justice Department wields." The man with glasses then returns, emphasizing that in Mueller, "you have the ultimate presence who's discreet, but also experienced." Throughout, the lighting alternates between warm, focused tones and stark black-and-white, emphasizing the weight of the narrative, with faint background audio of a female news anchor reporting "breaking news" about the White House in crisis and the Justice Department appointing a special counsel.

event_blocks.summary: Amidst tense, dramatic music, a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt warns of being "very afraid" of Bob Mueller, setting the stage for a discussion involving Yamiche Alcindor from PBS NewsHour, Chris Whipple, and J.D. Gordon, a Trump campaign advisor, about the "Russia collusion probe" and its potential expansion. The scene transitions to a nighttime exterior of the White House, punctuated by a police officer in a yellow jacket and flashing police car lights, where the man in the dark suit narrates that Rosenstein's announcement of Mueller's appointment coincided with President Trump's meeting with Attorney General Sessions. Chris Whipple notes that "President Trump doesn't like to get bad news," while Yamiche Alcindor reports that Trump "unleash[ed] all his anger on Jeff Sessions," blaming him for the situation; J.D. Gordon corroborates this, stating that "Trump was furious and took it out on Sessions and humiliated him," feeling endangered by the special counsel and losing his temper. The segment concludes with Yamiche Alcindor asserting that "Trump's law is loyalty to him and what he wants," as a black-and-white close-up shows Donald Trump in profile, looking downward solemnly.

Yamiche Alcindor.description: A woman with short, curly black hair, wearing a green dress with a V-neckline, speaking expressively in a softly lit room, identified by on-screen text as 'YAMICHE ALCINDOR, PBS NewsHour' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, providing articulate analytical commentary in a clear voice about the special counsel appointment, Trump's anger towards Sessions, and his demand for loyalty.

Police Car Lights.description: Flashing red and blue lights on a police car in the foreground, with the White House in the background, creating a sense of urgency and tension.

White House.description: Exterior view at nighttime, illuminated against a dark sky, with trees and lawns visible, establishing a secure and official atmosphere, serving as the backdrop for discussions about the President's meetings and Mueller's appointment.

Chris Whipple.description: A man with short brown hair, wearing a blue suit and striped tie, identified by on-screen text as 'Author, The Gatekeepers,' speaking in a dark interview setting, providing calm and analytical commentary in a clear voice about Trump's dislike for 'bad news' and the 'Russia collusion probe.'

Man with Graying Hair and Contemplative.description: An older man with graying hair, shown in black-and-white, his face illuminated with intense, contemplative expression against a dark background, suggesting a moment of reflection or gravity, visually reinforcing the serious tone of the discussions.

Police Officer in Yellow Jacket.description: A police officer wearing a bright yellow jacket with 'POLICE' printed on the back, walking past the White House fence, indicating a security presence.

J.D. Gordon.description: A man with a receding hairline, wearing a dark suit, purple tie, and an American flag pin, speaking in a formal interview setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Trump campaign adviser,' providing commentary in a distinct, slightly gravelly voice about the probe expanding and Trump's furious reaction to Sessions.

Man in Dark Suit and Light Blue Shirt.description: A man with short, graying hair, wearing a dark suit and light blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting, his expression earnest and engaged, providing analytical commentary with a clear, serious voice, asking rhetorical questions about being 'afraid' of Mueller, and narrating events at the White House.

Donald Trump.description: Seated in a formal interview setting, wearing a dark suit, blue striped tie, and an American flag pin, speaking with expressive facial movements and a serious demeanor in black-and-white shots, with the American flag and Presidential Seal in the background; though he does not speak in this segment, his actions and reactions to 'bad news' and Jeff Sessions are extensively discussed by the commentators.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking seriously, asking if one should be worried about Bob Mueller and emphatically stating, "You should be afraid, you should be very afraid," all set to tense, dramatic background music. A dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man with graying hair, his face illuminated with an intense, contemplative expression, follows. Yamiche Alcindor, identified as from PBS NewsHour, is heard discussing the department naming a special counsel, while Chris Whipple, identified as an author, mentions the "Russia collusion probe," which J.D. Gordon, a Trump campaign adviser, adds "could expand." The scene shifts to a nighttime exterior of the White House, with a police officer in a yellow jacket and flashing police car lights, as the man in the dark suit narrates that "At the White House, the President happened to be meeting with Attorney General Sessions" when "Rosenstein called to announce Mueller's appointment." A White House window with a white object and an American flag is briefly shown. J.D. Gordon is then seen speaking in a formal interview, while Chris Whipple comments that "President Trump doesn't like to get bad news, and this was bad news. It was more than bad news, it was terrible news." Another black-and-white close-up of the man with graying hair, looking upward seriously, precedes Yamiche Alcindor, who speaks expressively, stating that Trump "unleash[ed] all his anger on Jeff Sessions, and plainly tells Jeff Sessions that you are the reason why all of this is happening." A black-and-white shot of Donald Trump speaking with an intense expression is shown. J.D. Gordon then adds that "Trump was furious and took it out on Sessions and humiliated him," and "obviously felt himself endangered by a special counsel and uh lost his temper." The segment concludes with a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump in profile, looking downward solemnly, as Yamiche Alcindor states, "Trump's law is loyalty to him and what he wants," with the dramatic music persisting.

event_blocks.summary: NPR's Carrie Johnson, in a blue blazer over a black top, spoke expressively in a dimly lit interview setting, questioning "where is my Roy Cohn?" and asserting that Jeff Sessions' actions would not be forgiven by Donald Trump, as dramatic, tense background music commenced. The scene shifted to a black-and-white close-up of a man's face, likely James Comey, gazing upward contemplatively, while a commentator noted Sessions "had had enough of the president's anger." Peter Baker of The New York Times, clad in a dark suit and patterned tie, addressed the camera directly in a warm-toned setting, as another voice described Sessions "bolting out of the White House, rushing out to his car." An outdoor sequence depicted a man in a dark suit, accompanied by another, walking toward a black SUV with Virginia license plate 'WRP-2855', while a third male voice explained Sessions was "resigning as Attorney General," feeling "distraught" and "at the end of his rope" after being "insulted by Trump." A man in a blue suit and striped tie then spoke seriously, as a fourth voice declared, "In the West Wing, all hell broke loose." A nighttime exterior shot of a white building's window, possibly the White House, with a dimly lit interior, preceded the third male voice recounting Don McGahn bursting into Priebus's office to announce a special counsel appointment and Sessions' resignation. A black-and-white close-up of a man in a suit, his face shadowed, was shown as a fifth voice quoted Priebus saying, "you're kidding me." The man in the blue suit spoke again, while the third voice described Priebus running to the West Wing parking lot, finding Sessions in his car, and banging on the door. The video then presented a black-and-white close-up of another man's face with wide, intense eyes, followed by a man in a dark suit walking down a hallway lined with framed pictures, his expression stern. Peter Baker, now gesturing with his hand, emphasized a point in his commentary, all against the persistent dramatic music. The segment opened with a close-up of a man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, urgently stating, "You gotta come out, you gotta come back in, you can't leave this way. You can't just blow up like this." Investigative music began as the scene transitioned to black-and-white footage of an older man in a suit and striped tie, looking out from a car window with a serious expression, while a male voice narrated about Priebus almost having to drag someone back into the West Wing, where Vice President Pence and Steve Bannon then joined to "talk Sessions off the ledge." Another man in a blue suit and striped tie spoke with a focused demeanor, as a female voice declared, "It's clear that the Mueller investigation is just getting started," and another male voice added that "The new Special Counsel Robert S. Mueller the III was just getting started" across town in a secure location. This was followed by a dramatic black-and-white close-up of an older man with graying hair, his face illuminated against a dark background, conveying gravity, as a male voice explained the demanding nature of becoming a special prosecutor, emphasizing the initial lack of staff, agents, prosecutors, or even basic supplies like a legal pad. The camera then panned across the modern glass facade of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, before returning to black-and-white footage of a man in a suit and patterned tie, bowed and looking down, evoking distress. Finally, Robert Ray, identified as 'Fmr. Independent Counsel,' appeared in a formal interview setting with a serious expression, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and blue polka-dot tie, with the 'FRONTLINE' logo visible, briefly heard saying "reasonable suspicion." The segment concluded with a black-and-white close-up of a man in a suit and polka-dot tie, his face illuminated with a stern, intense expression, as the investigative music continued.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a black-and-white portrait of a young Robert Mueller in a Marine Corps dress uniform, his serious expression illuminated by a spotlight, accompanied by a somber, investigative score. A woman in a blue blazer, speaking with concern in a dimly lit room, recounts Mueller's service, stating he "volunteered to serve in Vietnam as a United States Marine, highly decorated, wounded in action." The visuals shift to black-and-white close-ups of an older Robert Mueller in a suit and tie, his contemplative expressions captured from various angles, including a shot of him seated before a bookshelf. A voiceover reveals that in the 1990s, Mueller "had tried his hand in the private sector at a prestigious law firm. He hated it." Marc Fisher of The Washington Post, interviewed in color, explains that despite earning "$400,000 a year, he felt like he wasn't doing the Lord's work. He quit." Michael Isikoff, Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' then appears in a color interview, expressively detailing Mueller's decision to take "a substantial pay cut to become a line prosecutor," working "homicide in Washington D.C.," and how his "great joy was putting away bad guys and answering his phone, Mueller, Homicide." The video returns to a black-and-white shot of Mueller standing before the FBI seal, emblazoned with 'FIDELITY,' 'BRAVERY,' and 'INTEGRITY,' followed by a dramatic, high-contrast profile close-up emphasizing his stern expression. Marc Fisher's voice returns, asserting, "Bob Mueller cares about one thing and one thing only... indicting bad guys and putting them in prison." A voiceover concludes by noting Mueller, "a Republican, he'd run the FBI for both George W. Bush and Barack Obama."
