event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a black-and-white close-up of a man's intense face, introducing Robert Mueller as "back at the center of the action" with a team of "more than three dozen attorneys, investigators," described by one commentator as "ninja assassins of attorneys." The scene shifts to the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, establishing an official setting, before cutting to a color interview with a younger man with dark hair and glasses, who likens Mueller's team to "the Avengers" reassembling in Washington for a new mission. Subsequent black-and-white footage shows an older man with graying hair walking seriously and a bald man with glasses adjusting his tie outdoors. A commentator highlights Mueller's team as "maybe the A-team of prosecutors for an entire generation," naming Aaron Zebley, a former FBI agent and prosecutor, and Michael Dreeben, a highly skilled attorney with over 100 Supreme Court arguments. A woman with shoulder-length gray hair, identified as Jeannine Re, a respected prosecutor, speaks animatedly, followed by Andrew Weisman, a "scorch earth prosecutor," expressing concern. The audio also notes Mueller's inclusion of Greg Andres, an experienced mob prosecutor. The segment transitions to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man with his finger to his lips, followed by a side profile of another man, conveying seriousness, as a narrator describes them as "killers, Steve Bannon calls them." The narrator explains Mueller's team had broad authority to investigate Russian interference, the Trump campaign, and possible obstruction of justice following the Comey firing. A color interview features a man with glasses in a dark suit explaining how the President's actions, particularly after firing James Comey, created an issue of obstruction of justice now under federal investigation. A legal document, a May 9, 2017 memorandum from Rod J. Rosenstein, is shown with underlined phrases like 'the Director made serious mistakes' and 'he needed loyalty.' The narrator details Mueller's evidence, including Comey's memos about the President asking for loyalty, to go easy on Mike Flynn, berating Sessions, and using the Rosenstein memo. The segment concludes with Robert Bennett, a former President Clinton lawyer, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting, stating that "improper acts, even if they are not in and of themselves criminal, amount..." A consistent, slightly dramatic background score plays throughout.

Maybe it does, maybe it doesn't, but that issue didn't even exist before he fired James Comey, now it does. Now it's the subject of a federal investigation by a special prosecutor.'

Rosenstein, Deputy Attorney General, dated May 9, 2017, with key phrases underlined in red, such as 'the Director made serious mistakes' and 'we do not hold press conferences to release derogatory information,' emphasizing ethical and procedural violations within the FBI. The audio explicitly references its content, detailing 'Comey's memos,' 'President asking Comey for loyalty,' 'go easy on Mike Flynn,' 'berating of Sessions for his recusal,' and 'the use of the Rosenstein memo,' directly correlating with the underlined phrases shown.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man’s face, his expression intense and contemplative, as the audio introduces Robert Mueller being "back at the center of the action," having "quietly gathered a team of more than three dozen attorneys, investigators." The scene transitions to a modern glass building, likely the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, with reflections of an American flag and trees, establishing an official setting. A male voiceover explains that from a secure location, Mueller "built a formidable team," which one commentator describes as "ninja assassins of attorneys." The camera then cuts to a color shot of a younger man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a suit and red tie, speaking directly to the camera in a formal interview setting. He elaborates on Mueller's team, likening it to "the Avengers movies where all the superheroes are spread across the globe," called by "Bob Mueller" to "reassemble together in Washington to take on this new mission." This is followed by a series of black-and-white shots, including an older man with graying hair and a patterned tie walking with a serious expression, and a bald man with glasses adjusting his tie as he walks outdoors near a stately building. The commentator continues, stating that "Team Mueller has assembled, maybe the A-team of prosecutors for an entire generation," and begins naming key members: "Aaron Zebley, who is an FBI agent before becoming a prosecutor," and "Michael Dreeben, who is one of the smartest people I know," having "argued over 100 Supreme Court cases." The video then shows a woman with shoulder-length gray hair, wearing a dark top, speaking animatedly with her hands clasped, identified as "Jeannine Re, who is a highly respected prosecutor in the US Attorney's office." This is followed by a man with dark hair and glasses, speaking with a concerned expression, identified as "Andrew Weisman," who "has a reputation for being a scorch earth prosecutor." The audio also mentions "Mueller put Greg Andres on his team, who was an experienced mob prosecutor in New York." The final shot is a color close-up of an older man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a blue shirt and red patterned tie, speaking with expressive gestures in a warmly lit interview setting, concluding that this was "the first sort of warning sign for the Trump White House." The overall narrative flows from intense personal moments to formal institutional settings, blending black-and-white archival-style footage with color interviews to convey a story of political or legal significance, centered around the formation and impact of Mueller's team.

The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man, his finger pressed to his lips, followed by a side profile of another man, both conveying intense seriousness, as a narrator's voice describes "killers, Steve Bannon calls them." The narrator continues, explaining that "Mueller's team had broad authority to investigate Russian interference, the Trump campaign," and in the wake of the Comey firing, "possible obstruction of justice by the President himself." The scene transitions to color, showing a man with glasses in a dark suit speaking directly to the camera, his voice explaining, "this is question of whether that act by itself constitutes obstruction of justice. Maybe it does, maybe it doesn't, but that issue didn't even exist before he fired James Comey, now it does. Now it's the subject of a federal investigation by a special prosecutor." The visual then cuts to a legal document, a May 9, 2017 memorandum from Rod J. Rosenstein, Deputy Attorney General, with key phrases like 'the Director made serious mistakes' and 'we do not hold press conferences to release derogatory information' underlined in red. The narrator's voice details how "Mueller also had the evidence from Comey's memos," specifically mentioning "The President asking Comey for loyalty. To go easy on Mike Flynn. The berating of Sessions for his recusal. The use of the Rosenstein memo." Further underlined text on the document highlights controversial statements such as 'he needed loyalty' and 'I hope you can let this go.' The segment concludes with Robert Bennett, identified as a former President Clinton lawyer, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting with the 'FRONTLINE' logo visible, his serious voice stating, "So, you know, you start stringing all these together, and that's how a prosecutor would present the case. He's improper acts, even if they are not in and of themselves criminal, amount..." A consistent, slightly dramatic background score plays throughout the segment.

event_blocks.summary: James Clapper, Fmr. Dir. of Natl. Intelligence, stated with a serious expression, "to her an intent to obstruct justice," as dramatic, tense background music began, transitioning to a black-and-white silhouette of a man, likely James Comey, in a suit, first in profile and then bowed in contemplation. A male narrator described the period, "The White House was under siege. The President in anger and desperation, returned to Roy Cohn's strategy, a forceful counter-attack." Animated Twitter posts from Donald J. Trump overlaid the silhouette, with Trump audibly proclaiming, "This is the single greatest witch hunt of a politician in American history," followed by, "There is no collusion and no obstruction. I should be given apology," and then, "You are witnessing the single greatest witch hunt in American political history, led by some very bad and conflicted people." Lisa Desjardins of PBS NewsHour, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting with an earnest expression, explained how "The President definitely seized on that term witch hunt... It's something that he felt was working to undermine the Mueller investigation." Another tweet from Trump appeared, with his voice stating, "After seven months of investigations and collusion with the Russians, nobody has been able to show any proof. Sad." Yamiche Alcindor of PBS NewsHour, wearing a green V-neck dress, spoke expressively with gesturing hands in a softly lit room, commenting that President Trump calling the Mueller investigation a "witch hunt has an impact in Washington," all underscored by the persistent dramatic music.

Trump.description: His Twitter posts are displayed as animated text overlays, with his distinct voice audibly stating claims about a 'witch hunt,' 'no collusion & no obstruction,' and 'After 7 months of investigations &... “collusion with the Russians,” nobody has been able to show any proof. Sad!' The tweets are shown with engagement metrics and the Twitter interface.

It's something that he felt was working to undermine the Mueller investigation." Another tweet from Trump appears, again with his voice stating, "After seven months of investigations and collusion with the Russians, nobody has been able to show any proof. Sad." The final shot is of Yamiche Alcindor of PBS NewsHour, wearing a green V-neck dress, speaking expressively with gesturing hands in a softly lit room, commenting that President Trump calling the Mueller investigation a "witch hunt has an impact in Washington." The overall tone is investigative and critical, focusing on Trump’s public denials and the political controversy surrounding the Russia investigation, all underscored by the persistent dramatic music.

event_blocks.summary: A rapid montage of Fox News broadcasts, beginning with a female anchor in a green top discussing 'GOP SLAMMED FOR NOT 'PROTECTING' PRESIDENT,' transitions to the Statue of Liberty with a wildfire ticker, and then to the 'FOX & friends' set where anchors discuss 'WHITE HOUSE VS SPECIAL COUNSEL,' with one male voice noting, "And at Fox News, that's just what happened," another stating, "The President is really mad," and a third adding, "He tweeted this out as the phony Russian witch hunt continues." A male anchor appears with the chyron 'REPORTS: TRUMP LEGAL TEAM LOOKING INTO POSSIBLE CONFLICTS AMONG MUELLER'S HIRES,' followed by another discussing 'NEW DEVELOPMENTS IN DEMOCRATIC SCANDALS.' A split-screen shows two men discussing 'FBI CHIEF MCCABE FACES THREE SEPARATE FEDERAL INQUIRIES,' while Howard Kurtz emphatically states, "This is a very dangerous witch hunt," echoed by others agreeing and calling to "put an end to the political witch hunt against President Trump." Howard Kurtz, identified as 'FOX News' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, speaks as Gabriel Sherman explains, "Trump was avidly watching. He likes what he sees on these Fox opinion shows, and they often get the benefit of having access to the President." The montage continues with a male anchor presenting 'ANTI-TRUMP BIAS EXPOSED IN MUELLER PROBE,' followed by a split-screen of a female and male anchor highlighting that 'more than half of the attorneys who have served on Mueller's team have contributed to Democrats.' During this, Lou Dobbs exclaims, "Lou, get rid of Mueller!" with others echoing calls like, "Mueller should be dismissed," and "Robert Mueller must be fired immediately." Lou Dobbs is then shown with the chyron 'CRITICIZES SESSIONS OVER RUSSIA RECUSAL,' as a male voice warns, "So he either pulls the plug now or this will be going on years from now." This is followed by a male anchor reporting on 'KAREN HANDEL (R) WINS GEORGIA 6TH DISTRICT CONGRESSIONAL SPECIAL ELECTION,' while Gabriel Sherman describes Trump's "daily conversations with the hosts there," enabling him to "get his talking points out there." Gabriel Sherman, identified as 'Author, The Loudest Voice in the Room' under 'FRONTLINE,' speaks as a male voice declares, "The FBI is a shadow government now," another questions, "And what they did in leaking this information was illegal, correct?" to which another responds, "Absolutely illegal and almost becomes a soft coup in a sense," with a final voice adding, "They're leaking..." The segment concludes with a male anchor presenting 'GOP LAWMAKERS CITE NEW ALLEGATIONS OF POLITICAL BIAS IN FBI,' a split-screen of two male anchors under the 'Hannity' logo, and a female anchor in a red dress speaking against an American flag backdrop, all maintaining an intense, critical tone.

event_blocks.summary: The New York Times uncovered a meeting between the Trump campaign and Russians, specifically Donald Trump Jr. hosting Natalia Veselnitskaya, Jared Kushner, and Paul Manafort, a fact undisclosed during the campaign, prompting the newspaper to seek comment from the White House while President Trump was at the G20 Germany 2017 summit with Vladimir Putin, a situation Philip Rucker described as a "big distraction." As the White House prepared a statement, President Trump and Hope Hicks worked on it aboard Air Force One, with Trump dictating a message for his son, Donald Trump Jr., while his lawyers reportedly "lost their minds" and were not present. Trump's initial statement, crafted for his son, downplayed the meeting as concerning Russian orphan adoption, but the truth emerged upon Trump's return to Washington, with news reports detailing a campaign meeting involving a Kremlin-linked lawyer. The "potential bombshell" of emails, including one from Donald Trump Jr. to Jared Kushner and Paul Manafort stating, "The Crown prosecutor of Russia offered to provide the Trump campaign with some official documents and information that would incriminate Hillary and her dealings with Russia," confirmed the advertised intent of the meeting was collusion. Special Counsel Robert Mueller's investigation put Trump and his family in jeopardy, leading Trump to blame Attorney General Jeff Sessions, whose recusal from the Russia investigation was described by Emily Bazelon as 'the pebble in his shoe' and 'the original sin.' Trump publicly criticized Sessions, questioning his actions regarding the FBI, which Lisa Desjardins noted as remarkable, and Yamiche Alcindor stated that in attacking Sessions, Trump was attacking the nature of the Attorney General's role and the Department of Justice.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a dynamic street-level shot of a yellow NYC taxi driving past the modern glass facade of The New York Times building, its iconic logo prominently displayed, as a female voice is heard discussing a 'cleansing needed in our FBI and Department of Justice.' The camera then tilts upward, emphasizing the building’s height, while a male narrator explains that 'At The New York Times that summer,' they had a lead on what would become 'the biggest story yet,' having discovered 'another meeting between the Trump campaign and the Russians.' This transitions to a close-up interview with Matt Apuzzo of The New York Times, identified by on-screen text under the FRONTLINE logo, who explains that he and his colleagues had been reporting on 'another Russian meeting that we didn't totally understand' and that 'had been undisclosed during the campaign.' The scene then shifts to a series of black-and-white close-ups of various individuals—a man in a suit and tie, a woman in a dark blazer with a necklace, and another man in profile, all with serious expressions—as the narrator details that 'Donald Trump Jr. had hosted the meeting' with Russian lawyer Natalia Veselnitskaya, also noting the presence of Jared Kushner and Paul Manafort. The video then moves to a formal diplomatic setting at the G20 Germany 2017 summit, where Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin are seated in white chairs, flanked by American and Russian flags, with photographers capturing the moment amidst the sound of camera clicks and murmuring. The camera zooms in on Putin as the narrator states that 'The newspaper wanted a comment from the White House,' but that week, the President was in 'Hamburg, Germany for the G20 summit and his first meeting with Vladimir Putin.' The segment concludes with an interview with Philip Rucker of The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text under the FRONTLINE logo, speaking in a dimly lit setting, describing the situation as 'a big distraction on the sidelines of the summit.' Throughout, a serious, investigative-style background music underscores the narrative.

The segment opens at the G20 Germany 2017 summit, where Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin are seen seated in white chairs, flanked by their national flags, engaging in conversation amidst aides and photographers. A man with graying hair and a beard, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting, explains the challenge of responding to a New York Times inquiry, quoting the White House's request for time. He then recounts how, after the summit, the president took control of the situation, and he received a call from an Air Force One operator asking for more time as they worked on a statement. As Air Force One is dynamically shown accelerating on a runway and lifting off into a clear sky, its 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA' livery visible, the man reveals that Hope Hicks and President Trump were working on this statement at the front of the plane. A black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump in profile, speaking intensely to a woman, underscores his central role. Yamiche Alcindor, identified by on-screen text, then states that the president was physically dictating a message for his son, Donald Trump Jr. This is followed by a black-and-white close-up of a serious Donald Trump on a phone call inside an aircraft, an American flag pin on his lapel, while Carol Leonnig, also identified by on-screen text, comments that the president's lawyers were 'losing their minds' and clarifies they were not on Air Force One or in Germany.

The segment opens with a black-and-white, cinematic shot of Donald Trump seated at a large, dimly lit desk, speaking on a telephone with a serious expression, as a calm male voice discusses hearing secondhand about a statement being issued to the New York Times. This transitions to a color interview with Robert Bennett, identified as 'Fmr. Pres. Clinton lawyer,' who, in a dimly lit room, earnestly states in an exasperated tone that writing a statement is 'amateur hour' and that lawyers 'couldn't control their client' and 'still can't control their client.' The scene cuts back to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Trump, his face illuminated from the side, as a news anchor's voice reports the White House's response to a New York Times report claiming Donald Trump Jr. met with someone. A deeper, authoritative voice then details Trump's statement, written for his son, about a meeting concerning the adoption of Russian orphans, followed by another male voice (possibly Trump Jr.) confirming it was a 'short introductory meeting' to discuss a program about Russian children. A montage of black-and-white newspaper clippings, with red-highlighted text, visually reinforces the narrative of political scrutiny, while the authoritative voice notes a reason for the meeting that the president's statement omitted, and a female news anchor reports the New York Times publishing details about a campaign meeting involving a Kremlin-linked lawyer. As the authoritative voice states that 'it didn't take long for the truth to come out' upon the president's return to Washington, a wide shot shows a helicopter flying over Washington D.C., with the Washington Monument visible, before landing on the White House lawn. The final scene shows Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, stepping down from the helicopter with the Presidential Seal visible, greeted by personnel, while an energetic news anchor announces 'explosive news about President Trump and Russia,' involving Donald.

The segment opens at night with Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, descending the steps of Marine One, his serious expression captured as he walks across a grassy lawn, while an urgent male voice on the audio track immediately discusses Donald Trump Jr. and a "potential bombshell" that would "completely blow up." The scene then transitions to a close-up of a New York Times front page from July 10, 2017, with the headline 'TRUMP TEAM MET RUSSIAN OFFERING DIRT ON CLINTON,' as the audio explains that "The New York Times discovered a series of emails" setting up the meeting. This leads into a detailed view of an email from Donald Trump Jr. to Jared Kushner and Paul Manafort, dated June 3, 2016, with key phrases highlighted in red, while the audio directly quotes the email's content: "The Crown prosecutor of Russia offered to provide the Trump campaign with some official documents and information that would incriminate Hillary and her dealings with Russia, and would be very useful to your father," further emphasizing that this was "part of Russia and its government’s support for Mr. Trump." The segment concludes with a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, audibly reacting to the email content with "Oh my God, it says it."

The video opens with a close-up of a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit and checkered shirt, speaking seriously in a dimly lit setting, as a male voice, later identified as Michael Isikoff, states, "It says it in an email? This is part of the Russian government's efforts to support Donald Trump." The scene transitions to an exterior shot of Trump Tower in New York City, with its golden signage and glass facade reflecting surrounding buildings, and a police car and yellow taxi visible at street level, accompanied by the distant sound of a siren. The camera then tilts upward, capturing the towering height of the building against a dusky sky. The video shifts to a series of black-and-white close-ups: a man in a suit with a serious expression, followed by a man with slicked-back hair gesturing while speaking, then a woman with dark hair and earrings looking intently, and a woman with curly hair in a green top, identified by on-screen text as Yamiche Alcindor, whose voice explains, "We're talking about top aides in the middle of the campaign. We're talking about Jared Kushner, Paul Manafort, Donald Trump Jr. sitting down with a Russian woman who has told them she's going to give them some sort of information on Hillary Clinton. It's a crystal clear reason why they're there." The sequence continues with a black-and-white shot of a man in a suit and tie, his mouth open as if speaking. The video then cuts to Michael Isikoff, identified by on-screen text as 'Co-Author, Russian Roulette' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, wearing a dark suit, light blue shirt, and red patterned tie, speaking expressively. A close-up of an email from Donald Trump Jr. is shown, with the phrase 'I love it especially later in the summer' highlighted in red, as a male voice (from the Man with Glasses and Blue Patterned Tie) asks, "What does Don Jr. write back in an email if it's what you say, 'I love it'?" and Isikoff responds, "I love it, especially later in the summer." The scene returns to Michael Isikoff, who continues speaking with emphasis, while the male voice from the Man with Glasses and Blue Patterned Tie adds, "Coming on top of everything else that had come out about all these Russian contacts with the campaign. The Trump Tower email trail was incredibly damning." Another email is displayed, with the phrase 'part of Russia and its government's support for' underlined in red. The video then shows the man with glasses and a blue patterned tie, speaking in a color interview, as Isikoff asserts, "There's no ambiguity about this. It's there in black and white. And whatever they actually talked about in the meeting, the advertised intent of the meeting was collusion." The video then shows a black-and-white shot of Donald Trump speaking at a podium, his expression intense and his hand raised. The final shot is a close-up of Donald Trump in black-and-white, his face illuminated, looking directly at the camera with a serious expression, as a siren is heard again.

The segment opens with a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, wearing a dark suit and tie, speaking with a serious and intense expression, his face illuminated against a dark background, his voice heard dismissing a meeting, stating, "Nothing happened from the meeting, zero happened from the meeting, and honestly, I think the press made a very big deal over something that really a lot of people would do." A male narrator then explains that the president would downplay the importance of the meeting. The scene transitions to exterior shots of a modern building with large glass windows reflecting the sky, identified by the PBS and FRONTLINE logos, suggesting a documentary or investigative context, as a female voice (Carol Leonnig) interjects, "Now we've got another email. An email..." The camera pans across the building’s facade, emphasizing its structured, official appearance. The scene then cuts to Carol Leonnig, a woman with shoulder-length brown hair, wearing a dark sleeveless top and a bright pink beaded necklace, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, her expression serious and engaged. The narrator continues, noting that Special Counsel Robert Mueller was paying close attention and posing the question of whether anything illegal occurred. This is followed by a black-and-white shot of a man with graying hair, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, looking downward with a contemplative expression, possibly representing James Comey, while a male voice discusses 'The President's lawyers,' expressing intense concern that the President has "added to an obstruction case." The video then shifts to a color interview with Frank Montoya, Jr., identified by on-screen text as 'Fmr. FBI Special Agent in Charge,' wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking earnestly, asking if deliberately crafting a lie is "another step in the obstruction investigation" or "the conspiracy/collusion investigation." The final shot returns to a black-and-white image of Donald Trump in profile, standing in a room with a mural of clouds and trees, his expression stern and pensive, as another male voice concludes that it "shows that the Trump team was willing to engage with the Russians." Throughout the segment, a serious, investigative background score underscores the gravity of the discussion.

The segment opens with a black-and-white, low-angle shot of Donald Trump, looking serious in a dimly lit, ornate room, as a male narrator explains that Special Counsel Robert Mueller's actions put Trump and his family in jeopardy, leading Trump to blame his Attorney General. The scene transitions to a color close-up of Emily Bazelon, identified as from The New York Times Magazine, who, with her curly brown hair and red top, articulately explains how Jeff Sessions' recusal from the Russia investigation increasingly grated on Donald Trump, calling it 'the pebble in his shoe' and 'the original sin.' The visual returns to a black-and-white shot of Trump, intensely speaking at a desk, before cutting to Peter Baker, identified as from The New York Times, who, with a serious expression, recounts how he and other reporters were invited into the Oval Office to hear Trump 'really trashing Jeff Sessions.' This leads to a digital display of a green audio waveform labeled 'New York Times recording' and 'TRUMP_WH_AUDIO_1.mp3,' from which Donald Trump's distinct, frustrated voice is heard stating, 'He should have never recused himself. And if he would, if he was going to recuse himself, he should have told me before he took the job, and I would have picked somebody else.' The segment concludes with Peter Baker continuing to speak, adding that they had previously reported on Trump's unhappiness with Sessions.

The segment opens with a black-and-white, dimly lit shot of Donald Trump, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, speaking intensely while seated at a desk, partially obscured by foreground silhouettes, as a male voice comments on his public message, followed by an intense male voice, likely Trump's, declaring, "It's extremely unfair, and that's a mild word to the president." The scene transitions to Lisa Desjardins, identified by on-screen text, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, her clear, analytical voice stating that Trump "was telling the world that he didn't have confidence in his own Attorney General. And it was remarkable." A stark black-and-white silhouette of a man in profile, likely James Comey, gazes thoughtfully as a male narrator explains that "in case Sessions didn't get the message, it was time for him to leave. On Twitter, the president ramped up the attack." This leads to a series of animated Twitter posts from Donald J. Trump, displayed as overlays, criticizing Attorney General Jeff Sessions and questioning why Sessions didn’t replace Acting FBI Director Andrew McCabe, with a distinct male voice reading the tweets aloud. The scene then cuts to Yamiche Alcindor, identified by on-screen text, speaking expressively in a softly lit room, her voice asserting, "In attacking Jeff Sessions, he's attacking the very nature of the Attorney General's role, and he's attacking the very nature of the Department of Justice." The final shot is a wide, sunlit exterior of the U.S. Capitol Building, as the narrator concludes, "But on Capitol Hill..."

event_blocks.summary: The Capitol Building, bathed in sunlight, serves as the backdrop for a narrative detailing the political maneuvering surrounding Jeff Sessions' tenure. Carrie Johnson of NPR explains that Sessions' Senate allies, including Mitch McConnell and Chuck Grassley, strongly defended him against Donald Trump's desire for his removal, a level of defiance unusual in other matters. Chris Whipple, author of 'The Gatekeepers,' emphasizes Sessions' significant congressional support, which led Trump to understand the difficulty of confirming a replacement. A dramatic, spotlight-illuminated black-and-white image of Donald Trump against an American flag underscores the President's predicament, with the narrator confirming Sessions' position was secure. The segment concludes with Jeh Johnson, former Secretary of Homeland Security, drawing an analogy from the 'private world' about a lawyer unable to perform their duties, likely referencing Sessions' situation.

Capitol Building.description: Shown in a wide, sunlit exterior shot, showcasing its grand dome, columns, and ornate architecture, symbolizing legislative power and the political context of the narrative.

Chris Whipple.description: A man with short brown hair, wearing a blue suit and striped tie, identified as 'Author, The Gatekeepers,' he explains that Jeff Sessions had significant congressional support, making it difficult for Trump to replace him.

Carrie Johnson.description: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a blue blazer over a black top, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, identified as 'Carrie Johnson, NPR,' she discusses how Jeff Sessions' allies in the Senate strongly defended him against Donald Trump's attempts to remove him.

Man with Glasses.description: A man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking in a close-up interview, he states that Mitch McConnell and Chuck Grassley made it clear they stood by Sessions.

Jeh Johnson.description: A bald man in a pinstripe suit and light blue tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, identified as 'Jeh Johnson, Former Secretary of Homeland Security,' he draws an analogy about hiring a lawyer who cannot perform the requested job, relating it to the political context.

Donald Trump.description: Shown in black-and-white, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, standing in a dimly lit room with an American flag behind him, his face illuminated by a spotlight, conveying intensity and isolation, he is discussed by interviewees regarding his attempts to remove Jeff Sessions and the challenges he faced in doing so.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a sunlit, wide exterior shot of the Capitol Building, its grand dome and classical architecture establishing a formal political atmosphere, accompanied by a serious, underlying musical score. A male narrator's voice sets the scene, explaining that Jeff Sessions, having served 20 years in the Senate, had powerful allies prepared to fight back against the President. The scene transitions to Carrie Johnson of NPR, appearing in a dimly lit room in a blue blazer, who elaborates on how Jeff Sessions' allies in the Senate strongly defended him when Donald Trump signaled his desire to remove him, a level of defiance not seen on other issues. Next, a man with dark hair and glasses in a suit, speaking with a serious expression, states that Mitch McConnell and Chuck Grassley made it very clear they stood by Sessions. Chris Whipple, author of 'The Gatekeepers,' then appears in a blue suit and striped tie, explaining that despite any opinions on Sessions, he had significant congressional support, leading Trump to realize he likely couldn't get a replacement confirmed if he proceeded with removal. A dramatic black-and-white image of Donald Trump, illuminated by a spotlight against an American flag, conveys intensity and isolation as the narrator confirms the President was stuck and Sessions wasn't going anywhere. Finally, Jeh Johnson, former Secretary of Homeland Security, in a pinstripe suit, speaks directly to the camera, drawing an analogy from the 'private world' about hiring a lawyer who cannot perform the job they were hired to do, likely in reference to Sessions' situation. The lighting throughout alternates between bright exteriors and moody, shadowed interiors, reinforcing the serious political tone.

event_blocks.summary: Jeh Johnson, in a dimly lit interview, explained that if one is fired, they would "go find somebody else," and that Donald Trump feels "stuck with his current attorney general" because "the Senate has a say in this too." The White House at dusk, framed by trees and a fountain, was shown as a narrator stated Trump's "strategy of confrontation hadn't worked," leading him to "reluctantly change tactics" and "turn to lawyers steeped in the ways of Washington." This shift was exemplified by Sarah Ellison of The Washington Post explaining, "We're going to bring in the professionals now," referring to "people who have real Washington pedigrees" who "know certainly who Robert Mueller is" and "are going to cooperate and are going to kind of play by the rules." The narrative then introduced John Dowd, a "long-time Washington criminal defense specialist," and Ty Cobb, an "expert trial lawyer," who "joined the team." Matt Apuzzo of The New York Times quoted Ty Cobb as "openly saying, 'I have great respect for Bob Mueller. I think he's a patriot,'" and that Cobb "says to Mueller, 'I I'm here and and we're going to we want to be cooperative.'" The segment also noted that Trump's lawyers expected the probe to end soon, with some predicting a conclusion within "four to six weeks," and that Ty Cobb had mentioned Thanksgiving in relation to the investigation's timeline. However, Philip Rucker of The Washington Post indicated that people knowledgeable about the investigation believed it could last "at least another year," a sentiment echoed by a skeptical female voice and a CNN split-screen interview highlighting fears of a "meltdown" amidst Trump's predicted exoneration. A Fox News segment discussed Trump lawyers expecting an "expeditious" conclusion, though a male voice cautioned against using that term with a special counsel, while Robert Costa described the investigation as "humming" and "moving," with Mueller arriving before 6 AM daily and witnesses entering through a garage, all while discussing Russian interference on social media, concluding it's a "sprawling, active investigation." The video concluded with an exterior view of the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit.

Sarah Ellison.description: A woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, wearing a purple blouse, speaking directly to the camera in a warm-lit setting, identified as 'Sarah Ellison, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, explaining the need to 'bring in the professionals' with 'real Washington pedigrees' who know 'Robert Mueller' and 'are going to cooperate'.

White House.description: Exterior view at dusk, illuminated against a dark sky, with trees and lawns visible, and a fountain spraying water in the foreground, establishing a secure and official atmosphere.

Matt Apuzzo.description: In a color interview, wearing a dark blazer over a patterned shirt, speaking with hand gestures, identified by on-screen text as 'The New York Times' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, quoting Ty Cobb's respect for 'Bob Mueller' and desire to be 'cooperative'.

Jeh Johnson.description: A bald man in a pinstripe suit and light blue tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, his expression serious and composed, discussing being fired and an attorney general being 'stuck' due to the Senate's say.

Donald Trump.description: Shown in black-and-white, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, standing in a dimly lit room with an American flag behind him, his face illuminated by a spotlight, conveying intensity and isolation, with audio context indicating his predicted exoneration and increasing frustration with the ongoing investigation.

The Takeout Podcast.description: A podcast setting with two men in suits and headphones, one with a mustache and glasses, speaking into microphones, with the CBSN logo visible. One host states that 'Cobb was speaking to an audience of one' and sees 'no reason for it not to conclude soon,' prompting the other host to ask 'What is soon?', to which the first host defines 'soon' as 'within the next, you know, four to six weeks.'

Man in Dark Suit and Light Blue Shirt.description: A man with short, graying hair, wearing a dark suit and light blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting, his expression earnest and engaged, who states a strategy involving 'providing more than a million documents, agreeing to interviews with White House staff, and keeping the president from tweeting.'

Chris Cillizza.description: A man with glasses and a patterned tie, shown on a CNN broadcast, identified as 'CNN Politics Reporter & Editor-at-Large,' who reports on 'TRUMP PREDICTS EXONERATION IN RUSSIA INVESTIGATION,' conveying that lawyers wanted Trump to hear a 'positive message,' that 'They expect the probe to be over soon,' and that there's 'a light at the end of the tunnel,' later mentioning 'Ty Cobb said it was Thanksgiving.'

Philip Rucker.description: A man with a beard and dark hair, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, identified by on-screen text as 'PHILIP RUCKER, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, stating that people with knowledge of the investigation believe it could last 'at least another year.'

Robert Costa.description: A man with dark hair, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking directly to the camera in a formal interview setting, identified by on-screen text as 'ROBERT COSTA, Moderator, Washington Week' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, describing the investigation as 'humming,' 'moving,' with Bob Mueller coming in before 6 AM daily, witnesses entering through a garage, and every White House official being brought in, all while discussing Russian interference on social media, concluding it's a 'sprawling, active investigation.'

event_blocks.summary: The E. Barrett Prettyman United States Courthouse is shown as news reports detail Special Counsel Robert Mueller's investigation going public with subpoenas, leading to George Papadopoulos pleading guilty to lying about Russian contacts and making false statements, as evidenced by legal documents and newspaper headlines. Concurrently, Trump campaign chairman Paul Manafort and deputy Rick Gates were indicted on charges including conspiracy and money laundering, while Mike Flynn pleaded guilty to lying to the FBI. A black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump's intense face precedes an interview with John Cassidy of The New Yorker, who notes the failure of a 'get along' strategy, followed by an older man reflecting on a past mistake and the persistence of an inquiry. Later, a dramatic close-up of Robert Mueller is shown as his lawyers' advice proves incorrect, and newspaper headlines announce subpoenas for Trump's businesses, with superimposed images of Trump and Mueller. The audio confirms Mueller's team is legally demanding information from the Trump Organization. A black-and-white shot of Donald Trump gesturing emphatically under a spotlight accompanies commentary on his unhappiness with the special counsel and his "screaming matches" with lawyers John Dowd and Ty Cobb. Lead lawyer John Dowd, in a color close-up, is identified as advising Trump to provide information, but he resigned the following week, with Cobb's days also numbered, indicating a legal team shake-up. Images of a stern man in a dark suit and polka-dotted tie, and an older man with a mustache and glasses looking downward, are shown as the audio notes Trump's dissatisfaction with everyone because the investigation is not over. The segment concludes with a black-and-white close-up of a partially shadowed Donald Trump, the PBS and FRONTLINE logos appearing, as the audio states Trump's willingness to cooperate is over and the United States is under a criminal investigation.

The audio directly references these events, stating 'Mueller's team is no longer just asking... they're legally demanding' and 'Mueller issued a subpoena, directed at Trump's private company, the Trump organization.'

I should have come out fighting, given into my own instincts from the very beginning,' and noting that 'once the end of the year came and the inquiry hadn't gone away.'

He is also shown in a final black-and-white close-up, partially shadowed, with a serious and pensive expression.

The audio frequently refers to him as 'Mueller' and 'Special Counsel Mueller,' highlighting his role in the investigation and the issuance of subpoenas to the Trump organization.

Barrett Prettyman United States Courthouse, as news reporting from the Wall Street Journal discusses Special Counsel Robert Mueller's investigation finally going public and issuing subpoenas. Black-and-white footage then shows a man in a suit walking through a hallway, followed by a mugshot-style portrait of George Papadopoulos, identified by audio as Trump campaign foreign policy aide George Papadopoulos, who "pled guilty to lying about Russian contacts" and "making false statements." This transitions to a montage of legal documents, including one for Papadopoulos detailing "False Statements," and newspaper headlines like 'Indicted Trump campaign aide aimed high' and 'Flynn pleads guilty to lying to FBI.' The audio further elaborates that "Trump's campaign chairman Paul Manafort and his deputy Rick Gates were indicted on numerous charges ranging from conspiracy to money laundering" and were told to surrender, while "Mike Flynn pled guilty to that charge of lying to the FBI." A black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump's intense, isolated face follows. The scene shifts to a color interview with John Cassidy of The New Yorker, who, in a dimly lit room, comments that "the 'get along' strategy had failed." The segment concludes with a black-and-white close-up of an older man with a serious, contemplative expression, whose voice reflects, "Eventually just came to the conclusion that I made a mistake. I should have come out fighting, given into my own instincts from the very beginning," adding that "once the end of the year came and the inquiry hadn't gone away."

The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller, his face intense and contemplative against a dark background, as a tense, investigative score begins, and a voice states that what he'd been told by his lawyers turned out to be 'completely incorrect.' This transitions to a montage of grayscale newspaper headlines, including 'Mueller subpoenas Trump’s businesses' and 'Trump lashes out at special counsel Mueller,' with superimposed images of Donald Trump and Mueller, while the audio confirms that 'Mueller's team is no longer just asking the Trump organization for information, they're legally demanding,' and that 'Mueller issued a subpoena, directed at Trump's private company, the Trump organization.' A black-and-white shot of Donald Trump, seated and gesturing emphatically under a spotlight in a dimly lit room, accompanies commentary that 'The President week in, week out, is festering, he's unhappy with this special counsel,' and 'He gets into screaming matches with Dowd and Cobb.' A color close-up then shows a man in a dark suit and red tie speaking directly to the camera, identified as 'lead lawyer John Dowd,' who, along with 'Cobb,' advised Trump to 'provide them with information,' but 'Dowd resigned the next week,' and 'Ty Cobb's days were also numbered,' signaling 'another legal team shake-up.' This is followed by a black-and-white image of a stern-looking man in a dark suit and polka-dotted tie, then an older man with a mustache and glasses looking downward seriously, as the audio continues about Trump being 'unhappy with everyone because it's not over.' The final frames return to a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, partially shadowed, as the PBS and FRONTLINE logos appear, and the audio concludes that 'Trump's willingness to cooperate with the investigation was over,' and 'The United States is currently under a criminal investigation.'

event_blocks.summary: The FBI dramatically escalated a showdown with Donald Trump, raiding the office of his personal lawyer, Michael Cohen, on April 9, 2018, an event that was broadcast across multiple networks including ABC, Fox News, and CNN. Trump was reportedly furious and consumed by the news, finding it troubling and scary, while White House advisors noted he was agitated by watching the televisions. The FBI seized emails, tax documents, and business records, with the sight of Michael Cohen's name on a chyron sending Trump into a rage, described as no-knock raids by FBI agents. This raid, stemming from a referral by Special Counsel Robert Mueller, was seen as a personal assault on Trump, colliding his personal life in New York with his presidency in Washington. Cohen, described as not just a lawyer but a fixer, had been used by Trump for years to protect his image, similar to how Trump used Roy Cohn decades prior. The investigation into Cohen felt like an arrow pointed directly at Trump's chest, aimed at uncovering his history in deeply sensitive ways. Trump, speaking animatedly from a formal conference room with American flags and a presidential seal, declared the raid a "disgrace" and a "witch hunt," an assessment echoed by Carol Leonnig of The Washington Post, who noted Trump's enraged and obsessed reaction. Matthew Miller, a former Department of Justice official, commented that the scrutiny of Cohen was viewed by Trump as a threat to him. Jack Goldsmith, a former Asst. Attorney General, explained that Cohen's role as a close adviser and attorney involved in Trump's "shady business" presented a new avenue of potential criminal exposure for the president, with discussions of impeachment arising amidst the raid on Cohen and Mueller's ongoing investigation.

ABC News Broadcast.description: A live news segment with a 'BREAKING NEWS' graphic, featuring a male anchor in a dark suit and tie, with a chyron reading 'FBI RAIDS COHEN'S OFFICE' and the ABC News logo.

Stephen Ryan.description: Michael Cohen’s attorney, shown in a New York Times graphic, with a photo of Cohen beside his quote about the FBI seizing privileged communications.

Fox News Alert Graphic.description: A red and yellow graphic with bold text 'FOX NEWS ALERT' and 'SPECIAL COUNSEL INVESTIGATION' displayed during a live broadcast, with a split-screen showing Trump and Cohen. The Fox News anchor explicitly states, 'This is a Fox News Alert,' when this graphic is shown.

FRONTLINE.description: A red rectangular logo with white text, appearing below the PBS logo, identifying the documentary series. An initial unseen narrator, whose dramatic tone sets the scene, suggests the documentary context.

ABC News Chyron.description: A red and white chyron reading 'BREAKING NEWS: FBI RAIDS COHEN'S OFFICE' displayed during a live broadcast, with the ABC News logo and anchor David Muir.

Lester Holt.description: NBC News anchor, wearing a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, seated across from Trump, holding a notepad, listening intently, and occasionally gesturing during the interview. His distinct voice reports the 'breaking news' about the FBI raid on Michael Cohen.

Fox News Anchor.description: A male news anchor in a dark suit and red striped tie, reporting on the 'SPECIAL COUNSEL INVESTIGATION' with a serious expression, seated in a studio with a split-screen showing Trump and Cohen. His voice announces, 'This is a Fox News Alert,' and reports on 'breaking news.'

CNN Breaking News Graphic.description: A red and white 'BREAKING NEWS' graphic with text 'NYT: FEDERAL AGENTS RAID OFFICE OF TRUMP LAWYER MICHAEL COHEN' displayed during a live broadcast, with the CNN logo.

Michael Cohen.description: A man in a dark suit and pink tie, shown in color being escorted by law enforcement, later appearing in black-and-white walking with a stern expression, and in a split-screen close-up with Donald Trump; identified as 'Trump’s lawyer' whose office was raided by the FBI, described as 'not just a lawyer, but a fixer' who 'brings it right back to Trump Tower,' and someone Trump 'used... to protect his image,' likened to a 'day-to-day Roy Cohn'.

Michael Cohen (in news footage).description: A man in a dark suit and pink tie, shown in color, being escorted by law enforcement, with a serious expression, appearing in multiple news broadcasts with chyrons like 'FBI RAIDS TRUMP'S LAWYER.'

Donald Trump.description: Seated at the head of a long wooden table in a formal conference room, wearing a dark blue suit, white shirt, blue patterned tie, and an American flag pin on his lapel. He speaks with animated gestures and an intense expression, his face illuminated by soft lighting, conveying authority and focus, stating in his distinctive voice, "constantly going on for over 12 months now" and later declaring, "This is a pure and simple witch hunt. Thank you very much. Thank you."

The New York Times.description: Exterior view of the modern glass building with the large, black 'The New York Times' logo prominently displayed across the facade, reflecting the surrounding cityscape, and later a graphic with Stephen Ryan's quote. A news anchor reports 'New York Times breaking the news' about the FBI raid.

CNN Broadcast.description: A live news segment with a 'BREAKING NEWS' graphic, featuring a male anchor in a dark suit and tie, with a red and white design and a timestamp of 2:08 PM PT. News commentators mention Trump watches CNN and will 'go back to CNN' to follow the news.

Stephen Ryan (in news graphic).description: Michael Cohen’s attorney, shown in a New York Times graphic, with a photo of Cohen beside his quote about the FBI seizing privileged communications.

Alan Dershowitz.description: Professor at Harvard Law School, wearing glasses, a dark suit, a blue shirt, and a striped tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting with the 'FRONTLINE' logo, explaining the concept of lawyer-client privilege and why 'President Trump' might be 'little upset' about investigations into his communications with lawyers.

MSNBC Screen.description: A news broadcast screen showing breaking news with a chyron reading 'BREAKING NEWS: NYT: FBI RAIDS OFFICE OF TRUMP LAWYER MICHAEL COHEN,' with the MSNBC logo and a live timestamp, indicating the urgency of the event, with the audio confirming the FBI raid on 'Trump lawyer Michael Cohen's office'.

Sam Nunberg.description: Former Trump political adviser, shown in a dark suit with a patterned tie and an American flag pin, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting.

Man with Glasses.description: Wearing a dark suit and striped tie, shown in black-and-white, sitting at a desk with a lamp and a duck figurine, holding a notepad and appearing focused.

Jonathan Mahler.description: From The New York Times Magazine, shown in a color interview wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking with a serious expression.

Jane Mayer.description: From The New Yorker, shown in a color interview wearing a dark blazer over a blue collared shirt, speaking with a serious and expressive demeanor in a dimly lit room.

Donald Trump Jr..description: Shown in black-and-white, wearing a plaid blazer over a white shirt, first holding a phone to his ear with a concerned expression while a confrontational voice states, 'I'm more than happy to discuss with your first and with your legal counsel... you're gonna need it,' then walking briskly down a busy New York City street, and later pushing through a door with a group of men, suggesting a sequence of urgent or high-pressure events.

Man in Bow Tie.description: An older man in a dark suit and bow tie, seen in black-and-white, walking with Donald Trump Jr. through a door, likely a staff member or advisor, adding to the formal entourage.

Emily Bazelon.description: A woman with curly brown hair, wearing a red top under a black cardigan and a large pendant necklace, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Emily Bazelon, The New York Times Magazine,' explaining that 'He cleans up messes' and discussing Cohen's role in solving problems for Donald Trump related to affairs and compensation.

Man with Glasses (Second Instance).description: A man with glasses and graying hair, wearing a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, shown in a black-and-white shot seated at a desk with a lamp and a duck figurine, holding a notepad and appearing focused, his presence coinciding with audio discussing Cohen being the target of a federal investigation and the possibility of his cooperation.

Conference Room.description: A large, ornate room with a long wooden table, high ceilings, hanging lamps, and framed pictures on the walls. American flags are visible, and the room is filled with officials and military personnel seated around the table, indicating a formal meeting or hearing. A camera operator is present, filming the event, while a narrator introduces it as a national security meeting where Trump chose to "go on the attack."

Man in Suit and Tie (Political Figure).description: A man in a dark suit and striped tie, wearing an American flag pin, speaking directly to the camera in a color interview with a serious expression, stating that 'Michael is very good at killing stories' and has helped Trump out of many issues, implying his role as a political commentator or analyst.

Blonde Woman (Photo).description: A woman with long blonde hair, identified by narration as the 'adult film star' from the 'Stormy Daniels story' who received a 'hush money payment' to conceal a 'sexual encounter with Trump,' seen exiting a building marked '2000' while followed by photographers, smiling in a black-and-white photo with Donald Trump, and later being escorted through a crowd, indicating media attention and public scrutiny related to the scandal.

Woman in Blazer.description: A woman with long blonde hair, identified by narration as the 'adult film star' from the 'Stormy Daniels story' who received a 'hush money payment' to conceal a 'sexual encounter with Trump,' seen exiting a building marked '2000' while followed by photographers, smiling in a black-and-white photo with Donald Trump, and later being escorted through a crowd, indicating media attention and public scrutiny related to the scandal.

Carol Leonnig.description: A woman with short brown hair, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red beaded necklace, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting. On-screen text identifies her as 'Carol Leonnig, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. She describes the President as "enraged and obsessed" and notes his repeated use of the word "disgrace" during a public briefing.

Matthew Miller.description: A man with dark hair, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting. On-screen text identifies him as 'Matthew Miller, Department of Justice, 2009–11' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. He explains that the President views Michael Cohen coming under scrutiny from the Department of Justice as a "threat to him."

Mike Pence.description: Seated to Trump's left at the conference table, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and blue tie, with a serious expression, his hands clasped on the table.

Military Officer.description: Seated at the far left of the table, wearing a dark military uniform with numerous medals and insignia, hands clasped, appearing attentive.

Nelson Cunningham.description: A man with gray hair, wearing a dark suit, light blue shirt, and a bright yellow tie, speaking in a dimly lit setting with a serious expression, gesturing with his hands, identified by on-screen text as 'Fmr. Pres. Clinton legal adviser'. He articulates that "The investigation of Michael Cohen has to feel to the president like an arrow pointed directly at his chest," emphasizing it's "aimed precisely at uncovering the president's own history" both before and after taking office, in "deeply sensitive" ways.

Jack Goldsmith.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and red polka-dot tie, speaking animatedly in a dimly lit interview setting, identified by on-screen text as 'Jack Goldsmith, Fmr. Asst. Attorney General'. He explains that this presents "a whole other avenue of potential exposure, criminal exposure to the president," noting that the individual was "a very close adviser and attorney to the president" involved in "the president's shady business."

John Bolton.description: Seated to Trump's right at the conference table, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie, with a mustache and glasses, holding a yellow folder, identified by a nameplate as 'Ambassador John Bolton'.

Camera Operator.description: A person in a green jacket and black cap, holding a professional camera, filming the meeting from the side, visible in the final shot.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, his face intensely lit by a spotlight in a dimly lit room, conveying intensity and confrontation, as an unseen narrator dramatically states, "And then the FBI dramatically escalated the showdown." The scene quickly transitions to a television screen where NBC's 'Nightly News' anchor Lester Holt, holding a notepad, reports on April 9, 2018, "Breaking news tonight, and it's a bombshell. The FBI raided the office of President Trump's personal lawyer, Michael Cohen." As breaking news footage of Michael Cohen being escorted by law enforcement flashes across multiple networks including ABC, Fox News, and CNN, an unseen male commentator describes Trump's reaction, stating he "was furious," "erupted," "very upset," and "consumed by this news all day," finding it "very troubling for him and scary for him." Another male news anchor details the "FBI raiding his office, his home, and a hotel room," while a female commentator reveals that "White House advisors are saying, can we turn off the televisions? All the President is doing, they say, is getting himself agitated." A Fox News anchor then announces, "This is a Fox News Alert," over a split-screen of Trump and Cohen, as the rapid cuts continue, showing chyrons like 'FBI RAIDS COHEN'S OFFICE' and 'NYT: FEDERAL AGENTS RAID OFFICE OF TRUMP LAWYER MICHAEL COHEN.' Further commentary from a male news anchor notes Trump "watches CNN" and will "go back to CNN," reporting that the FBI "also seized emails, tax documents, and business records." The segment concludes with the anchor explaining that seeing "Michael Cohen" on the chyron "sends him into a rage," detailing "the no-knock raids by FBI agents," emphasizing the urgent and dramatic tone of the unfolding political implications.

The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white shot of Donald Trump, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, gesturing emphatically in a dimly lit room with American and presidential flags, as a narrator describes how a referral by Special Counsel Robert Mueller was a 'personal assault' to 'the president' from the FBI, Department of Justice, and Mueller. This transitions to a color interview with Alan Dershowitz, Professor at Harvard Law School, who, in a dimly lit setting with the 'FRONTLINE' logo visible, explains that 'a lawyer is just like a priest, a doctor, and a wife in terms of privilege,' and he doesn't 'blame President Trump for being a little upset' about someone looking into what he may have told his lawyers. The scene then shifts to MSNBC breaking news footage showing Michael Cohen, Trump’s lawyer, being escorted by law enforcement, with a chyron announcing 'BREAKING NEWS: NYT: FBI RAIDS OFFICE OF TRUMP LAWYER MICHAEL COHEN.' A narrator states that 'the Cohen raid was a sign Trump's personal life in New York was colliding with his presidency in Washington.' This is followed by a color interview with an unnamed man in a dark suit and red tie, who asserts that 'Cohen brings it right back to Trump Tower,' revealing 'how Trump really operated for decades,' having 'someone like Michael Cohen, not just a lawyer, but a fixer at his side.' The video cuts to black-and-white footage of Michael Cohen walking sternly, as a narrator explains that 'for years, Trump had used Cohen to protect his image, just as he used Roy Cohn decades before.' Another voice adds that Trump 'looked at Michael as somebody who would be his day-to-day Roy Cohn.' The sequence concludes with a split-screen close-up of Cohen and Trump, both with intense, contemplative expressions, reinforcing the gravity and tension of their relationship. Throughout, a dramatic, investigative background score underscores the unfolding legal drama.

Somber, investigative background music plays as a narrator states that the press was invited to a national security meeting, but Trump wanted to "go on the attack." The camera pans across the table, showing men in military uniforms and suits, before focusing on Donald Trump at the head of the table. Dressed in a dark blue suit, white shirt, blue patterned tie, and an American flag pin, Trump speaks animatedly, gesturing with an intense expression, his face illuminated by soft lighting. He addresses the room, saying, "Come on in folks," and then declares, "I just heard that they broke into the office of one of my personal attorneys, good man. And it's a disgraceful situation, it's a total witch hunt." The scene cuts to Carol Leonnig of The Washington Post, in a dimly lit interview setting, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red beaded necklace, speaking seriously to the camera. Another interview segment features Matthew Miller, identified as a former Department of Justice official, in a dark suit and purple tie, speaking in a similarly dimly lit environment. The video alternates back to Trump at the conference table, who concludes, "These people have the biggest conflicts of interest I've ever seen. And I have this witch hunt," maintaining a tone of political gravity and scrutiny throughout.

He speaks animatedly, gesturing with his hands and an intense expression, stating in his distinctive voice that something has been "constantly going on for over 12 months now."
